knee-pain.intro: "Knee pain" is not one problem - it's a symptom that several different tissues produce for different reasons, which is why the person next to you with the "same" knee pain may need the opposite fix. In most people it traces back to a load-and-support mismatch: the muscles above and below the joint (your hips and quads) aren't absorbing force, so it lands on structures that can't take it - the back of the kneecap, the patellar tendon, the outer edge where the IT band crosses, or worn cartilage. Add a training spike, extra body weight, or connective tissue starved of collagen-building raw materials, and a tissue starts losing the race between damage and its own repair. The skill is figuring out which tissue is yours: a diffuse ache behind the kneecap on stairs points one way, a pinpoint sore spot just below the cap points another, pain on the outer edge that builds with running distance points to a third, and morning stiffness after 45 points to a fourth. Find your driver and the protocol stops being a random pile of pills.
ladder: Your knee hurts because one specific tissue - kneecap cartilage, the patellar tendon, the IT band's insertion, or the joint surface - is being loaded past what it can repair
That tissue gets overloaded when the muscles around the knee (hips + quads) stop sharing the force, or when the load itself (training volume, body weight) outpaces recovery
Muscles weaken and load climbs from under-use, training-volume spikes, excess body weight, and connective tissue starved of collagen-building nutrients
Root fix: strengthen the hips and quads, correct the load, and feed the tissue - the same few levers help almost every cause

causes.name: Hip & quad weakness with patellofemoral pain

chain.node: Underused or deconditioned hip abductors (gluteus medius) and quadriceps

Under load the knee tends toward dynamic valgus and the kneecap is thought to track laterally - but prospective data show this is often a CONSEQUENCE of pain, not its cause, and strengthening reduces pain even without changing tracking, so the mechanism is associative (tier 2), the fix is what's proven
Pressure concentrates on patellofemoral cartilage and subchondral bone
Dull ache around/behind the kneecap, worse on stairs, squatting, and after long sitting

tell.symptoms: Ache is around or behind the kneecap and hard to point to a single spot; worst going DOWN stairs, squatting, and after prolonged sitting (the 'theatre sign'). A single-leg squat or step-down is often weak and poorly controlled, sometimes with visible knee cave.
tell.labMarker: No blood test - diagnosis is clinical (pain on patellar loading/compression; tenderness under the patellar edges; weak, uncontrolled single-leg squat)

fixes.what: Progressive hip-abductor + quadriceps strengthening (side-lying/banded abduction, step-downs, split squats) - the best-evidenced intervention for PFP, works by restoring load-sharing capacity
Coach single-leg control and temporarily cut deep-squat/downhill volume while rebuilding strength
Collagen peptides 15-20 g + vitamin C taken 30-60 min before loading to support the connective tissue under the kneecap
Omega-3 to dampen low-grade joint inflammation

causes.confusedWith: Patellar tendinopathy - that pain is a pinpoint spot just below the kneecap, not a diffuse ache around it

causes.name: Cartilage degeneration (early osteoarthritis)

chain.node: Aging + cumulative joint load + genetic predisposition (and prior injury)
IL-1beta/MMP-driven cartilage matrix breakdown outpaces chondrocyte repair, with low-grade synovitis
Cartilage thins, subchondral bone remodels, osteophytes form
Stiff, achy knee that's worse after resting ('gelling'), with grinding/creaking (crepitus)

tell.symptoms: You're over ~45; the knee is stiff after resting or first thing in the morning but loosens within 30 minutes, grinds or creaks with movement, and aches more after overuse. May see bony enlargement around the joint.
tell.labMarker: X-ray: joint-space narrowing + osteophytes. No blood test diagnoses OA; hsCRP may be mildly raised in the inflammatory phenotype

fixes.what: Progressive resistance + low-impact aerobic loading - cartilage and the joint need load to stay healthy; disuse accelerates decline
Lose 5-10% body weight to cut per-step joint load and adipokine drive
Omega-3 plus Boswellia (5-LOX inhibition via AKBA) to lower catabolic joint inflammation

Glucosamine + chondroitin - evidence is mixed and effect sizes small; a modest symptom benefit in a subset, the combination not clearly better than placebo, so treat as a low-cost trial, not a cornerstone. Collagen peptides may also help symptoms
Higher-fibre, lower-sugar diet to reduce systemic inflammatory load

causes.confusedWith: Inflammatory arthritis (RA) or gout - those bring hot, swollen, sharply painful joints and abnormal blood markers (RF/anti-CCP, serum urate)

causes.name: Training-load error (patellar tendinopathy)

chain.node: Rapid spike in jumping/running/deceleration volume (load exceeds the tendon's adaptation rate)
Repetitive tensile overload; MMP-mediated matrix breakdown and a stalled 'failed healing' response outpace collagen synthesis
Collagen disorganization and neovascularization at the patellar tendon (inferior pole of the kneecap)
Sharp, localized pain just below the kneecap when you jump, run, or go downstairs

tell.symptoms: You can point to ONE spot right at the bottom tip of the kneecap. Pain is worst jumping, decelerating, or descending stairs; often 'warms up' during activity then aches for hours afterward. Common in jumpers and runners who recently ramped up.
tell.labMarker: Ultrasound/MRI: thickened, hypoechoic tendon with neovascularity. No blood marker

fixes.what: Heavy-slow resistance and isometric loading (e.g. Spanish squats) progressed over 8-12 weeks - out-performs eccentric-only and beats rest, which just deconditions the tendon
Fix the training-load spike: manage and periodize volume rather than stopping completely (tendons need graded load)
Collagen peptides + vitamin C 30-60 min before loading - shown to roughly double collagen synthesis markers around the loading window
Reduce added sugar to limit collagen glycation and cross-link stiffening
BPC-157 - experimental only (animal data, not an approved or human-proven therapy); not first-line and legally grey, approach with caution

causes.confusedWith: Patellofemoral pain (diffuse ache around the kneecap, not a pinpoint spot below it); in teens, Osgood-Schlatter (pain lower, at the shinbone bump)

causes.name: Excess body weight & metabolic-inflammatory load

chain.node: Excess adiposity
Each pound of body weight adds roughly 4x that force per step across the knee, PLUS elevated leptin/adipokines drive cartilage catabolism (the adipokine arm is tier 2)
Higher and more frequent mechanical loading plus a catabolic joint environment, usually in both knees
Aching, load-related knee pain that eases markedly with weight loss; faster OA progression over time

tell.symptoms: Pain scales with body weight and weight-bearing activity, usually affects both knees, and improves noticeably with even 5-10% weight loss. Often coexists with a bigger waist and low energy.
tell.labMarker: Elevated hsCRP and/or leptin; raised HbA1c and waist circumference flag the metabolic-inflammatory phenotype

fixes.what: Sustained 5-10% weight loss - each pound lost removes roughly 4 lb of load from the knee per step
Protein-forward, high-fibre, lower-sugar diet in a modest calorie deficit to lose fat while preserving muscle
Low-impact strength training and walking to burn fat while sparing the joint surface
Omega-3 to counter adipokine-driven systemic inflammation

causes.confusedWith: Primary osteoarthritis independent of weight - but in practice the two almost always coexist and amplify each other

causes.name: IT band syndrome (lateral overload)

chain.node: Ramp in running/cycling volume, downhill work, or worn shoes, usually on a base of weak hip abductors

Repetitive knee flexion-extension near ~30 degrees compresses the richly innervated fat and connective tissue beneath the iliotibial band against the lateral femoral epicondyle (a compression model, not the older 'friction' bursa idea)
Local irritation and inflammation of that tissue at the outer knee
Sharp or burning pain on the OUTER edge of the knee that builds at a predictable distance/time into a run and settles with rest

tell.symptoms: Pain is on the outside of the knee (not front, not joint line), comes on after a set distance or time - especially downhill - and eases when you stop. Common in runners and cyclists who spiked mileage. Often paired with weak hip abductors.
tell.labMarker: No lab; clinical diagnosis (lateral epicondyle tenderness, pain reproduced near 30 degrees of flexion). MRI only if unclear

fixes.what: Progressive hip-abductor and lateral-chain strengthening (glute med/max) - the same lever as the top cause
Cut running/downhill volume, then reintroduce load gradually; check cadence and worn footwear
Omega-3 to dampen local inflammation while it settles

causes.confusedWith: Lateral meniscus or patellofemoral pain - but ITB pain sits on the outer bony ridge and is tied tightly to running distance, not stairs or twisting

causes.name: Meniscal / structural injury

chain.node: A twisting load on a planted foot (acute), or age-related meniscal fraying (degenerative)
Meniscal tear disrupts load distribution and can create a mobile flap of tissue
Joint-line irritation, mechanical catching, and joint effusion
Sharp catching/locking or giving-way pain along the joint line, often with swelling

tell.symptoms: Pain sits along the joint line (inner or outer edge), with catching, locking, or the knee giving way. Often started with a specific twist and swelled within a day (acute), or nags with squats/stairs in an older knee (degenerative).
tell.labMarker: No lab; MRI confirms a tear. Exam: joint-line tenderness, positive McMurray

fixes.what: Quadriceps/hip strengthening + activity modification - for degenerative tears, structured rehab is non-inferior to arthroscopy at up to 5 years (ESCAPE trial)
Avoid deep squatting and loaded twisting while it settles
Omega-3 + collagen peptides to support the joint environment during rehab
Orthopaedic referral if the knee truly locks or gives way - a mechanical block (e.g. bucket-handle tear) may need arthroscopy

causes.confusedWith: Osteoarthritis - degenerative meniscal tears overlap heavily with early OA, and both can produce mechanical catching

knee-pain.theOneThing: For most non-traumatic knee pain, the single highest-leverage move is progressive strengthening of the hips (gluteus medius) and quadriceps. It restores the muscles' capacity to share load, offloads the tendon, cartilage, and IT band, and is the one intervention with evidence across nearly every cause - patellofemoral pain, tendinopathy, OA, IT band syndrome, and degenerative meniscus alike (note: for PFP it reliably cuts pain even though it may not visibly change kneecap tracking). Pair it with collagen peptides + vitamin C taken 30-60 minutes before loading to feed the connective tissue you're rebuilding, and if body weight is elevated, add a modest deficit - each pound lost removes about 4 lb of load from the knee per step.

ladder: The surface problem: blood sugar climbs and energy crashes after meals, and weight creeps on around the middle
The proximate cause: your muscle and liver cells stop obeying insulin's "open the door" signal — in muscle, the GLUT4 glucose gates that insulin is supposed to summon stay stuck inside the cell
The upstream drivers: fat spilling into the liver and muscle, rarely-contracted muscle, liquid sugar, short sleep, and chronic stress each jam that signal a different way
The root behaviours: more calories and liquid fructose coming in than muscle movement, sleep, and stress-recovery taking it back out
insulin-resistance.intro: Insulin resistance is not one disease with one cause; it is a shared failure state that several different lifestyles converge on. The common thread is simple: insulin knocks on the cell, and the cell no longer answers. But WHY it stops answering differs from person to person. For one person it is a fatty liver from a decade of sweet drinks; for another it is a body that sits fourteen hours a day so its biggest glucose-burning organ (muscle) has powered down its glucose gates; for another it is six hours of broken sleep or a nervous system stuck in stress that keeps dumping sugar into the blood. Most people with insulin resistance have two or three of these running at once, stacking on top of each other. That is why a single fix rarely works and why the goal here is diagnostic: find which of these are YOURS, because the fix for a fatty liver is different from the fix for an under-used muscle or an over-cortisoled brain.

causes.name: Visceral & ectopic fat (fat spilling into liver and muscle)

chain.node: Excess calories stored as visceral belly fat once fat cells are full
Fat overflows as free fatty acids + inflammatory adipokines (TNFα)
Diacylglycerol accumulates inside liver and muscle cells
DAG activates PKCθ, which serine-phosphorylates (Ser1101) and disables IRS-1
Insulin signal is broken; glucose and insulin both stay high in the blood

tell.symptoms: Weight sits first on the belly; expanding waistband even at a stable weight; the classic 'skinny-fat' or apple shape. This is the driver for most people whose waist has grown.
tell.labMarker: Waist circumference >90cm (Asian men) / >80cm (women, IDF); triglyceride-to-HDL ratio >3.0 in mg/dL units (≈>1.5 if your lab reports in mmol/L); ALT mildly elevated or fatty liver on ultrasound

fixes.what: Sustained modest calorie deficit plus resistance training to shrink visceral and liver fat specifically (this fat is the first to leave)
High-protein, high-fiber meals to blunt intake and preserve muscle while losing fat
GLP-1 agonist (semaglutide) or dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist (tirzepatide) for significant adiposity that isn't shifting with diet alone
Berberine to improve glucose disposal while fat loss is underway

causes.confusedWith: Cushing's syndrome (central fat plus purple striae, easy bruising, high cortisol) — rule out if central fat is extreme and appeared fast

causes.name: Physical inactivity (the muscle glucose sink powered down)

chain.node: Sedentary days: muscle rarely contracts
Skeletal muscle handles ~80% of insulin-stimulated glucose disposal
Unused muscle downregulates its GLUT4 glucose transporters and loses contraction-driven uptake
Post-meal glucose has nowhere to go and lingers high, then crashes

tell.symptoms: You may be near-normal weight but sit most of the day, have low muscle mass, and feel a heavy energy crash 1-2 hours after carb-heavy meals. IR present despite a normal waistline points here.
tell.labMarker: HOMA-IR >2.0 (early) to >2.5 (clear) or fasting insulin >10 µIU/mL despite normal-ish BMI and waist

fixes.what: A 10-15 minute walk right after your two largest meals — muscle contraction pulls glucose in WITHOUT needing insulin, bypassing the broken signal
Resistance training 2-3x/week to rebuild GLUT4-rich muscle mass (the size of your glucose sink)
Metformin (Rx) or Berberine (OTC) as an AMPK-side adjunct while you build the exercise habit

causes.confusedWith: Latent autoimmune diabetes (LADA) or type 1 — insulin DEFICIENCY, not resistance; suspect if lean, progressive, and insulin is LOW not high

causes.name: Liquid sugar & fructose (fatty liver from the inside)

chain.node: Sugar-sweetened drinks, juice, and refined carbs — high fructose load
Fructose is metabolised independent of insulin and drives hepatic de novo lipogenesis via SREBP-1c and ChREBP
New fat is deposited in the liver (steatosis)
Fatty liver becomes insulin-resistant and over-produces glucose overnight

tell.symptoms: High intake of sweet drinks, bubble tea, juice, or desserts; fasting (morning) glucose creeping up faster than post-meal readings; often a fatty liver on scan. Sugar-driven IR loads the LIVER first.
tell.labMarker: Elevated ALT; hepatic steatosis on ultrasound; fasting triglycerides elevated

fixes.what: Cut liquid fructose first — sweet drinks and juice are the single biggest lever here because fructose feeds liver fat via a pathway that keeps running even when insulin is high
Eat fiber and protein before the carb portion of a meal to flatten the glucose curve
Acarbose to blunt post-meal glucose absorption
Green tea extract as an adjunct for hepatic fat and glucose handling

causes.confusedWith: Reactive hypoglycemia (the crash without the sustained high) — overlapping symptoms but a different fasting-glucose picture

causes.name: Short or fragmented sleep (incl. sleep apnea)

chain.node: Chronically short or broken sleep, or untreated obstructive sleep apnea
Sympathetic drive and evening cortisol rise, growth-hormone/circadian signals shift
Peripheral tissues take up ~11-25% less glucose the very next day (clamp studies)
Morning glucose is higher and sugar cravings intensify

tell.symptoms: Sleeping under ~6.5 hours, snoring or daytime sleepiness, waking unrefreshed; blood sugar and cravings are noticeably worse after bad nights. A single bad week moves the needle here.
tell.labMarker: Sleep study (AHI) if apnea suspected; morning glucose elevated relative to bedtime; even one night of restriction measurably lowers insulin sensitivity

fixes.what: Protect 7-9 hours on a consistent schedule — this is one of the fastest-reversing IR drivers
Screen for and treat obstructive sleep apnea if you snore or are sleepy despite time in bed
Magnesium in the evening to support sleep depth (also directly required for insulin-receptor tyrosine-kinase signaling)

causes.confusedWith: Untreated sleep apnea itself — it is both a cause of poor sleep AND an independent driver of insulin resistance, so don't stop at 'just sleep more'

causes.name: Chronic stress (cortisol-driven)

chain.node: Sustained psychological or physiological stress
Chronically elevated cortisol (strongest at Cushing's / exogenous-steroid levels; everyday-stress version is weaker and more variable)
Liver ramps up gluconeogenesis (PEPCK/G6Pase) while insulin-stimulated muscle GLUT4 translocation is suppressed
Fasting glucose stays high despite a clean diet

tell.symptoms: High fasting glucose that doesn't match your good diet; 'wired but tired'; poor sleep, tension, central weight gain during a stressful stretch of life.
tell.labMarker: Elevated late-night salivary cortisol; fasting glucose high with a dawn-rise pattern

fixes.what: Daily stress-down-regulation: slow breathing, morning outdoor light, and a hard stop on work at night to drop evening cortisol
Ensure adequate magnesium, which is depleted by stress and needed for insulin signaling
Myo-inositol to support insulin signaling, particularly useful where stress overlaps with PCOS-type patterns

causes.confusedWith: Subclinical hypothyroidism (fatigue, weight gain, sluggishness overlap) — check TSH before assuming stress

insulin-resistance.theOneThing: Contract your muscles every day — a 10-15 minute walk after your two biggest meals, plus 2-3 short resistance sessions a week. Working muscle pulls glucose out of the blood through GLUT4 WITHOUT needing insulin, which bypasses the exact signaling block that defines insulin resistance, and rebuilding muscle enlarges your single largest glucose sink (it handles ~80% of insulin-stimulated glucose disposal). It is the one action that helps regardless of which driver is yours.

ladder: Surface: you can't switch off - racing thoughts, chest tension, a low hum of dread
Proximate: your brain is stuck in over-arousal - excitatory glutamate and adrenaline are outrunning your GABA 'brakes', and the amygdala is firing on high alert
Upstream: several different switches keep flipping that alarm on - stress hormones, caffeine and alcohol rebound, blood-sugar crashes, sleep debt, and a breathing pattern that keeps CO2 too low
Root: it comes down to what you drink and when, what and when you eat, how much you sleep, how you breathe, and whether you ever actually downshift the stress axis
anxiety.intro: Anxiety feels like one thing - a mind that won't stand down - but underneath it is almost always the same physics: your brain's excitatory signalling (glutamate, adrenaline) is running faster than its inhibitory braking (GABA), so the threat-detection circuitry stays switched on when nothing is actually wrong. What differs person to person is WHY the alarm keeps getting tripped. For one person it's a stress axis that never resets after years of pressure; for another it's the third coffee, or the wine the night before; for another it's a blood-sugar crash at 4pm that dumps adrenaline into a calm afternoon; for another it's simply not sleeping enough; for another it's a fast, shallow breathing habit quietly keeping CO2 too low. Most people have two or three of these running at once. The point of this page is to help you find YOURS - because the fix for a caffeine-driven jitter is nothing like the fix for a stress-axis stuck in overdrive.

causes.name: Chronic stress / HPA-axis overdrive

chain.node: Sustained or perceived stress load (work, money, relationships)
Amygdala drives CRH release from the hypothalamus
Persistent ACTH-driven cortisol signalling with blunted negative feedback
Amygdala becomes hyperreactive while hippocampal/prefrontal 'off switch' weakens
Baseline worry, tension, feeling permanently on-edge

tell.symptoms: Worst under deadlines and open loops; 'wired but tired'; often peaks in the morning and you struggle to switch off even in downtime. Tension lives in jaw/shoulders/gut.
tell.labMarker: Salivary diurnal cortisol - typically a flattened curve or elevated evening value (note: cortisol and anxiety correlate inconsistently, so this supports rather than proves the link). A low DHEA-S relative to cortisol is a soft supporting sign. Single blood cortisol is noisy and often normal - don't rule it out on one draw.

fixes.what: Daily deliberate downshift: 5-10 min of slow-exhale breathing (exhale longer than inhale) to raise vagal tone and close the stress loop
Regular zone-2 aerobic exercise - the most reliable non-drug way to lower resting sympathetic tone
Ashwagandha - repeatedly lowers serum cortisol and subjective stress/anxiety in human RCTs (roughly 25-28% cortisol drop vs placebo)
Magnesium - blunts HPA-axis output and is commonly depleted by chronic stress

causes.confusedWith: Subclinical hyperthyroidism - check TSH before assuming it's 'just stress', especially with tremor, weight loss or heat intolerance.

causes.name: Stimulant load & substance rebound (caffeine, alcohol, nicotine)

chain.node: High or late caffeine, alcohol the night before, or nicotine
Caffeine antagonises adenosine A1 and A2A receptors, removing adenosine's natural calming brake on arousal
Alcohol/nicotine rebound: GABA is down-regulated and glutamate surges as the drug wears off
Sympathetic over-arousal - raised heart rate, tremor
Jitteriness, palpitations, panic-like surges

tell.symptoms: Anxiety tracks the cup of coffee (palpitations within an hour), or is worst the morning after drinking ('hangxiety'). Slow caffeine metabolisers get it from a single cup. Improves noticeably on a caffeine-free day.
tell.labMarker: No specific lab; a persistently elevated resting heart rate on a wearable that falls when you cut stimulants is supportive.

fixes.what: Cap caffeine at ~200mg/day and none after midday; trial a 2-week taper to see if your anxiety is caffeine-shaped
If you keep coffee, co-ingest L-Theanine - it blunts the jittery adrenergic edge without killing the alertness
Reduce and space alcohol; the next-day GABA/glutamate rebound is a direct, reversible anxiety driver

causes.name: Blood-sugar instability / reactive hypoglycemia

chain.node: Refined-carb or high-sugar meal, often on an empty stomach or after skipped meals
Exaggerated insulin release drives a reactive glucose crash 1.5-3h later
Counter-regulatory adrenaline and cortisol surge to pull glucose back up
Palpitations, tremor, sweating - the physical body of a panic attack
Sudden anxiety between meals that eases fast once you eat

tell.symptoms: Anxiety spikes mid-morning or late afternoon, comes with a shaky/'hangry' feeling, and lifts within minutes of eating. Worse after a sugary breakfast or a skipped meal.
tell.labMarker: Symptoms coinciding with a low glucose during an episode confirm the reactive crash; HbA1c or HOMA-IR (>~2.5) flags the insulin resistance that deepens the swings.

fixes.what: Anchor meals with protein, fat and fibre first; cut refined sugar and don't skip meals
A short walk after meals blunts the glucose spike that sets up the crash
Magnesium - improves insulin sensitivity and flattens the swings

causes.confusedWith: Panic disorder - the adrenaline symptoms are near-identical, so time your episodes against meals before concluding it's primary panic.

chain.node: Short or fragmented sleep, chronic sleep debt
Loss of prefrontal (mPFC) top-down control over the amygdala
Amygdala and anterior insula show anticipatory hyperreactivity to threat
Next-day anticipatory worry, edginess and dread

tell.symptoms: Anxiety is clearly worse after bad nights and eases after good ones. If you're a natural worrier, one short night hits you disproportionately hard (this is a replicated experimental finding, not just correlation).
tell.labMarker: No diagnostic lab; a drop in overnight/morning HRV on a wearable tracks the over-arousal and tends to fall with sleep debt (supportive, not diagnostic).

fixes.what: Fixed wake time, 7-9h opportunity, morning daylight, and a screen + caffeine curfew
Apigenin (as in chamomile) - mild GABA-A/benzodiazepine-site activity, may help sleep onset
Magnesium and/or L-Theanine at night to lower pre-sleep arousal

chain.node: Low magnesium intake or high demand, plus constitutionally low GABA tone
Too little magnesium to block the NMDA receptor, so glutamate over-excites
Weak GABA-A inhibitory braking on top of that excitation
Cortical and limbic hyperexcitability
Racing thoughts, muscle tension, an inability to wind down even when calm is 'allowed'

tell.symptoms: A constant baseline of tension rather than discrete episodes; muscle cramps/twitches, poor relaxation, often a low-magnesium diet. You feel keyed-up even when nothing is wrong.
tell.labMarker: Serum magnesium is a poor marker - it often reads normal despite tissue deficiency; RBC magnesium is more sensitive. Don't rule magnesium out on a normal serum level. (The NMDA/GABA pharmacology is solid, but human evidence that low magnesium causes clinical anxiety is limited and mostly preclinical - treat this as a plausible contributor, not a proven root.)

fixes.what: Magnesium (glycinate is well-tolerated and calming) - restores the NMDA block and supports GABA-A sensitivity
Magnesium-rich foods daily: leafy greens, nuts, seeds, legumes
L-Theanine and Apigenin - both nudge the excitation/inhibition balance back toward inhibition

causes.name: Dysfunctional breathing / chronic over-breathing (hypocapnia)

chain.node: Habitual fast, shallow, upper-chest or mouth breathing - often a learned stress response
Over-breathing blows off too much CO2, driving hypocapnia and respiratory alkalosis
Alkalosis binds free blood calcium and constricts cerebral vessels
Tingling, dizziness, air hunger, chest tightness, palpitations
Panic-like surges and a sense you 'can't get a full breath'

tell.symptoms: You sigh or yawn a lot, breathe through the mouth, feel short of breath at rest, and get tingling/light-headedness during surges. Symptoms ease when you deliberately slow down and nasal-breathe.
tell.labMarker: No routine lab; capnometry showing low resting end-tidal CO2 (below ~35 mmHg) confirms chronic hypocapnia (specialist setting; supportive, not diagnostic alone).

fixes.what: Breathing retraining: slow nasal breathing with a longer exhale and gentle air-hunger tolerance to raise resting CO2 tolerance (capnometry-guided if available)
During a surge, resist over-breathing - slow the rate and breathe low into the belly rather than gulping air

causes.confusedWith: Panic disorder and asthma - the breathlessness overlaps; a normal exam with symptoms reproduced by voluntary over-breathing points to a breathing-pattern problem.

anxiety.theOneThing: Audit your caffeine and morning-after alcohol first. It's the single most common hidden driver, it fully mimics 'real' anxiety (adenosine-blocked over-arousal plus GABA rebound), and it's the fastest to test: cap caffeine at ~200mg with nothing after midday, cut the evening drinks, and see how much of your anxiety disappears within two weeks. If it does, you've found a driver you can control for free. If it doesn't, you've cleanly ruled it out and can move to the stress-axis, blood-sugar, sleep and breathing levers.

acne.intro: Acne is never just "dirty skin." Every spot starts the same way — an oil gland over-produces sebum, the pore's lining turns sticky and plugs, and Cutibacterium acnes blooms inside the trapped oil and triggers inflammation — but what DRIVES that cascade differs from person to person. For one person it's blood-sugar and dairy spikes flooding the skin with insulin and IGF-1; for another it's androgens carving deep, tender cysts along the jaw; for a third it's a comedogenic moisturiser, a mask, or a stressful, sleep-starved month. Most people have two or three of these running at once. So the job here isn't to find THE cause of acne — it's to find YOURS, because the fix for hormonal jaw cysts is nothing like the fix for a pore-clogging cream.
ladder: Spots, oiliness and clogged pores show up on the surface
The pore plugs and its oil gland over-produces and inflames
Sebum plus androgen/IGF-1 signalling ramps up while dead cells and C. acnes clog the follicle
Driven underneath by blood-sugar spikes, dairy, hormones, stress and pore-clogging products — usually a mix of two or three
acne.theOneThing: A nightly topical retinoid (adapalene or tretinoin) is the single highest-leverage move for most people, because it normalises the plugged follicle that every form of acne funnels through. If your breakouts clearly flare with sugary food or dairy, pair it with cutting high-glycemic carbs and trialling dairy removal — that combination covers the two most common drivers at once. One caveat: deep, scarring or widespread nodulocystic acne warrants a doctor early rather than self-treatment, because oral options (isotretinoin, or a hormonal route in women) prevent permanent scarring that topicals alone won't.

causes.name: Insulin / IGF-1 diet axis (high-glycemic carbs + dairy)

chain.node: High-glycemic carbs, sugary drinks + dairy (skim milk, whey)
Insulin & IGF-1 surge
AKT suppresses FoxO1, activating mTORC1/SREBP-1
Sebocyte lipogenesis and androgen-receptor activity rise
Oily skin, comedones and inflammatory papules

tell.symptoms: Oily T-zone and jaw; breakouts flare 1-2 days after sugary meals, sodas or a whey protein shake; often paired with sugar cravings or central weight gain. Honest tiering: the insulin/IGF-1 to FoxO1/mTORC1/SREBP-1 to sebum mechanism is well established in vitro and in Melnik's model; low-glycemic-diet trials show real acne improvement (moderate human evidence, tier 2); the dairy link specifically is observational only, with no randomized trials, so treat it as an association to test, not a proven cause.
tell.labMarker: Fasting insulin / HOMA-IR elevated; HbA1c high-normal; fasting glucose to screen for insulin resistance.

fixes.what: Cut high-glycemic carbs and sugary drinks; build meals around protein, fibre and low-GI carbs
Trial removing milk and whey protein for 6-8 weeks (both are strongly insulinotropic despite milk's low GI)
Add soluble fibre to blunt post-meal glucose and insulin spikes
Zinc — modest adjunct; lowers sebum and inflammation downstream rather than touching the insulin axis itself
Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) to dampen sebocyte inflammation

causes.confusedWith: PCOS-driven acne — also insulin-linked, but with irregular cycles, extra facial/body hair and menstrual timing

causes.name: Androgen-driven (hormonal / jawline) acne

chain.node: Higher androgens (testosterone) and/or low SHBG raising free androgen
5-alpha-reductase converts testosterone to DHT in the skin
DHT binds the sebocyte androgen receptor
Sebaceous gland enlarges and sebum output climbs
Deep, tender cysts on jaw/chin/neck; premenstrual flares

tell.symptoms: Lesions cluster on the lower face, jawline and neck, deep and cystic rather than surface bumps; in women they flare the week before the period and may come with irregular cycles, excess facial/body hair or scalp thinning.
tell.labMarker: Free and total testosterone and DHEAS high or high-normal; SHBG low (low SHBG raises free androgen); consider PCOS workup in women with irregular cycles.

fixes.what: Lower dietary insulin load — insulin lowers SHBG and amplifies androgen action at the gland
Nightly topical retinoid to normalise the follicle regardless of hormone level
Spironolactone or a combined oral contraceptive (women) — blocks the androgen receptor / lowers free androgen; requires a doctor
Zinc — modest 5-alpha-reductase inhibition (~30%) plus anti-inflammatory effect; adjunct, not a substitute for anti-androgens

causes.confusedWith: PCOS specifically — worth confirming or excluding if cycles are irregular

causes.name: Follicular plugging + C. acnes inflammation

chain.node: Abnormal keratinocyte shedding + sebum low in linoleic acid
Microcomedo (plugged follicle) forms
C. acnes overgrowth (loss of phylotype diversity, IA1 dominance) and biofilm in trapped sebum
TLR2 innate-immune activation and IL-1 inflammation
Blackheads, whiteheads, red papules and pustules

tell.symptoms: Lots of blackheads, whiteheads and small gritty bumps (comedones), not just deep cysts; skin feels rough; improves with exfoliation and retinoids but barely responds to diet changes alone. This follicular-plugging-plus-C.-acnes cascade is the shared final common pathway of essentially all acne — it is not overgrowth of a foreign bug but a shift in the skin's own C. acnes population toward the pro-inflammatory IA1 phylotype.
tell.labMarker: None routine — diagnosis is clinical; bacterial culture rarely adds value.

fixes.what: Topical retinoid nightly — the core fix; normalises shedding and clears microcomedones
Niacinamide to calm inflammation and support the barrier
Benzoyl peroxide to reduce C. acnes without driving antibiotic resistance
Omega-3 and linoleic-acid-rich fats — acne sebum runs low in linoleic acid (~6% vs ~45% in clear skin), which promotes plugging

causes.confusedWith: Fungal (Malassezia) folliculitis — uniform, itchy same-size bumps that worsen on antibiotics

causes.name: Cosmetic / mechanical (acne cosmetica & barrier damage)

chain.node: Comedogenic products, occlusion, friction, or over-washing
Pore occlusion plus a stripped, damaged barrier
Reactive irritation and impaired barrier lowering the threshold for plugging
Bumps mapped to where products, mask or hair touch skin

tell.symptoms: Breakouts map to where you apply heavy creams, wear a mask/helmet, rest your phone or hands, or use hair products (hairline/forehead); often started after a new product or an aggressive scrubbing routine. Evidence is largely clinical/pattern-based (tier 2) — comedogenicity is real but individual product reactions vary.
tell.labMarker: None — diagnosis is pattern-based.

fixes.what: Switch to non-comedogenic, fragrance-light skincare and stop the suspect new product to test
Stop over-washing and harsh scrubs; gentle cleanser twice daily to protect the barrier
Niacinamide to rebuild the barrier and reduce reactive oiliness

causes.confusedWith: True inflammatory acne — cosmetica usually clears fast once the trigger is removed

causes.name: Stress / cortisol / poor sleep

chain.node: Chronic stress and short sleep
CRH acts on sebocyte CRH-R1 (autocrine), driving lipogenesis and 3-beta-HSD
More sebum, higher systemic inflammation, worse insulin sensitivity
Flares during deadline weeks and poor-sleep stretches

tell.symptoms: Breakouts track your stress and sleep — flaring in high-pressure or exam/deadline weeks and calming on holiday; often paired with jaw-clenching, poor sleep and high stress load. The CRH-to-sebocyte lipogenesis mechanism is confirmed in vitro; the stress-to-acne link in people is observational (tier 2).
tell.labMarker: No routine test; a morning cortisol only if an endocrine cause is genuinely suspected.

fixes.what: Protect 7-9 hours of sleep — sleep loss worsens insulin sensitivity and inflammation
Daily stress down-regulation — training, breathwork, morning sunlight
Magnesium to support sleep and the stress axis
Omega-3 to lower baseline inflammatory tone

causes.confusedWith: Diet-driven acne — stress weeks often also mean more sugar and dairy, which can be the real driver

low-back-pain.intro: Most lower-back pain is not one thing wrongly labelled \"a bad back\" - it is a final common symptom that several different chains produce, and yours is usually a blend. For a desk-bound person it is often muscular: hours of sitting shorten the hip flexors, leave the glutes deconditioned and underused, and force the spinal erectors to hold you up all day until they ache. For someone whose back suddenly \"tweaks,\" the deep stabilisers (transversus abdominis, multifidus) fire a fraction of a second too late, so a heavy or awkward movement lands on unguarded tissue. Others have a disc that has lost height and leaks inflammatory chemicals onto a nerve root, producing pain that can radiate down the leg - while others have facet joints at the back of the spine that flare with leaning back, standing and twisting, the mirror image of the disc pattern. And in pain that has dragged on for months, the nervous system itself has often turned up the gain, so the hurt outlives the original injury. The job of this page is to help you find WHICH of these is mostly yours - because the fix for a deconditioned posterior chain is nearly the opposite of the fix for a sensitised nervous system, and the fix for a disc is nearly the opposite of the fix for a facet.
ladder: Your back hurts
→ the tissue actually generating the pain (overloaded erectors, an irritated facet joint, a sensitised nerve root, a poorly-controlled segment, or an amplified nervous system)
→ what put that tissue under strain (all-day sitting, a weak/uncoordinated posterior chain, a disc or facet that has degenerated, poor sleep + stress, and - as a minor modifier - low vitamin D)
→ the daily behaviours you can change: how much you sit, how you load and train your hips and core, how you sleep, and what you eat

causes.name: Deconditioning + all-day sitting (weak posterior chain, tight hips)

chain.node: Prolonged sitting most of the day
Hip flexors shorten and glutes become deconditioned/underactive from disuse (the 'reciprocal inhibition' model is proposed but not cleanly proven)
Increased lumbar lordosis / sustained loading; erector spinae hold you upright all day
Dull, aching lower back that eases once you move

tell.symptoms: A dull background ache that is WORSE after long sitting or standing still and better once you walk or move; stiffness first thing or after a movie; glutes feel weak, hip flexors feel tight. No leg pain, no numbness.
tell.labMarker: None - this is a clinical/movement diagnosis. Weak single-leg glute bridge, poor hip hinge, and short hip flexors (Thomas test) point to it.

fixes.what: Break up sitting every 30-45 min; stand/walk 2-3 min
Rebuild the posterior chain: progressive hip hinges, glute bridges/hip thrusts, dead bugs, walking 8-10k steps
Daily hip-flexor and hamstring mobility to unload the lumbar erectors
Creatine to support strength-training adaptation and muscle capacity
Adequate protein (~1.6 g/kg) to actually build the posterior-chain muscle you train
Collagen peptides (with vitamin C, taken before loading) to support tendon/connective-tissue tolerance

causes.confusedWith: Hip osteoarthritis or gluteal tendinopathy, which can also present as deep buttock/low-back ache but hurt on direct hip loading.

causes.name: Poor motor control / core stability (the back that 'tweaks')

chain.node: Sudden, heavy, or twisting load
Delayed feed-forward activation of transversus abdominis and multifidus (documented in LBP, though it may be partly a consequence of pain rather than only a cause)
A lumbar segment moves before it is braced → local tissue strain
Sharp 'tweak', spasm, and days of guarding

tell.symptoms: Pain arrives as sudden episodes triggered by a specific movement (bending to a sock, lifting a box, a sneeze) rather than a constant ache; between flares you can feel relatively fine; the back feels 'unreliable' or like it could go at any time.
tell.labMarker: None - clinical. Reproduced by unguarded flexion/rotation; improves with abdominal bracing cues.

fixes.what: Motor-control / core-stability training: dead bugs, bird-dogs, Pallof press, side planks - bracing before load (note: general strengthening works too; deep-core-specific drills aren't proven superior)
Groove the hip hinge so bending/lifting is done at the hips, not the lumbar spine
Magnesium as a mild adjunct for muscle relaxation/cramping after a flare (weak evidence)
Omega-3 to modestly modulate the inflammatory response to minor tissue strain (adjunct only)

causes.confusedWith: A true acute disc or facet injury - if a 'tweak' comes with leg pain, numbness, or weakness, it is more than a control issue.

causes.name: Discogenic / radicular pain (disc degeneration or herniation)

chain.node: Annular tear / disc degeneration, often after repeated flexion-loading
Nucleus pulposus leaks and triggers an inflammatory cascade (TNF-alpha, IL-1beta) around the nerve root
Nerve root edema + nociceptor sensitisation ('chemical radiculitis'), with or without mechanical compression
Deep central back pain, often radiating into the buttock/leg, worse with sitting, bending, and coughing/sneezing

tell.symptoms: Pain that shoots or radiates below the knee, or numbness/tingling/weakness in the leg or foot; worse sitting and bending forward, sometimes eased by standing/walking; a positive straight-leg-raise. Note: many disc bulges on scans are painless (and 20-76% of root compressions are painless), so imaging alone doesn't prove it.
tell.labMarker: MRI shows herniation/degeneration but correlates poorly with pain - reserve for red flags or persistent radicular signs, not routine back ache.

fixes.what: Most resolve conservatively over weeks: stay active within tolerance, avoid prolonged flexed sitting, direction-specific (often extension) loading with a physio
Omega-3 to modestly dampen the inflammatory drive of the nerve-root irritation (adjunct, not a cure)
Seek medical review urgently for red flags: saddle numbness, bladder/bowel changes, or progressive leg weakness

causes.confusedWith: Piriformis syndrome or SI-joint pain, which can mimic sciatica but arise from the buttock, not the disc/nerve root.

causes.name: Facet (zygapophyseal) joint pain - the extension-pattern back

chain.node: Repeated lumbar extension/rotation loading, sustained standing, and age-related facet degeneration
Facet joint capsule irritation / osteoarthritis; the joint is richly innervated by the medial branches of the dorsal rami
Localised facet nociception with reflex paraspinal guarding
Localised (often one-sided) low back pain worse leaning back, standing and twisting; eased by sitting/bending forward

tell.symptoms: Pain WORSE with extension (leaning back, standing, walking) and twisting, and getting up out of a chair; BETTER when you bend forward or sit - the mirror image of disc pain. Usually one-sided, may refer into the buttock/thigh but rarely below the knee, with no true numbness or weakness.
tell.labMarker: None - clinical. No blood or imaging test confirms it; diagnostic medial-branch blocks are the reference standard but reserved for persistent cases. Facet arthrosis is common on scans and often painless, so imaging alone doesn't prove it.

fixes.what: Flexion-biased loading and hip-hinge mechanics to offload the facets; avoid sustained hyperextension (this is the OPPOSITE of the disc protocol)
Strengthen glutes/core and improve hip mobility so the lumbar spine isn't the hinge for extension movements
Omega-3 as a mild adjunct to low-grade joint inflammation (adjunct only)

causes.confusedWith: Discogenic pain (which does the opposite - worse with flexion/sitting), SI-joint pain, or hip osteoarthritis.

causes.name: Central sensitisation + poor sleep/stress (chronic, amplified pain)

chain.node: Pain persisting past normal tissue-healing time, plus poor sleep, stress, and fear of movement

Impaired descending inhibition (reduced conditioned pain modulation) + heightened dorsal-horn excitability, driven substantially by poor sleep (HPA/cortisol involvement is associative, not established as causal)
Central nervous system turns up pain gain - normal input is read as painful (hyperalgesia/allodynia)
Widespread, disproportionate, hard-to-localise pain that outlasts any injury

tell.symptoms: Pain that has lasted 3+ months, feels out of proportion to any structural finding, spreads or moves around, is worse on bad-sleep or high-stress days, and is often accompanied by fatigue or low mood. Multiple 'normal' scans.
tell.labMarker: None specific - diagnosed clinically (e.g. Central Sensitisation Inventory), not by imaging or blood test.

fixes.what: Prioritise sleep (7-9 h, consistent schedule) - even one night of total sleep loss measurably raises pain sensitivity, so this is a high-leverage target
Graded activity + pain education to reverse fear-avoidance; keep moving rather than resting
Stress regulation: breathing, walking outdoors, downshifting load
Magnesium as a mild adjunct for sleep quality and neuromuscular signalling (modest evidence)
Omega-3 for its role in resolving low-grade neuroinflammation (adjunct)

causes.confusedWith: Inflammatory back pain (e.g. ankylosing spondylitis / axial spondyloarthritis): onset before age 45, morning stiffness >30 min, night pain that wakes you, better with exercise not rest - needs rheumatology, not just self-management.

causes.name: Vitamin D deficiency / nutritional contributor (minor modifier)

chain.node: Low sun exposure / low intake → deficient serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D
Vitamin D signalling may influence muscle function and inflammation (VDR expression in mature skeletal muscle is itself debated); link to back pain is associative, not proven causal
Associated with muscle weakness/aches and higher inflammatory tone that may lower pain threshold
May contribute to background chronic musculoskeletal ache, usually stacked on another cause

tell.symptoms: Diffuse muscle aches and weakness alongside the back pain, low sun exposure, indoor lifestyle, darker skin at higher latitude, easy fatigue. This is usually a MODIFIER stacked on another cause, not the sole driver.
tell.labMarker: Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D: deficiency typically <20 ng/mL (50 nmol/L), insufficiency 20-30 ng/mL. Note: the large UK Biobank cohort found NO association with low back pain after full adjustment (severe deficiency linked only to chronic WIDESPREAD pain), so treat as tier-1 associative.

fixes.what: Test 25-OH-D and correct a genuine deficiency with vitamin D3 (dose to level, retest)
Regular sensible sun exposure and vitamin-D-containing foods (oily fish, eggs)
Don't expect supplementing a normal level to fix back pain - correct only true deficiency

causes.confusedWith: Other metabolic bone/muscle causes - if pain is bony, worse at night, or with unexplained weight loss, rule out pathology before blaming vitamin D.

low-back-pain.theOneThing: For most people the back pain is a load problem, not a damage problem: stop sitting still for hours and start progressively loading the posterior chain (daily walking plus hip hinges, glute bridges/hip thrusts, and core-bracing drills like dead bugs). Movement and strength - not rest - are the highest-leverage fix, and they directly counter the two most common drivers (deconditioning and poor motor control). The main exception is direction: if your pain is clearly worse bending forward/sitting it's likely disc-pattern (bias toward extension), and if it's clearly worse leaning back/standing it's likely facet-pattern (bias toward flexion) - so match the loading direction to the pattern.

ladder: Aching, tight neck and shoulders (and sometimes a tension headache or pain down the arm)
A structure is under strain: overloaded, oxygen-starved upper-trap/levator muscles, an irritated cervical facet joint, an impinged/loaded rotator-cuff tendon, or a compressed nerve root
Upstream: sustained low-level muscle contraction from static loading and unmanaged stress, deconditioned deep neck flexors + lower traps, or age-related disc/facet/tendon wear
Root behaviours: hours of static desk/phone loading with the head forward, shallow stressed breathing, and no counter-strengthening
neck-shoulder.intro: Your neck and shoulders are the body's shock absorber for both a heavy head held in a fixed position and a nervous system stuck in 'on'. Most neck-and-shoulder pain is not one thing gone wrong but a stack: the same aching upper trap can come from eight hours of near-static desk work, from a stressed mind that never lets those muscles switch off, from a knotted trigger point, from an irritated cervical facet joint, from a loaded rotator-cuff tendon, or from a nerve root compressed by an aging disc. Each produces a slightly different pain signature, and the fix for one does almost nothing for another. So the useful question is not 'how do I stop neck pain' but 'which of these is mine' — the person whose pain fades on holiday needs a very different plan from the one whose arm goes numb when they look up. One honest caveat up front: the popular 'bad posture causes pain' story is weaker than it sounds — measured posture correlates only loosely with pain, and the better-evidenced culprit is sustained static loading (how long you hold a position), not the exact angle you hold it at. Read the 'how to tell' for each and find the one (or two) that match you.

causes.name: Sustained static neck/shoulder loading ('tech neck')

chain.node: Hours of near-static desk/phone use holding the head and arms in a fixed forward position

Low-threshold 'Cinderella' motor units in the upper trapezius and levator scapulae stay continuously active and never fully switch off; deep neck flexors and lower/middle trapezius add little support (the forward-head, rounded-shoulder pattern)
Sustained low-level contraction compresses intramuscular capillaries → local ischemia/hypoxia and metabolite build-up in the upper trap and levator scapulae
Aching neck/shoulder tightness, stiffness, a band across the top of the shoulders, tension headache

tell.symptoms: Dull ache and tightness that builds through the workday and eases when you stand, walk or lie down; a tender band along the top of the shoulders; relieved by moving, not by rest. Forward-head/rounded-shoulder posture is often visible on a side photo, but treat that as a hint, not proof — how long you hold a position matters more than the exact angle.
tell.labMarker: No blood test. Functional signs only: reduced deep-neck-flexor (craniocervical flexion) endurance — a chin-tuck hold that fatigues well under ~30s, versus ~38-40s in pain-free people.

fixes.what: Break up static loading: raise the screen toward eye level and, every ~30 min, do a chin-tuck + shoulder-blade squeeze reset — the interruption of continuous loading is the active ingredient, more than any single 'correct' posture
Build the muscles that hold your head up: daily deep-neck-flexor (chin tucks) and lower-trap/serratus strengthening; stretch pecs and upper trap
Magnesium — worth it mainly to correct a deficiency; may modestly ease muscle tightness, but supplementation evidence in non-deficient people is weak (do not expect it to fix the loading problem)

causes.confusedWith: Cervical radiculopathy — if the pain shoots down the arm past the elbow or brings numbness/tingling, it is a nerve, not loading. The 'upper-crossed syndrome' muscle-imbalance label is a useful teaching model but not a proven cause of pain.

chain.node: Chronic psychological or occupational stress / anxiety
Sympathetic (SAM) and HPA activation raise resting muscle tone via catecholamines and cortisol
Low-threshold 'Cinderella' motor units in the upper trap keep firing at rest and never fully relax — measurable as raised resting trapezius EMG even during no-effort mental tasks
Continuous micro-contraction → local ischemia and micro-trauma with no structural injury
Persistent neck/shoulder tightness that can outlast the stressor

tell.symptoms: Pain tracks your stress level more than your activity; shoulders creep up toward the ears, jaw clenching, worse in anxious/busy weeks, noticeably better on holiday. Rest doesn't fix it because the muscle never switches off.
tell.labMarker: No reliable blood marker; the pattern (pain follows stress; raised resting trapezius EMG on testing) is the tell.

fixes.what: Down-regulate the nervous system: daily slow-breathing / relaxation, and consciously drop the shoulders during focused work
Prioritise sleep and regular aerobic exercise — both lower baseline sympathetic tone
Magnesium — supports muscle relaxation and may blunt stress-related neuromuscular excitability, strongest if you are actually low; not a substitute for reducing the stress load

chain.node: Sustained overload, static loading, stress or an acute muscle strain
Leading (but still unproven) 'integrated hypothesis': excess acetylcholine at the motor endplate drives a sustained localized sarcomere contracture — the palpable taut band
Local energy crisis and ischemia → release of substance P, bradykinin, protons (acidic pH), CGRP
Nociceptor sensitization → a palpable tender knot with a characteristic referred-pain pattern

tell.symptoms: You can feel discrete painful knots; pressing one reproduces or refers pain elsewhere (e.g. up to the temple or down the shoulder blade); sometimes a local twitch. Pain is focal and spot-specific rather than a diffuse ache.
tell.labMarker: No blood test and no imaging confirms it; diagnosis is by palpation of the taut band and reproducible referred pain — and note the trigger-point construct itself is debated, with inconsistent inter-examiner agreement.

fixes.what: Trigger-point release — sustained pressure/massage, foam-ball work, or dry needling — plus heat
Correct the upstream driver (static loading/stress) so the knots don't reform
Magnesium — deficiency raises neuromuscular excitability and is associated with muscle cramping/tightness; correct it if low

causes.name: Cervical facet (zygapophyseal) joint pain

chain.node: Age-related cartilage wear and cumulative loading of the cervical facet joints, sometimes triggered by a sudden awkward movement or a night in a sustained end-range position
The richly innervated facet joint capsule becomes mechanically sensitized; nociceptive afferents in the cervical medial branches fire
Nociceptive input arising from the joint itself — no nerve-root compression
Localized, often one-sided axial neck pain that can refer to the shoulder girdle, upper back or base of skull but NOT down the arm

tell.symptoms: One-sided neck pain that worsens when you tip your head back or rotate toward the painful side; can refer to the shoulder blade or base of the skull but stops at the shoulder — no numbness, tingling or weakness in the arm/hand; often stiff on waking or after holding one position.
tell.labMarker: No blood test, and X-ray/MRI degeneration correlates poorly with pain; the reference standard is diagnostic cervical medial-branch (facet) nerve blocks.

fixes.what: Range-of-motion work, cervical/scapular strengthening, manual therapy and activity pacing; avoid holding sustained end-range extension
For persistent, block-confirmed facet pain, cervical medial-branch blocks and radiofrequency neurotomy can give months of relief

causes.confusedWith: Cervical radiculopathy — facet pain stays in the neck and shoulder girdle with no arm numbness, tingling or weakness; if symptoms travel down the arm it is the nerve root, not the joint.

causes.name: Rotator-cuff related shoulder pain (impingement / tendinopathy)

chain.node: Repetitive or excessive overhead loading, often with scapular dyskinesis (weak lower trap/serratus lets the shoulder blade tilt)

Tendon load exceeds capacity — traditionally framed as reduced subacromial space 'pinching' the supraspinatus on elevation, though current models emphasise load-related tendon changes over pure mechanical impingement
Tendon micro-tears, collagen disorganization and tendinopathy
Pain lifting the arm overhead, a painful mid-range arc, and a deep ache at night lying on that shoulder

tell.symptoms: Pain is at the shoulder (not the neck), triggered by reaching overhead or behind; a painful arc roughly 60-120° of raising the arm; classic night ache lying on that side. Positive Hawkins-Kennedy / empty-can on exam.
tell.labMarker: No blood marker; ultrasound or MRI images the tendon if it persists (but incidental tendon changes are common in painless shoulders, so image only when it changes management).

fixes.what: Progressive rotator-cuff and scapular (lower-trap/serratus) strengthening; temporarily reduce overhead load while it settles — exercise, not rest or surgery, is first-line
Collagen peptides (or vitamin-C-enriched gelatin) taken ~30-60 min before loading, to raise tendon collagen synthesis — the effect needs the exercise stimulus to matter
Omega-3 as a modest anti-inflammatory adjunct for tendon pain (supportive, not a substitute for loading)
BPC-157 has shown tendon-healing effects, but evidence is almost entirely animal with only tiny uncontrolled human data (tier 1) — not established or approved in humans

causes.confusedWith: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) — that loses PASSIVE range of motion in all directions, whereas rotator-cuff pain keeps passive range.

chain.node: Age-related disc degeneration, disc-osteophyte complex or foraminal narrowing (cervical spondylosis), or an acute disc herniation
Nerve-root compression and inflammation with intraradicular ischemia and disrupted axoplasmic transport
Irritation/inflammation of a C5-C7 nerve root (C6 and C7 most common)
Neck pain radiating into the shoulder and arm with dermatomal numbness, tingling or weakness

tell.symptoms: Pain shoots down the arm past the elbow; numbness/tingling in specific fingers; possible grip or arm weakness; worse with neck extension/rotation toward the side (Spurling's), often relieved by resting the hand on top of the head (shoulder-abduction relief sign).
tell.labMarker: No blood test; MRI shows the compression and EMG/nerve conduction studies confirm the affected root.

fixes.what: Get clinically evaluated — imaging is warranted, and progressive weakness/numbness or any signs of cord compression (clumsy hands, gait change) are red flags needing prompt review
Guided postural work and nerve-gliding exercises; most cases improve conservatively over weeks to months
Omega-3 as an anti-inflammatory adjunct only — it does not decompress a structurally compressed root

neck-shoulder.theOneThing: For most people the everyday driver is sustained static loading plus stress (causes 1-3), and the highest-leverage move is not perfect posture but interrupting the load: raise your screen toward eye level and take a 30-second chin-tuck + shoulder-drop reset every half hour, while building the deep neck flexors and lower traps that hold your head up for you. Break the all-day continuous contraction and most neck/shoulder pain unwinds — but if pain travels down the arm with numbness or weakness, stop self-treating and get the nerve checked.

ladder: You feel hip pain and stiffness — but that ache is a downstream signal, not the cause
One hip structure is being overloaded: a tendon on the side, the joint cartilage in the groin, or the labrum deep inside (and rarely, the joint is inflamed rather than mechanically overloaded)
It's overloaded because the glutes/abductors aren't sharing the work, the joint is compressed or worn, or bony shape forces impingement

Root behaviours: too much sitting, too little progressive hip strengthening, compressive positions (crossed legs, side-sleeping), and unmanaged bodyweight/load — while first ruling out a non-mechanical inflammatory driver
hip-mobility.intro: Hip pain almost never has one cause — it has a location, and the location tells you which structure is failing. Pain on the outside (lateral) is usually an irritated gluteal tendon; pain in the groin is usually the joint itself (arthritis or a torn labrum); a pinch at the front on standing up is usually tight hip flexors and sleepy glutes from a chair-bound day. Each of these is a different mechanical story, they respond to different fixes, and two people with 'hip pain' can need almost opposite things. One caveat before you self-treat: stiffness that is worst in the morning for more than 30–60 minutes and eases with activity (rather than rest), especially in someone under 45 with night pain, points to an inflammatory joint problem, not a mechanical one — that needs a doctor, not a strengthening plan. So the job here is not to find THE cause — it's to find YOURS. Point to exactly where it hurts and when, and the driver usually names itself.

causes.name: Gluteal tendinopathy (greater trochanteric pain syndrome)

chain.node: Repetitive compression + friction of the glute med/min tendons over the greater trochanter (crossing legs, hip-hanging, side-sleeping, sudden mileage jumps)
Load exceeds tendon repair capacity → collagen disorganises and degenerates (degeneration, not classic inflammation; any bursal inflammation is secondary)
Gluteus medius/minimus enthesis at the greater trochanter (± trochanteric bursa)
Lateral hip ache, worse lying on that side at night and on single-leg loading (stairs, standing on one leg)

tell.symptoms: Pain on the bony point on the SIDE of the hip (not the groin); classic sign is being unable to sleep on that side; hurts to stand on one leg or climb stairs; tender to press directly on the bump.
tell.labMarker: No blood test; MRI or ultrasound shows gluteus medius/minimus tendinopathy or partial tear at the greater trochanter

fixes.what: Progressive hip-abductor loading — isometric holds first (e.g. wall isometric abduction), then heavy-slow resistance. In the LEAP RCT, education + exercise beat a corticosteroid injection at 8 weeks (77% vs 58% at least moderately better) and stayed at least as good at 1 year; injections give short-term relief but no lasting advantage
Stop compressing the tendon: don't sleep on the painful side, don't sit cross-legged or hang on one hip when standing, no aggressive IT-band/glute stretching into adduction
Collagen peptides (~15g) with vitamin C ~1h before loading to support tendon matrix synthesis (Shaw/Baar protocol; modest, tier-2 evidence, low-risk adjunct to loading)
Hit adequate daily protein to give the tendon and abductor muscles the substrate to adapt to loading
Creatine alongside the strengthening programme to support abductor strength gains

causes.confusedWith: Hip osteoarthritis (groin pain, not side pain) and lumbar referred pain

causes.name: Hip osteoarthritis

chain.node: Cumulative joint load over years ± prior FAI, dysplasia or injury
Cartilage matrix breakdown: IL-1β/TNF-α drive chondrocyte MMP-13, which cleaves type II collagen and aggrecan, with subchondral remodelling and low-grade synovitis
Femoral head–acetabulum articular cartilage and joint synovium
Groin/front-of-thigh ache, stiffness worst on waking or after sitting (short-lived, eases within minutes of moving), and progressive loss of internal rotation

tell.symptoms: Ache in the GROIN or front of the thigh; stiffness first thing in the morning or after sitting that eases within a few minutes of movement; hard to put on socks or rotate the leg inward; usually age 45+.
tell.labMarker: Weight-bearing hip X-ray: joint-space narrowing and osteophytes (Kellgren–Lawrence grade ≥2). On exam, reduced/painful internal rotation — ACR criteria use IR <15° (with flexion ≤115°); passive IR ≤25° is one item of the Sutlive clinical cluster

fixes.what: Progressive resistance training + low-impact conditioning (cycling, walking, swimming) — exercise reduces OA hip pain and is first-line, ahead of any pill
Reduce bodyweight if carrying excess — lowering load cuts hip joint reaction force; manage total energy intake to a slight deficit
Creatine to preserve/build muscle around the joint during strengthening, supporting the hip under load
Collagen peptides — modest, tier-1/2 evidence for reducing joint pain; low-risk adjunct to loading
Keep daily protein high to protect muscle mass as training load increases

causes.confusedWith: Femoroacetabular impingement/labral tear (younger, sharper deep-flexion pain), lumbar referred pain, and inflammatory arthritis (morning stiffness that improves with activity, not degenerative)

causes.name: Hip-flexor dominance & glute weakness from sitting

chain.node: Prolonged daily sitting → chronically shortened iliopsoas and under-used, deconditioned glutes

Reduced hip-extension capacity and weak/late-recruiting abductors; the 'reciprocal gluteal inhibition / dead butt' label is a biomechanical model — in practice this is more weakness than true neural shut-off (tier 2, association not proven mechanism)
Hip abductors/extensors and the anterior hip joint/iliopsoas
Front-of-hip pinch on standing up, side-hip ache under load, and the knee caving inward when squatting

tell.symptoms: You sit most of the day; pinch or tightness at the front of the hip when you stand after sitting; knee drifts inward on squats/single-leg work; hip feels 'tight' but stretching gives only brief relief. This overlaps the site's glute-weakness/hip-instability framing.
tell.labMarker: No lab; clinical Trendelenburg test (pelvis drops on single-leg stance) and observed knee valgus on squat

fixes.what: Break up sitting — stand and drive the hip into full extension every 30–60 min; this directly targets the sitting-driven loss of hip extension
Glute activation + strengthening: bridges, hip thrusts, banded lateral walks, single-leg work — load the abductors/extensors so they share the load (strengthening, not just 'activation', is the fix)
Gentle hip-flexor mobility (couch stretch, half-kneeling) to reduce the anterior pull — mobility supports, but strengthening is the fix
Creatine + adequate protein to accelerate the glute strength adaptation
Sufficient daily protein to build the posterior-chain muscle you're trying to recruit

causes.confusedWith: Gluteal tendinopathy (which is a tendon problem, not just weakness) — they often coexist

causes.name: Femoroacetabular impingement (FAI) / labral tear

chain.node: Cam or pincer bony morphology + repetitive deep hip flexion (deep squats, cyclists, kicking/pivoting athletes)
Bony abutment of the femoral neck against the acetabular rim shears the labrum (the morphology is mechanical; whether it always causes pain is tier 2 — many with cam morphology are asymptomatic)
Acetabular labrum and anterosuperior joint cartilage
Deep groin pain (patient cups the hip in a 'C'), catching/clicking, worse in deep squat or prolonged sitting

tell.symptoms: Deep pain IN the groin (not the side), often in a younger active person; catching, clicking or pinching at the bottom of a squat or after long sitting; you instinctively grip the hip in a C-shape. Provoked by flexion-adduction-internal-rotation (FADIR).
tell.labMarker: X-ray alpha angle >55° suggests cam morphology (≥60° is the more specific current threshold), or acetabular over-coverage (pincer); MR arthrogram confirms the labral tear; positive FADIR impingement test

fixes.what: Modify provocative movements: cap squat depth, avoid end-range flexion/adduction/internal rotation and long deep-sitting, adjust bike fit — deload the impingement
Hip and core stabilisation + abductor/rotator strengthening under a physiotherapist — first-line for symptom control
Collagen peptides + adequate protein as a low-risk connective-tissue support adjunct
Orthopaedic referral for imaging and, if symptoms persist despite rehab, arthroscopic labral repair/osteoplasty

causes.confusedWith: Hip osteoarthritis (older, stiffer) and athletic pubalgia/hip-flexor strain

causes.name: Referred pain from the lumbar spine

chain.node: Lower lumbar facet/disc irritation or upper-lumbar (L2–L4) nerve-root involvement
Somatic and radicular referral projecting pain into the hip and groin region
Lumbar spine referring to the hip/buttock/groin
Hip-area pain that tracks with back position, with a hip that moves and rotates normally

tell.symptoms: Hip/buttock pain that changes when you move your BACK (bending, arching, twisting) rather than your hip; often accompanied by back stiffness; the hip itself has full, painless rotation. This is a mimic worth ruling out before treating the hip.
tell.labMarker: Full painless hip internal rotation on exam points away from the joint; positive slump/straight-leg-raise or reproduction with lumbar movement; lumbar MRI if neurological signs

fixes.what: Assess and treat the spine, not the hip: if hip rotation is full and pain moves with back position, direct rehab to the lumbar spine and core
Medical/physio review for a lumbar source before invasive hip treatment — misattribution is common

causes.confusedWith: True intra-articular hip pathology (OA, FAI) — the discriminator is whether hip internal rotation is painful and limited

causes.name: Inflammatory arthritis (axial spondyloarthritis / sacroiliitis)

chain.node: Genetically primed immune activation (HLA-B27 association), onset typically before age 45, often with a family history or extra-articular signs (psoriasis, IBD, uveitis, heel/enthesis pain)
TNF-α / IL-17–driven inflammation at the sacroiliac joints and entheses, referring pain into the buttock, hip and groin
Sacroiliac joints and hip/pelvic entheses (± the hip joint itself)
Deep buttock/groin ache with prolonged morning stiffness that IMPROVES with activity and worsens with rest, plus night pain in the second half of the night

tell.symptoms: Under ~45; morning stiffness lasting more than 30–60 minutes; pain that gets BETTER with movement and worse with rest (opposite of mechanical); wakes you in the second half of the night; often alternating buttock pain; may have psoriasis, gut symptoms, eye inflammation or heel pain.
tell.labMarker: Raised CRP/ESR (often normal in non-radiographic disease); HLA-B27 frequently positive; MRI of the sacroiliac joints shows bone-marrow oedema/sacroiliitis (plain X-ray can be normal early)

fixes.what: Rheumatology referral — do NOT treat as a mechanical hip; this is a systemic inflammatory disease that can progress if missed
NSAIDs are first-line and often strikingly effective; a good response supports the diagnosis
Structured exercise/physiotherapy to preserve spine and hip mobility — a core part of long-term management
If inadequately controlled, biologic therapy targeting TNF-α or IL-17 under specialist care

causes.confusedWith: Mechanical hip OA and gluteal tendinopathy — the discriminator is stiffness that eases with activity (not rest), night pain, young age and extra-articular features

hip-mobility.theOneThing: Locate your pain first — SIDE of the hip vs GROIN — and screen out the inflammatory pattern (morning stiffness over 30–60 min that eases with movement, in someone under 45). Then, for the two commonest mechanical drivers (lateral gluteal tendinopathy and glute-weakness from sitting), do the same core move: progressively load the glutes and hip abductors (isometrics → heavy-slow strengthening) while cutting compressive positions (crossed legs, side-sleeping) and breaking up long sitting. Graded hip strengthening is the single highest-leverage action, and in trial evidence it out-performs stretching, rest, and injections for most people.

ladder: You feel pain at the elbow/wrist when you grip, lift, or twist
The tendon's collagen matrix is disorganised and failing to remodel — degeneration, not classic inflammation (which is why '-itis' is a misnomer and why anti-inflammatories alone don't fix it)
Load being applied keeps exceeding the tendon's current capacity to repair itself
Root drivers: a spike in repetitive gripping load, strain-concentrating technique/ergonomics, and weak recovery inputs (protein, sleep, blood-sugar control, hormones, certain drugs)
elbow-wrist.intro: Despite the "-itis," most wrist and elbow tendinopathy is not an inflamed tendon you can rest into healing — it's a tendon whose collagen has been micro-damaged faster than its cells can rebuild it, leaving a disorganised, painful, load-intolerant matrix. That imbalance is always the same, but the reason your tendon lost the race differs person to person. For a tradesperson or new climber it's a raw load spike; for the desk worker with a tennis racket it's strain-concentrating technique; for someone over 45, low estrogen or a metabolic problem may mean the tendon simply can't remodel even under normal load; and a subset are chemically fragile from a recent antibiotic. The fix hinges entirely on which of these is yours — so read each one and find the driver that matches your history, not just your pain.

causes.name: Load-capacity mismatch (overuse spike / deconditioned tendon)

chain.node: Sudden rise in repetitive gripping/lifting/twisting volume (new job task, racket sport, climbing, kettlebells)
Load exceeds tenocyte repair rate; matrix turnover shifts catabolic (degradation > synthesis), with collagenase and IL-1β upregulated at fatigue-damaged sites
Collagen fibrils disorganise at the extensor/flexor enthesis of the epicondyle or wrist tendons
Pain and weakness on gripping, lifting, or wringing

tell.symptoms: Pain started weeks after a clear increase or change in hand-loading activity; worst with gripping and the morning after; a tender pinpoint spot right on the bony bump of the elbow (lateral = tennis, medial = golfer's) or over the wrist tendon; eases with warm-up then returns.
tell.labMarker: None — diagnosis is clinical/history. Imaging (ultrasound) optional: shows hypoechoic thickened tendon, not fluid/inflammation.

fixes.what: Progressive tendon loading — isometric holds early for pain relief, then heavy-slow-resistance / eccentric wrist and grip work 3×/week. Loading, not rest, drives remodelling.
Deload the aggravating spike for 2–4 weeks (reduce volume ~50%, don't fully stop) so repair can catch up to load
Take 15g collagen peptides + 50–100mg vitamin C ~45–60 min before loading sessions — window of raised collagen-building substrate at the tendon
Hit ≥1.6 g/kg/day total protein to supply the amino acids for matrix synthesis

causes.confusedWith: Cervical radiculopathy or a nerve entrapment (radial/cubital tunnel) — refer these out if pain has tingling, numbness, or travels up/down the arm rather than sitting on the tendon.

causes.name: Strain-concentrating technique & ergonomics

chain.node: Poor mechanics or setup concentrates force on one tendon (tight/small racket or tool grip, wrist-extended mouse/keyboard, bar death-gripping, backhand from the wrist)
Same tissue takes disproportionate, repetitive peak load every rep
Focal collagen fatigue and microtearing at that entheseal hotspot
Localised pain that tracks with the specific movement/tool

tell.symptoms: Pain maps to one specific activity or tool and not others; using a different grip, a lighter/thicker handle, or a neutral wrist noticeably reduces it; often desk workers, racket-sport players, or anyone who grips hard all day.

fixes.what: Fix the interface: thicker/larger grip on tools and rackets, neutral-wrist keyboard/mouse setup, coaching on backhand/lifting mechanics to load the whole kinetic chain not just the wrist
Consciously reduce grip force to the minimum the task needs; use a forearm counterforce brace during high-load tasks as a bridge
Add forearm/grip strengthening so technique holds up under fatigue late in a session

causes.confusedWith: Simple acute overload — if changing technique/tools does nothing, the problem is total load, not distribution.

causes.name: Inadequate recovery substrate (protein / vitamin C / sleep deficit)

chain.node: Low dietary protein, low vitamin C, and/or chronic short sleep
Insufficient amino-acid and cofactor supply for collagen synthesis; blunted overnight anabolic (GH/IGF-1-driven) connective-tissue repair
Tendon remodelling can't keep pace with normal daily loading
Nagging, slow-to-settle tendon pain despite modest activity

tell.symptoms: Tendon niggles that linger far longer than expected and recur easily; low overall protein intake, minimal fruit/veg, sleeping under ~6.5h; often multiple tendons/joints grumble at once.
tell.labMarker: Optional: serum vitamin C low if diet is poor. No tendon-specific lab exists — this is diagnosed from diet/sleep history.

fixes.what: Raise protein to 1.6–2.0 g/kg/day, spread across meals
Ensure adequate vitamin C (co-factor for collagen prolyl/lysyl hydroxylation) — diet or ~100–500mg/day supplement
Add glycine (from collagen/gelatin or ~10–15g) — glycine is roughly one-third of tendon collagen's amino-acid backbone
Protect 7–9h sleep — the main window for growth-hormone-driven connective-tissue repair

causes.name: Metabolic glycation (insulin resistance / type 2 diabetes / high sugar)

chain.node: Chronically elevated blood glucose / insulin resistance / high added-sugar intake
Advanced glycation end-products cross-link collagen and impair tenocyte & tendon-stem/progenitor-cell function (leading, though not sole, mechanism)
Tendon becomes stiff, less able to slide/deform, and slow to heal
Recurrent, treatment-resistant tendinopathy with little provocation

tell.symptoms: Tendinopathy that flares with minimal load, heals poorly, and keeps coming back — often alongside central weight gain, or a diabetes/pre-diabetes diagnosis. Frequently affects several tendons.
tell.labMarker: HbA1c (pre-diabetes 5.7–6.4%, diabetes ≥6.5%); fasting glucose elevated. Supports the metabolic driver — the diabetes–tendinopathy link is well-established epidemiologically.

fixes.what: Cut added sugar and refined carbs to lower glucose exposure and AGE formation
Improve insulin sensitivity via resistance training and post-meal walking
If diabetic/pre-diabetic, get glycaemic control managed medically — tendon healing tracks with it
Support matrix repair with collagen peptides + vitamin C timed to loading (as above)

causes.confusedWith: Simple overuse — suspect a metabolic driver when there's no load spike to explain a stubborn, recurrent tendon.

causes.name: Drug- or hormone-driven collagen fragility (fluoroquinolones, low estrogen)

chain.node: Recent fluoroquinolone antibiotic (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin) OR estrogen decline (peri/menopause)
Upregulated collagen-degrading MMPs and oxidative/mitochondrial stress in tenocytes (fluoroquinolone); reduced collagen synthesis rate (low estrogen)
Weakened, poorly-remodelling collagen matrix
Tendon pain — or even rupture — out of proportion to the load applied

tell.symptoms: Fluoroquinolone: tendon pain/swelling appearing within days-to-weeks of a cipro/levo course, sometimes multiple tendons, can precede rupture — stop the drug and avoid loading to failure. Estrogen: new multi-tendon aches and stiffness clustering around perimenopause/menopause.
