tell.labMarker: No tendon-specific lab — established from medication history and menopausal status.

fixes.what: Fluoroquinolone-linked: stop the antibiotic in consultation with prescriber; avoid heavy tendon loading and corticosteroids during and shortly after the course (steroids compound rupture risk)
Menopause-linked: discuss HRT with a clinician — estrogen replacement raises tendon collagen synthesis rate and may aid recovery
Support the matrix with protein, collagen + vitamin C, and progressive loading once safe
Consider peptide therapies (BPC-157, TB-500) only as experimental adjuncts — promising in animal tendon-healing models but not established or approved in humans

causes.confusedWith: Overuse tendinopathy — the tell is that the load history is too trivial to explain the injury, pointing to a systemic cause.

elbow-wrist.theOneThing: Progressively load the tendon, don't rest it. Start with pain-tolerable isometric grip/wrist holds, then build to heavy-slow-resistance and eccentric forearm work 3×/week, while deloading (not stopping) the activity that spiked it. Tendons remodel in response to graded mechanical load — rest and anti-inflammatories alone leave the matrix disorganised and the pain recurring.

ankle-foot.intro: Your foot and ankle carry your whole body weight through a small stack of bones, tendons, ligaments and nerves thousands of times a day, so \"pain here\" is never one thing — it's whichever link in that chain is being overloaded, worn down, crystallised on, or losing its nerve supply. Most cases are mechanical: a tissue (plantar fascia, Achilles, a ligament) is being loaded faster than it can rebuild, or a joint's cartilage is wearing out, and the exact tissue depends on your foot shape, footwear, and training history. But a meaningful minority are metabolic — urate crystals dumping into a cold big-toe joint, or years of high blood sugar quietly killing the smallest nerve fibres. The trap is treating every foot pain as \"inflammation to rest away.\" Rest helps a fresh sprain and does nothing for a degenerative tendon, an arthritic joint, or a nerve. So the job is to find WHICH driver is yours: where exactly it hurts, when it hurts (first step vs. after running vs. worse-with-use vs. at night), and what it looks like (swollen and red vs. stiff vs. numb) sort these apart fast.
ladder: Foot / ankle pain — a specific structure is failing under the demands you place on it
A single tissue is overloaded, worn down, crystallised on, or losing its nerve/blood supply
Repair or maintenance capacity is being outpaced — by load spikes, weak/stiff calf & foot, cumulative joint wear, or a metabolic driver (urate, high blood sugar)
Root drivers: sudden mileage/volume jumps, poor footwear & foot strength, prior joint injury & age, alcohol/fructose/purine load, uncontrolled blood glucose
ankle-foot.theOneThing: For the mechanical causes that make up most foot/ankle pain (plantar fascia, Achilles, unstable ankle, and even arthritic joints), the highest-leverage move is progressive loading plus load management — NOT rest. Gradually strengthen or move the painful tissue (calf raises, heel drops, single-leg balance, gentle range-of-motion) while capping sudden jumps in training volume and wearing supportive footwear. Degenerative tendons and fascia rebuild in response to controlled load, and arthritic joints do better kept moving; complete rest just lets them weaken and the pain returns the moment you move. (The two metabolic drivers — gout and diabetic neuropathy — are the exception: those need the underlying chemistry fixed, not loading.)

causes.name: Plantar fasciopathy (plantar fasciitis)

chain.node: Load spikes + tight calf / limited ankle dorsiflexion repeatedly overstrain the fascia at the heel
Microtears at the medial calcaneal origin outpace the tissue's repair rate
Collagen degenerates (myxoid 'fasciosis') — histology shows disorganised collagen and largely NO true inflammatory cells despite the '-itis' name
Sharp stab under the heel/arch on the first steps out of bed or after sitting

tell.symptoms: Stabbing pain under the inner heel or arch on the first few steps in the morning or after sitting; eases as you walk, then returns after long standing. A tender pinpoint spot on the inner heel bone.
tell.labMarker: No blood test; ultrasound shows plantar fascia thickening (≥4 mm at the calcaneal origin — ~96% sensitive/100% specific, but thickening also occurs in some symptom-free feet). Clinical diagnosis.

fixes.what: Progressive plantar-fascia + calf loading (e.g. high-load heel raises with the toes extended over a rolled towel on a step), done into tolerable discomfort
Supportive/cushioned footwear and arch support; avoid barefoot on hard floors, especially first thing in the morning
Cap sudden jumps in walking/running volume; build mileage gradually
Collagen peptides (or gelatin) ~30–60 min before loading to supply building blocks; evidence is for raised collagen-synthesis markers, extrapolated to fascia
Vitamin C alongside collagen — it is the required cofactor for prolyl/lysyl hydroxylase, the enzymes that build stable collagen
Hit adequate daily protein (~1.6 g/kg) so repair isn't substrate-limited

causes.confusedWith: Heel fat-pad atrophy or a calcaneal stress fracture (deep, constant heel pain, tender on squeezing the whole heel) — those don't respond to fascia loading.

causes.name: Chronic ankle instability (recurrent sprain)

chain.node: An inversion (rolling) sprain overstretches or tears the lateral ligaments, chiefly the ATFL
Ligament heals lax AND articular/muscle-spindle proprioceptors are damaged
Impaired joint-position sense and altered peroneal neuromuscular control — combined mechanical + functional instability
The ankle repeatedly 'gives way,' with recurrent lateral pain and swelling on uneven ground

tell.symptoms: A history of one or more ankle sprains; the ankle feels unstable or gives way on uneven ground; recurrent swelling on the outer ankle. Single-leg balance is noticeably wobblier on that side.
tell.labMarker: No lab; clinical anterior-drawer / talar-tilt laxity on exam, confirmable on stress imaging or MRI if needed.

fixes.what: Balance/proprioception training (single-leg stance progressing to wobble board / eyes-closed) — rebuilds the neuromuscular control that actually breaks the re-injury cycle
Peroneal and calf strengthening to actively stabilise the joint
Brace or tape during cutting/court sports, and complete a graded return-to-play before full loading

causes.confusedWith: An osteochondral lesion of the talus or a peroneal tendon tear — persistent deep/lateral pain that balance work doesn't resolve; warrants imaging.

causes.name: Achilles tendinopathy

chain.node: Training-load spikes and a tight/weak calf repeatedly overload the heel cord
Repetitive microtrauma outpaces tenocyte repair; collagen disorganises, with neovessel and accompanying nerve ingrowth (implicated in the pain)
Degenerative tendinopathy of the mid-portion (2–6 cm above the heel) or the insertion — degenerative, not classically inflammatory
Stiff, painful heel cord, worst in the morning and when starting to run

tell.symptoms: Morning stiffness and pain in the tendon 2–6 cm above the heel (or at its insertion), a thickened/tender lump, and pain that flares at the start of a run then partly 'warms up.'
tell.labMarker: No blood test; ultrasound/MRI shows tendon thickening and hypoechoic degenerative change (± Doppler neovascularity).

fixes.what: Heavy-slow resistance or eccentric calf loading (heel drops) — comparably effective and the evidence-based driver of tendon remodelling; for insertional pain avoid dropping below level and use a small heel lift
Manage load: reduce sudden volume/hill/speed jumps
Collagen peptides/gelatin + vitamin C ~30–60 min before loading to raise collagen-synthesis markers
Omega-3 to support the tissue's resolution/repair environment
Cut chronically high sugar intake (advanced-glycation cross-links stiffen tendon collagen and impair its repair)

causes.confusedWith: Retrocalcaneal bursitis or a partial/complete Achilles tear (sudden pop, marked weakness pushing off) — the tear is a red flag needing assessment.

causes.name: Osteoarthritis of the foot/ankle joint (incl. first-MTP hallux rigidus)

chain.node: Prior joint injury (old sprain/fracture), repetitive high load, or age-related wear exceeds the cartilage's capacity to maintain itself
Articular cartilage thins and the joint remodels — subchondral sclerosis and marginal osteophytes form
Osteoarthritic joint — classically the first MTP (hallux rigidus), the midfoot, or a post-traumatic ankle
Deep, use-related joint pain and stiffness with reduced range of motion (e.g. stiff, painful big-toe push-off), worse later in the day, better with rest

tell.symptoms: Gradual, deep joint pain that WORSENS with weight-bearing/activity and eases with rest (the opposite of the nocturnal neuropathic pattern); stiffness after rest that loosens with movement; reduced joint range — e.g. can't fully bend the big toe. Often a history of prior injury to that joint.
tell.labMarker: No blood test; weight-bearing X-ray shows joint-space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis and osteophytes.

fixes.what: Load management plus low-impact strengthening and range-of-motion — keep the joint moving while cutting aggravating high-impact loading
Stiff-soled or rocker-bottom shoe / carbon footplate to offload a painful first MTP; cushioned supportive footwear
Lose excess weight — reduces joint load and systemic inflammatory drive
See a doctor for persistent/severe cases (intra-articular options, or surgical review for advanced hallux rigidus or ankle OA)

causes.confusedWith: Gout (also strikes the first MTP but comes on acutely red/hot/exquisitely tender rather than gradually) and inflammatory arthritis such as RA (multiple joints, prolonged morning stiffness).

causes.name: Gout / crystal arthropathy

chain.node: Chronic hyperuricemia — from urate under-excretion (~90% of cases) and/or high purine (organ meat/shellfish), alcohol (esp. beer) and fructose intake
Monosodium urate supersaturates and crystallises in a cool, distal joint — classically the big-toe (first MTP) or ankle
Crystals activate the NLRP3 inflammasome, driving an IL-1β cytokine surge
Sudden red, hot, exquisitely tender joint — often waking you overnight

tell.symptoms: Abrupt (often overnight) onset of a red, hot, swollen, extremely tender joint — classically the base of the big toe or the ankle; may follow alcohol or a rich meal; tends to recur.
tell.labMarker: Serum uric acid often elevated (crystal saturation above ~6.8 mg/dL ≈ 0.41 mmol/L), though it can read normal during an acute attack; joint-fluid negatively-birefringent needle crystals are definitive.

fixes.what: Cut alcohol (especially beer), high-fructose/sugary drinks, and organ meats/shellfish; lose excess weight; hydrate well
Reduce added sugar — fructose metabolism directly raises urate production
See a doctor for acute treatment (colchicine/NSAID/steroid) and, for recurrent attacks, urate-lowering therapy such as allopurinol titrated to target
Omega-3 may modestly dampen the inflammatory response / flare frequency

causes.confusedWith: Septic (infected) joint — also red, hot and swollen but a medical emergency; and pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate crystals). A hot swollen joint with fever needs urgent assessment.

causes.name: Peripheral (metabolic/diabetic) neuropathy

chain.node: Chronic hyperglycemia / metabolic syndrome — high glycemic load, insulin resistance
Oxidative stress plus microvascular hypoxia damage the small unmyelinated C and thinly-myelinated Aδ sensory fibres
Length-dependent distal small-fibre nerve degeneration (feet first, symmetric)
Burning, tingling, numb or stabbing pain in both feet, worse at night and unrelated to activity

tell.symptoms: Symmetric, both-feet burning/tingling/numbness in a 'stocking' pattern, worse at night, NOT triggered by a specific movement or step. Reduced sensation to touch or temperature.
tell.labMarker: Fasting glucose / HbA1c elevated (HbA1c ≥6.5% = diabetes; 5.7–6.4% = prediabetes); loss of 10 g monofilament sensation on foot exam.

fixes.what: Tighten glycemic control — cut refined sugar and high-glycemic carbs, lose weight, use regular exercise to restore insulin sensitivity (this slows or halts progression; established small-fibre loss is largely irreversible, so acting early matters most)
Correct contributing deficiencies (notably B12) and moderate alcohol, which independently damages nerves
Work with a doctor on glucose management if diabetic/prediabetic; alpha-lipoic acid is an option but its clinical-trial evidence is modest and mixed
Omega-3 shows emerging (not yet definitive) benefit for nerve-fibre health in diabetic neuropathy

causes.confusedWith: B12-deficiency neuropathy, alcohol-related neuropathy, or tarsal tunnel syndrome (usually one foot, tingling along the sole from nerve compression at the inner ankle).

ladder: Muscle mass and strength quietly shrink year after year
Breakdown starts to outpace synthesis — muscle stops 'hearing' the build signal
That signal fails from four directions: too little load, too little protein, aging nerves, and inflammation/hormone decline
Root levers: heavy resistance loading + enough per-meal protein (plus lowering inflammation and correcting vitamin D deficiency)
sarcopenia.intro: Sarcopenia isn't one disease — it's several forces pulling muscle down at the same time, and which one dominates differs from person to person. In one person it's a chair-bound day with zero heavy loading; in another it's a toast-and-coffee breakfast that never crosses the per-meal protein threshold aging muscle now demands; in a third it's silent nerve loss stripping the fastest, most powerful fibers, or a smoldering inflammation from belly fat and chronic illness that eats muscle faster than food can rebuild it, or a vitamin D and testosterone/IGF-1 decline that quiets the build signal inside the cell. Most people over 60 have two or three of these running together. The job isn't to crown the 'real' cause — it's to find which ones are yours and pull each lever, because the fixes stack on top of each other.

causes.name: Physical inactivity & disuse (mechanical unloading)

chain.node: Sedentary days, no resistance loading, or a spell of bed rest/illness
Loss of load-driven mechanotransduction that normally switches on protein synthesis
FOXO-driven ubiquitin-proteasome proteolysis rises as synthesis falls
Type II (fast-twitch) fibers atrophy preferentially in aging muscle
Strength and muscle size drop, fatigue on stairs

tell.symptoms: You sit most of the day and lift nothing heavy; strength drops faster than size, muscle feels soft, and you notice a clear step-down after any illness, injury, or hospital stay.
tell.labMarker: Grip-strength dynamometer below ~27 kg (men) / ~16 kg (women), or gait speed <0.8 m/s (EWGSOP2 cutoffs); DEXA appendicular lean mass low.

fixes.what: Progressive resistance training 2-3x/week, all major muscle groups, taken close to failure
Break up sitting: daily walking plus loaded carries (rucking, groceries, farmer's walks)
Creatine monohydrate 3-5 g/day to amplify training strength and lean-mass gains
After any illness or surgery, get moving within days — don't let short disuse stack up

causes.confusedWith: Sarcopenic obesity — muscle loss hidden under normal or high body weight, so the scale looks fine while function declines.

causes.name: Anabolic resistance & inadequate protein/energy

chain.node: Low per-meal protein, low leucine, appetite decline ('anorexia of aging')
Increased splanchnic amino-acid extraction — less protein reaches muscle
Blunted mTORC1/p70S6K response to the leucine that does arrive
Post-meal muscle protein synthesis fails to exceed breakdown
Net muscle loss meal after meal, year after year

tell.symptoms: Your protein is skimpy and back-loaded — light breakfast, carb-heavy lunch, most protein at dinner. You fill up fast and often skip meals. Aging muscle needs a bigger per-meal dose to respond, and you're under it.
tell.labMarker: 24-h diet recall showing <1.0 g/kg/day protein (target 1.2-1.6); look for <25-30 g protein at breakfast and lunch.

fixes.what: Eat 1.2-1.6 g/kg/day protein, split into 25-40 g doses across 3-4 meals (not all at dinner)
Anchor each meal with ~2.5-3 g leucine (aging muscle may need up to 3-4 g) — whey, eggs, dairy, meat, or fish
Whey or casein protein to hit the per-meal leucine threshold when whole food falls short
EAAs or HMB when appetite or total intake is low, to protect against breakdown
Eat enough total calories — chronic undereating forces the body to catabolize muscle for fuel

causes.confusedWith: Malabsorption or undereating from GI disease, dental problems, or depression — the protein is 'eaten' but not absorbed or is simply too little overall.

causes.name: Neuromuscular junction degeneration & motor-unit loss

chain.node: Age-related death of alpha motor neurons (accelerates after ~60)
Neuromuscular junction remodeling, fragmentation, and denervation
Incomplete reinnervation — orphaned fast fibers are lost or converted to slow
Motor-unit number falls, muscle shifts to a slow, weaker phenotype
Loss of power and speed, poor balance, falls

tell.symptoms: You lose power and speed out of proportion to size — hard to move fast, catch a stumble, or climb stairs quickly. Balance is worse, and you may notice cramps, twitches, or wobbly legs before size visibly drops.
tell.labMarker: No routine blood test; specialist EMG shows enlarged, fewer motor units (reduced motor-unit number estimation). Slow chair-rise and gait speed are practical proxies.

fixes.what: Heavy and explosive (power) resistance training to preserve type II fibers and drive reinnervation of surviving motor units
Adequate protein so the motor units you keep can rebuild maximally
Creatine monohydrate to support power output and high-velocity work
Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) to support neuromuscular function and blunt anabolic resistance

causes.confusedWith: Peripheral neuropathy or motor-neuron disease (ALS) — rapid, asymmetric, or progressive weakness with wasting needs a neurology workup, not just training.

causes.name: Chronic low-grade inflammation ('inflammaging')

chain.node: Visceral fat, gut permeability, or a chronic disease (CKD, COPD, RA, heart failure, diabetes)
Persistently elevated TNF-alpha and IL-6
NF-kB and FOXO3a drive the ubiquitin-proteasome atrophy program (MuRF1, atrogin-1) plus anabolic resistance
Myofibrillar protein breakdown accelerates faster than intake can replace
Muscle wastes despite seemingly adequate food and effort

tell.symptoms: Wasting alongside belly fat ('sarcopenic obesity'), persistent fatigue, and usually a known chronic condition. Muscle keeps slipping even when you eat well and train, because inflammation is overriding the build signal.
tell.labMarker: hs-CRP elevated (>3 mg/L), IL-6 elevated; often low albumin/prealbumin in advanced cases.

fixes.what: Resistance exercise — muscle contraction releases anti-inflammatory myokines that lower systemic cytokine tone
Reduce visceral fat with a whole-food, fiber-rich, lower-processed diet
Omega-3 EPA/DHA to lower cytokine signaling and improve the protein-synthesis response
Prioritize sleep — short/poor sleep raises IL-6 and cortisol, both catabolic
Treat the underlying chronic disease driving the inflammation with your physician

causes.confusedWith: True cachexia from cancer, advanced heart failure, or COPD — active involuntary weight loss with rapid wasting, a distinct and more aggressive syndrome than age-related sarcopenia.

causes.name: Low vitamin D & anabolic hormone decline

chain.node: Little sun exposure/intake, aging gonads, indoor lifestyle
Low 25(OH)D plus falling testosterone, GH, and IGF-1
Weakened VDR and androgen anabolic signaling inside the muscle cell
Lower protein synthesis and fewer activated satellite cells
Weakness, especially proximal legs (trouble rising from a chair)

tell.symptoms: Proximal leg weakness — hard to stand from a low chair or climb stairs — plus bone/muscle aches, low energy, and (in men) reduced libido. Often an indoor, low-sun lifestyle or older age.
tell.labMarker: Serum 25(OH)D <30 ng/mL (deficient <20); in symptomatic men, low morning total testosterone for age.

fixes.what: Vitamin D3 to restore 25(OH)D to ~30-50 ng/mL, then retest — the benefit is mainly in correcting true deficiency, not in supplementing the already-replete
Resistance training — the strongest natural amplifier of anabolic signaling regardless of hormone level
Adequate protein plus calcium and magnesium cofactors
If clinically low testosterone with symptoms, evaluate TRT with a physician — don't self-treat

causes.confusedWith: Hypothyroidism or statin-associated myopathy — both cause proximal weakness and aches and are reversible once identified, so check TSH and review medications.

sarcopenia.theOneThing: Progressive resistance training 2-3x/week paired with 1.2-1.6 g/kg/day protein spread as 25-40 g per meal. Heavy loading plus enough per-meal leucine is the only intervention that simultaneously reverses disuse, overcomes anabolic resistance, and drives reinnervation of aging motor units — no pill or powder substitutes for it, they only amplify it.

ladder: The belly you can't shift is largely VISCERAL fat — packed around your organs and metabolically active, not just skin-deep padding
It expands when storage signals — insulin, cortisol, and shifting sex hormones — tell that specific depot to hoard fat and resist release
Those signals are set by how MUCH and WHAT you eat, how you sleep, your stress load, and your hormonal life stage
Root levers: protein + fiber to satiety, cut refined carbs and liquid sugar, lift weights, sleep 7-9h, lower chronic stress
visceral-fat.intro: The stubborn fat around your middle is mostly visceral fat — the depot wrapped around your liver and gut — and it behaves differently from the soft, pinchable fat elsewhere: it is more sensitive to hormonal signals and is often the first fat to grow when those signals go wrong. Here is the catch: it rarely has ONE cause. For one person it's a slow calorie surplus and too many refined carbs feeding fat straight into the liver; for another it's cortisol from chronic stress and short sleep parking fat preferentially at the belly; for a man in his 40s or a woman through menopause it's a sex-hormone shift that redistributes fat inward even with no change in diet. Most people have two or three of these running at once, in different proportions. The whole game is figuring out which are YOURS — because the fix for a hormone-driven belly is not the fix for a refined-carb belly, and chasing the wrong one is why generic advice stalls.
visceral-fat.theOneThing: For most people the single highest-leverage move is to eat protein and fiber to satiety while cutting refined carbs and liquid sugar, paired with 2-3 resistance sessions a week. Visceral fat is metabolically active and insulin-responsive, so it tends to mobilize early on this combination — often shrinking your waist before the scale moves much.

causes.name: Chronic energy surplus

chain.node: Sustained calorie intake exceeds expenditure
Elevated insulin drives fat storage and suppresses fat release (lipolysis)
Lipoprotein lipase loads triglycerides into adipocytes
Visceral and subcutaneous fat cells enlarge (hypertrophy)
Waistline creeps up alongside overall weight

tell.symptoms: Gain is spread across the whole body, not just the belly; waist rises in step with total weight; you can point to the cause (bigger portions, snacking, alcohol, sugary or liquid calories, eating out). Belly is often soft and pinchable rather than hard.
tell.labMarker: Waist-to-height ratio > 0.5 is the simplest flag; scale weight trending up over months confirms surplus.

fixes.what: Create a modest sustained deficit (~300-500 kcal/day); cut liquid calories (alcohol, sweet drinks) first
Anchor each meal with protein to satiety (~1.6-2.2 g/kg/day) to blunt appetite
Push dietary fiber toward 25-35 g/day to increase fullness and slow absorption
Resistance train 2-3x/week to preserve muscle so the deficit burns fat, not lean mass
GLP-1 agonist for significant excess weight — strongly reduces appetite and produces meaningful visceral fat loss (not a selective belly-fat effect)
Dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist — larger appetite and weight effect than GLP-1 alone, with marked visceral and liver fat reduction
Caffeine or green tea extract as a modest thermogenic/appetite adjunct, not a primary lever

causes.confusedWith: Bloating and gut distension, which come and go with meals and are not fat gain

causes.name: Refined-carb / hyperinsulinemia-driven visceral & liver fat

chain.node: High intake of refined carbohydrate, sugar and fructose
Fructose and hyperinsulinemia activate hepatic lipogenesis programs (SREBP-1c and ChREBP)
Liver makes new fat (de novo lipogenesis) and stores it locally; excess is exported as VLDL and deposited in the visceral depot
Adipose insulin resistance raises free-fatty-acid flux back to the liver, self-reinforcing
Central belly fat and fatty liver, often with thinner limbs

tell.symptoms: Fat concentrated at the middle with relatively lean arms/legs; energy crashes and hunger 1-2h after high-carb meals; skin tags or darkened neck/armpit skin (acanthosis nigricans); family history of type 2 diabetes. Belly often feels firm, not pinchable.
tell.labMarker: Elevated fasting insulin and HOMA-IR (roughly > 2); high triglyceride-to-HDL ratio; raised ALT / fatty liver on ultrasound; fasting glucose and HbA1c drifting up.

fixes.what: Cut refined carbs, added sugar and sweetened/fructose drinks — the direct substrate for liver fat synthesis
Replace with protein and high-fiber whole foods to flatten the insulin response
Add zone-2 cardio plus resistance training — muscle contraction clears glucose without insulin
Berberine to improve insulin sensitivity and lower fasting glucose (AMPK activation; ~1500 mg/day in trials)
Green tea extract as a modest adjunct for insulin sensitivity and fat oxidation

causes.confusedWith: Soft subcutaneous belly fat (pinchable, calorie-surplus type) versus firm visceral/insulin-resistant belly — press on it to tell them apart

causes.name: Sex-hormone shift (andropause in men / menopause in women)

chain.node: Age-related testosterone decline (men) or estradiol fall at menopause (women)
Loss of sex-hormone restraint on adipose LPL and regional fat distribution
Fat redistributes from gluteofemoral/subcutaneous toward the visceral depot
In men, visceral fat's aromatase converts testosterone to estradiol; the excess estradiol suppresses pituitary LH, lowering testosterone further — a self-reinforcing loop
New central belly appears despite little change in diet

tell.symptoms: Belly showed up in your 40s-50s or across the menopause transition; fat visibly shifted from hips/thighs to waist. Men: low libido, fatigue, loss of morning erections, dropping strength. Women: hot flashes, cycle changes, sleep disruption.
tell.labMarker: Men: low total and free testosterone (below reference range), often with low SHBG. Women: low estradiol with rising FSH (postmenopausal FSH climbs well above premenopausal levels).

fixes.what: Resistance training — builds muscle, improves body composition, and directly counters visceral redistribution
Lose visceral fat by any means to break the aromatase loop in men (fat lowers testosterone, which grows more fat)
Prioritize 7-9h sleep — testosterone secretion is sleep-dependent and short sleep measurably lowers it
Menopausal hormone therapy reduces total and visceral adiposity in appropriate women — discuss with a clinician
Testosterone replacement for genuinely hypogonadal men (confirmed low labs + symptoms) under medical supervision

causes.confusedWith: Hypothyroidism, which also causes midlife weight gain and fatigue — check TSH before assuming it's sex hormones

causes.name: Cortisol / chronic-stress-driven storage

chain.node: Chronic psychological stress and HPA-axis activation
Visceral fat cells carry ~4x the glucocorticoid-receptor density and express 11β-HSD1, which regenerates active cortisol from cortisone inside the cell, amplifying local glucocorticoid signaling
Glucocorticoid receptor activation switches on LPL, adipogenesis and glucose uptake preferentially in visceral fat
Fat parks preferentially at the belly, amplified by stress-driven eating
Central weight gain 'even when I eat okay'

tell.symptoms: Gain concentrated at the belly despite a reasonable diet; high sustained stress; wired-but-tired feeling; cravings for sweet/salty comfort food under pressure; poor sleep. Belly tends to be firm.
tell.labMarker: No reliable routine blood test — random serum cortisol is unhelpful. If frank Cushing's is suspected (purple stretch marks, moon face, proximal muscle weakness), a clinician can order late-night salivary cortisol or a dexamethasone suppression test.

fixes.what: Attack the stressor and add daily downregulation (breathwork, walks, boundaries) — the only real fix for the driver
Protect sleep, which itself lowers evening cortisol
Ashwagandha lowers serum cortisol in stressed adults (its effect on self-rated perceived stress is less consistent across trials)
Rhodiola rosea for stress/fatigue — evidence is contradictory and trials are low-quality, so treat as optional
Magnesium to support the stress response and sleep quality, especially if intake is low
Front-load protein and fiber to blunt stress-eating swings

causes.confusedWith: Cushing's syndrome — rare but must be ruled out if there are purple abdominal striae, a moon face, easy bruising and proximal weakness

causes.name: Short sleep / circadian disruption

chain.node: Chronic short sleep (<6-7h), shift work, or late-night screens
Raised hunger and altered appetite hormones (higher ghrelin, lower leptin) plus higher evening cortisol
Resulting energy surplus is channeled preferentially into the visceral depot
A controlled sleep-restriction trial showed visceral fat rising ~11% while normal sleep held it flat

tell.symptoms: You routinely sleep under 6-7 hours; belly and weight gain track your worst-sleep stretches; strong next-day hunger and carb/fat cravings; late-night eating. Often overlaps with the cortisol driver.
tell.labMarker: No specific blood marker; a sleep tracker or a simple log showing chronic short/irregular sleep is the tell.

fixes.what: Extend sleep opportunity to 7-9h with a consistent wake time — the direct fix, shown to prevent the visceral gain
Cut late-evening screens, caffeine after early afternoon, and late heavy meals
Magnesium to support sleep onset and quality, especially if intake is low
Keep protein and fiber high the day after poor sleep to counter the hunger spike

causes.confusedWith: Undiagnosed obstructive sleep apnea, which both fragments sleep and independently worsens visceral fat and insulin resistance — screen if snoring, gasping, or daytime sleepiness are present

ladder: You feel drained all day, no matter how much you rest
Your cells and brain aren't getting enough usable energy or oxygen delivered — or your drive to spend energy is switched down
One specific bottleneck is throttling it: unrefreshing sleep, low iron stores, low mood or chronic stress, blood-sugar swings, a sluggish thyroid, or weak mitochondria
Which one is driving YOURS traces back to daily inputs: sleep timing, stress and mood load, diet quality, nutrient stores, and movement
chronic-fatigue.intro: Fatigue is a final common exit for a dozen different upstream problems, which is exactly why it's so hard to fix: two people with identical exhaustion can have completely different machines breaking down. Most cases in practice trace to a short list — sleep, mood/stress, iron, thyroid, or blood sugar — and the job is to find which one is yours rather than treating the symptom blind.

causes.name: Sleep debt & circadian disruption

chain.node: Short, irregular, or fragmented sleep; late-night light exposure
Incomplete overnight adenosine clearance and a shifted melatonin rhythm
Reduced slow-wave and REM restoration in the brain
Daytime sleepiness, low drive, brain fog, caffeine dependence

tell.symptoms: Energy improves markedly on weekends or holidays when you sleep in; you rely on caffeine to function; worst in the early afternoon 'dip'; you rarely wake feeling refreshed. Total sleep is under ~7h or is broken up.
tell.labMarker: No routine blood test; a wearable/sleep tracker showing <7h or low deep sleep is the signal. If you snore loudly and wake unrefreshed despite adequate hours, order a home sleep apnea test.

fixes.what: Anchor a fixed wake time and protect a 7-9h sleep window every day, including weekends
Get 10+ min of bright outdoor light within an hour of waking to set the circadian clock
Cut caffeine after early afternoon (~2pm) — its half-life is ~5-6h, so it still blocks adenosine into the evening
Magnesium glycinate in the evening may support sleep onset and depth (modest evidence)

causes.confusedWith: Obstructive sleep apnea — loud snoring and unrefreshing sleep despite 8h in bed; needs a sleep study, not just better sleep hygiene.

causes.name: Iron deficiency (with or without anemia)

chain.node: Heavy menstrual loss, low dietary iron, or poor gut absorption
Depleted ferritin stores starve hemoglobin and iron-dependent (Fe-S cluster) mitochondrial enzymes
Less oxygen delivered to tissue plus impaired electron transport in muscle
Fatigue, breathlessness on exertion, pallor, restless legs

tell.symptoms: Breathless or heart-pounding climbing stairs, pale skin and inner eyelids, cold hands, hair shedding, restless legs at night, ice cravings. Common if you have heavy periods or eat little red meat.
tell.labMarker: Ferritin: <30 ng/mL is diagnostic of iron deficiency; RCTs show repleting to >50 ng/mL reduces fatigue even without anemia, so <50 is worth treating in symptomatic people. Confirm alongside a CBC (hemoglobin) and transferrin saturation. Note: ferritin can be falsely raised by inflammation.

fixes.what: Eat iron-rich foods: red meat, liver, shellfish, lentils and beans, dark leafy greens
Supplement iron (every-other-day dosing absorbs as well and is gentler on the gut)
Take vitamin C with iron-containing meals to boost non-heme absorption
Avoid tea, coffee, and calcium supplements within an hour of iron — they block uptake

causes.confusedWith: Anemia of inflammation (chronic illness), which lowers hemoglobin but keeps or raises ferritin — iron supplements won't help and may harm.

causes.name: Depression, chronic stress & burnout

chain.node: Sustained stress, loss, or burnout; persistent low mood, rumination, or anhedonia
HPA-axis dysregulation and low-grade inflammatory signaling (mechanism partly associative)
Down-regulated drive in reward/motivation circuits and disrupted sleep architecture
Fatigue that rest doesn't fix, loss of interest, poor concentration, often heaviest in the morning

tell.symptoms: Fatigue paired with low mood, loss of interest or pleasure, or unusual irritability; nothing feels worth the effort; often worse in the morning; concentration and memory feel foggy. Sleep and rest don't restore energy the way they should.
tell.labMarker: No blood test confirms it — labs are used to rule out mimics (thyroid, iron, B12). Validated screens like the PHQ-9 quantify severity and track response.

fixes.what: Behavioral activation and graded exercise — the best-evidenced non-drug lever for fatigue-with-low-mood
Seek evaluation for depression/anxiety; structured psychotherapy (e.g. CBT) when indicated
An SSRI/SNRI when depression is moderate-plus and clearly indicated (physician-guided)
High-EPA omega-3 as an adjunct — modest antidepressant signal, not a standalone fix

causes.confusedWith: Hypothyroidism and iron deficiency, which share the fatigue-plus-low-mood picture and must be excluded by labs; and genuine ME/CFS, defined by post-exertional malaise rather than low mood.

causes.name: Blood-sugar swings & insulin resistance

chain.node: Refined-carb meals low in fiber and protein
Glucose spike triggers an exaggerated, overshooting insulin surge
A rapid glucose decline (true reactive hypoglycemia is uncommon) plus the post-spike insulin swing dips brain alertness
Post-meal crash: sleepy, foggy, sometimes shaky, hungry again within hours

tell.symptoms: Energy nosedives 1-3 hours after carb-heavy or sugary meals; a classic afternoon slump after a starchy lunch; feel foggy or ravenous between meals; steadier on higher-protein, lower-carb days.
tell.labMarker: Fasting glucose and HbA1c drifting toward the prediabetic range (HbA1c 5.7%+); elevated fasting insulin or HOMA-IR. A CGM showing sharp spikes followed by rapid drops is the clearest signal — but note documented sub-70 mg/dL hypoglycemia is actually rare.

fixes.what: Eat protein and fiber before or instead of refined carbs; cut sugary drinks and snacks
Take a 10-15 min walk after meals to blunt the glucose spike
Alpha-lipoic acid may support insulin sensitivity and glucose handling (modest evidence)
Magnesium — low levels track with insulin resistance

causes.confusedWith: Simple postprandial somnolence — a normal, mild 'food coma' from any large meal that doesn't come with shakiness or repeated between-meal crashes.

causes.name: Hypothyroidism (overt; subclinical is contested)

chain.node: Autoimmune thyroid damage (Hashimoto's), or iodine/selenium inadequacy
Falling thyroid hormone drives TSH up; less active T3 reaches tissues
Slowed cellular metabolic rate and reduced Na/K-ATPase activity everywhere
Fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, sluggish mood and bowels

tell.symptoms: Cold when others aren't, dry skin, constipation, unexplained weight creep, thinning of the outer third of the eyebrows, puffiness, low mood, slow heart rate.
tell.labMarker: TSH elevated: overt hypothyroidism means TSH high (often >10 mIU/L) WITH low free T4 — this is the form that reliably causes fatigue and responds to treatment. Subclinical (TSH ~4.5-10, normal free T4) is contested: RCTs show levothyroxine usually does NOT relieve fatigue, especially over 65. Add TPO antibodies to check for Hashimoto's.

fixes.what: Get a full thyroid panel (TSH, free T4, TPO antibodies) — this is a diagnosis you can't fix blind
Levothyroxine replacement for overt hypothyroidism; for subclinical, don't expect fatigue relief — trial only if clearly symptomatic (physician-guided)
Ensure adequate selenium and iodine — Brazil nuts, seafood, eggs, dairy

causes.confusedWith: Depression and anemia, which share the fatigue-plus-low-mood picture; a TSH test separates them.

causes.name: Mitochondrial inefficiency & cofactor depletion

chain.node: Aging, statin use, oxidative stress, or depleted CoQ10 / B-vitamin cofactors
Impaired electron transport chain lowers ATP output per unit of fuel
Reduced ATP supply in high-demand muscle and brain tissue
Still drained despite good sleep and normal iron/thyroid; poor stamina and slow recovery

tell.symptoms: You've ruled out the obvious — sleep is good, mood is fine, iron/thyroid/blood sugar all normal — yet you're still low on energy, with exercise intolerance and slow recovery after exertion. Sometimes worse on a statin.
tell.labMarker: Standard labs are typically normal — this is largely a diagnosis of exclusion, and 'mitochondrial fatigue' in otherwise-healthy people is a plausible but under-proven label. In genuine mitochondrial disease, blood lactate may rise abnormally with modest exertion.

fixes.what: Zone-2 aerobic exercise — the strongest and best-evidenced stimulus to build new mitochondria
Creatine to buffer cellular ATP — reasonable evidence for reducing mental fatigue
CoQ10 / ubiquinol is depleted by statins and age, but trials for statin-related symptoms and fatigue are mixed-to-negative — a reasonable trial, not a proven fix
PQQ and alpha-lipoic acid are theorised to support mitochondrial function; human evidence is weak

causes.confusedWith: Simple deconditioning — real, fixable fatigue from being sedentary, which also responds to graded aerobic training; and post-viral fatigue/ME-CFS, where exertion makes things worse.

chronic-fatigue.theOneThing: Fix sleep first, before reaching for any supplement. Anchor a consistent wake time, get bright light within an hour of waking, and protect a 7-9 hour sleep window. It's the single biggest lever for most people — and it also acts as a diagnostic: once your sleep is genuinely solid for two weeks, any fatigue that remains points you to a deeper driver (mood/stress, iron, thyroid, or blood sugar) worth testing.

cravings.intro: A sugar craving is almost never about weak willpower — it's a signal, and the signal has several possible sources that differ from person to person. For one person the driver is a glucose spike-and-crash that leaves the brain convinced it's running low on fuel two hours after lunch; for another it's a single short night that pushes hunger and reward signalling out of balance; for another it's cortisol from a stressful week nudging the reward system toward anything sweet; for another it's simply eating too little protein, so appetite stays switched on until the body gets what it's actually hunting for; and for people who menstruate, the week before a period reliably ratchets cravings up on its own. Most people have two or three of these stacked on top of each other. The job here is to find YOURS — the pattern below that matches when, and why, your cravings actually hit.
ladder: You crave sugar or can't stop snacking a few hours after eating
Your brain is receiving a false 'need fuel' or 'need reward' signal
Driven by glucose crashes, high ghrelin and reward drive, cortisol, unmet protein, or cyclical hormones
Rooted in how you build meals, how you sleep, your stress load, and (cyclically) your hormones — mostly modifiable
cravings.theOneThing: Rebuild your first two meals of the day around 30–40g of protein plus real fiber, eaten before any refined carbs. This single change flattens the glucose spike-and-crash that drives most afternoon cravings, satisfies the protein appetite that keeps hunger switched on, and gives a stable base so that sleep, stress and cyclical drivers have far less to pull on.

causes.name: Blood-sugar swings (reactive glucose dips)

chain.node: High-glycemic, low-fiber, low-protein meal
Rapid glucose spike drives a large insulin response
Glucose overshoots downward, dipping toward or below fasting baseline ~2–3h later (reactive dip)
Adrenaline (with glucagon) counter-regulation plus hypothalamic hunger drive fire — adrenaline is the main symptom driver
Craving for fast carbs, shakiness, energy slump, irritability

tell.symptoms: Cravings and hunger arrive 2–4h after a carby meal, often with shakiness, irritability or an energy slump; a few bites of sugar fix it within minutes. Worse after breakfasts of cereal/toast/juice than after eggs. Note the effect size is real but individual — some people 'dip' far more than others on the same meal.
tell.labMarker: CGM shows glucose rising sharply then dipping toward/below fasting baseline within ~2–3h; large 'dippers' report more hunger and eat more (PREDICT study — though the correlations are modest, r≈0.16–0.27). Importantly these dips occur even in metabolically healthy people, so a dip alone does NOT prove insulin resistance; check fasting insulin/HOMA-IR only to see whether IR is also present.

fixes.what: Build meals protein-and-fiber-first, carbs last, to blunt the spike and the rebound dip
Front-load 25–40g protein and 8–12g fiber per meal
Berberine to improve insulin sensitivity and flatten post-meal glucose (only useful if IR is present)
Myo-inositol to improve insulin signaling (best evidence in PCOS/insulin resistance)
Magnesium — repletion supports insulin sensitivity if you're deficient
Semaglutide (GLP-1 agonist) for refractory cravings with insulin resistance/obesity — slows gastric emptying, blunts glucose swings and appetite

causes.confusedWith: True organic hypoglycemia (rare — e.g. insulinoma) or early/undiagnosed diabetes; get glucose formally checked if dips are severe, occur fasted, or don't track meals.

causes.name: Sleep debt (ghrelin up, reward drive up)

chain.node: Short or fragmented sleep (<7h)
Ghrelin (hunger hormone) rises — the most reliable hormonal change
Endocannabinoid tone (2-AG) and mesolimbic reward sensitivity to calorie-dense food rise
Heightened sweet-taste preference plus more waking hours to eat
Increased hunger, sweet/starchy comfort-food cravings, evening/late-night snacking

tell.symptoms: Cravings are markedly worse the day after a poor or short night, skew toward sweets and starchy comfort food, and hit hardest in the evening. Good sleep = the cravings largely disappear.
tell.labMarker: No reliable clinical lab. Acute sleep-restriction crossover RCTs reliably raise ghrelin, sweet-taste preference and food intake; leptin changes are inconsistent across studies, so the popular 'leptin crashes when you're tired' story is oversimplified. Confirm by tracking sleep duration against next-day craving intensity.

fixes.what: Protect a consistent 7–9h sleep window with a fixed wake time
Cut caffeine after early afternoon and dim bright/blue light in the evening
Protein-rich breakfast to counter the next-day hunger/reward shift
Magnesium in the evening may support sleep quality if you're deficient

causes.name: Chronic stress (cortisol-driven hedonic eating)

chain.node: Chronic psychological stress / poor stress recovery
Sustained cortisol elevation signals a need for quick energy and raises reward sensitivity
Reward system shifts toward sugar/fat (nucleus accumbens, opioid system) while prefrontal self-control is impaired
Comfort-eating; palatable food transiently blunts glucocorticoid stress reactivity, reinforcing the loop
Emotional, non-hunger sugar cravings, especially after stressful days

tell.symptoms: Cravings track emotion and stress rather than physical hunger, target specific 'comfort' foods, and spike in the evening after a demanding day. You eat when tense/tired, not when your stomach is empty.
tell.labMarker: No routine diagnostic test; an elevated or flattened late-evening salivary cortisol can corroborate high stress load, but this cause is identified behaviorally, not by a lab.

fixes.what: Insert a non-food stress down-regulation habit at the usual craving time (walk, breathwork, short exercise)
Keep protein + fiber at each meal so stress cravings don't land on an empty, glucose-swinging tank
Prioritize sleep — sleep debt and cortisol amplify each other
Magnesium — depleted by chronic stress; may support HPA-axis regulation if deficient

causes.confusedWith: Binge-eating disorder — if eating feels out of control with distress/secrecy, that needs targeted support beyond a craving protocol.

chain.node: Low-protein diet diluted by processed carbs and fats
Protein target unmet → FGF21, the leading candidate 'low-protein' signal, rises and drives protein appetite (strong in rodents; emerging human data)
Drive to keep eating until protein need is met, overshooting on carbs/calories
Persistent grazing, never feeling truly satisfied, snack cravings

tell.symptoms: You feel hungry again soon after meals, graze all day, and are never quite 'full' — typically on a diet light in protein (skipped/carby breakfast, low-meat/low-dairy days). Cravings ease noticeably on higher-protein days.
tell.labMarker: No lab; estimate dietary protein — chronic intake below ~1.2 g/kg body weight (or <25–30g per meal) makes protein leverage plausible. Protein leverage is a well-supported hypothesis, not settled fact.

fixes.what: Hit 25–40g of protein at each main meal, starting with breakfast
Eat protein first in the meal to reach satiety before excess carbs

chain.node: Frequent ultra-processed foods engineered with sugar+fat+salt
Mesolimbic dopamine reward surge on eating them
Cue-conditioned 'wanting' (incentive sensitization) builds while normal satiety signalling is blunted
Cravings triggered by cues/habit rather than hunger; escalation and 'can't stop once I start'

tell.symptoms: You crave specific hyperpalatable foods even when physically full; cravings are triggered by cues (a screen, the vending machine, a time of day) and it's hard to stop once you start. Portion 'tolerance' has crept up over time.
tell.labMarker: No lab; identified by the cue-driven, reward-seeking pattern rather than a metabolic marker.

fixes.what: Design the environment — remove the trigger foods and cues from home/desk so the conditioned response isn't fired
Reduce ultra-processed food exposure for a few weeks to let reward sensitivity re-normalize
Swap to whole-food versions that satisfy without the engineered reward spike
Semaglutide (GLP-1 agonist) reduces reward-driven 'wanting'/food noise and hedonic eating in resistant cases (reduces 'wanting' more than 'liking')

causes.name: Luteal-phase / premenstrual cravings (cyclical)

chain.node: Late luteal (premenstrual) phase — for people who menstruate
Progesterone high and estrogen falling; central serotonin availability drops
Carbohydrate transiently raises serotonin (mood self-medication) while resting metabolic rate and genuine energy needs rise
Predictable, cyclical cravings for carbs/sweets and chocolate in the ~week before menses

tell.symptoms: Cravings appear predictably in the ~7–10 days before your period, ease once bleeding starts, and often come with low mood, irritability or fatigue. Strongest for carbs, sweets and chocolate.
tell.labMarker: No lab needed; diagnosed by cycle-tracking — cravings clustering in the late-luteal (premenstrual) week. Measured carbohydrate intake is highest in the luteal phase across studies, and eating somewhat more then is physiologically appropriate (RMR is elevated).

fixes.what: Track your cycle so luteal cravings are expected, not treated as a willpower 'failure'
Anchor the luteal week on steady complex carbs + protein to raise serotonin without a spike-and-crash
Don't over-restrict calories in the luteal week — energy needs are genuinely higher
Magnesium — limited evidence for reducing PMS symptoms/cravings, reasonable to trial

causes.confusedWith: PMDD (premenstrual dysphoric disorder) — if mood symptoms are severe or disabling, that's a distinct condition needing clinical treatment, not just a craving protocol.

ladder: Cold, tired, gaining weight, dry skin, foggy
Too little active thyroid hormone (T3) is reaching your cells
Either the gland can't make enough T4, or your body can't convert T4 into active T3
Missing raw materials (iodine, selenium, iron), an autoimmune attack on the gland, or too little food and too much stress
thyroid.intro: Feeling cold, slow, and puffy when everyone else feels fine is what it looks like when your cells stop getting enough active thyroid hormone (T3) — the master dial on your metabolic rate. But "slow thyroid" is not one disease with one fix. In one person the gland itself is under quiet autoimmune attack; in another the gland is fine but starved of the iodine it needs as raw material; in a third the gland makes plenty of the storage hormone T4 but the body can't convert it to active T3 because selenium, iron, or calories are missing. These produce nearly identical symptoms yet need opposite fixes — and a "normal TSH" can hide several of them. The job below is to help you find YOUR driver, because chasing the wrong one wastes months.

causes.name: Hashimoto's autoimmune thyroiditis

chain.node: Genetic + environmental autoimmune trigger
Anti-TPO / anti-thyroglobulin antibodies + cytotoxic T-cell attack
Lymphocytic infiltration, fibrosis and gradual destruction of thyroid follicles
Falling T4/T3 output, rising TSH as the pituitary shouts louder
Cold, fatigue, weight gain, dry skin, hair thinning

tell.symptoms: Symptoms creep in over months to years; often a personal or family history of autoimmune disease (celiac, type 1 diabetes, vitiligo, RA); gland may feel enlarged (goiter) early then shrink; symptoms fluctuate.
tell.labMarker: TPO antibodies positive (assay-dependent, typically >35 IU/mL) is the confirming test; usually with TSH high and free T4 low or low-normal.

fixes.what: Levothyroxine (T4) replacement titrated to TSH, added T3 if conversion is poor
Selenium 100-200 mcg/day modestly lowers TPO antibody titres in trials (effect real but inconsistent) and supports the gland's antioxidant defence
Screen for and treat coexisting celiac disease; trial gluten removal if antibodies stay high
Correct low vitamin D and the nutrient deficits (iron, zinc) that ride alongside autoimmune thyroiditis

causes.confusedWith: Transient/postpartum thyroiditis, which can cause the same picture but self-resolves and is antibody-variable

causes.name: Iodine deficiency (or excess)

chain.node: Low dietary iodine (no iodized salt, little seafood/dairy/eggs) or, conversely, iodine excess
Not enough iodide substrate for TPO to iodinate thyroglobulin and build hormone
Thyroid enlarges (goiter) trying to trap scarce iodine
Low T4/T3, sluggish metabolic rate

tell.symptoms: Diet genuinely low in iodized salt, seafood, dairy and eggs; pregnancy/breastfeeding raises the requirement; visible or palpable goiter. Note the mirror-image: chronic iodine EXCESS (kelp/iodine megadoses) can also suppress the gland.
tell.labMarker: Low urinary iodine concentration (median <100 µg/L is the population marker) with elevated TSH.

fixes.what: Use iodized salt as the default salt at home
Eat seafood, dairy and eggs; seaweed occasionally but not daily (kelp overshoots badly)
A modest iodine supplement (~150 mcg) only if intake is truly low — do not megadose, and pair with selenium

causes.confusedWith: Iodine-EXCESS hypothyroidism (failure to escape the Wolff-Chaikoff effect, where excess iodine transiently inhibits TPO) — more iodine makes this one worse, not better

causes.name: Selenium deficiency — impaired T4-to-T3 conversion

chain.node: Low selenium intake (selenium-poor soils, low brazil-nut/seafood/organ-meat diet)
Selenoprotein deiodinases (DIO1/DIO2) under-function; glutathione peroxidase falls
Peripheral conversion of storage T4 into active T3 drops; free T4 accumulates
Hypothyroid symptoms despite a normal-looking T4 and TSH

tell.symptoms: Persistent cold/fatigue/weight symptoms even though TSH and total/free T4 read 'normal'; often overlaps Hashimoto's (selenium also blunts TPO antibodies).
tell.labMarker: Low serum/plasma selenium; a HIGH free-T4-to-free-T3 ratio is the fingerprint of poor conversion (documented in selenium-deficient patients).

fixes.what: 1-2 brazil nuts daily, or seafood/organ meat, as a food-first selenium source
Selenium 100-200 mcg/day (selenomethionine); do not exceed ~400 mcg — excess is toxic
Ensure adequate zinc, a cofactor for deiodinase activity and thyroid receptor function

causes.confusedWith: Overt primary hypothyroidism — here TSH is often normal because the gland output (T4) is fine; the problem is downstream conversion

causes.name: Iron / ferritin deficiency — impaired TPO synthesis

chain.node: Low iron stores (heavy menstrual loss, low intake, poor absorption/low stomach acid)
Heme-dependent thyroid peroxidase loses activity without iron
Incomplete iodination of thyroglobulin inside the thyroid follicle
Reduced hormone synthesis; fatigue compounded by anemia

tell.symptoms: Most common in menstruating women and vegetarians; fatigue plus hair shedding, pallor, breathlessness on exertion, cold hands; symptoms improve when iron is repleted.
tell.labMarker: Low ferritin (<30 ng/mL suggestive of depletion, <15 deficient); TSH may drift upward as stores fall.

fixes.what: Iron-rich foods (red meat, legumes) paired with vitamin C to boost absorption; separate from tea/coffee/calcium
Oral iron supplement if ferritin is low, rechecked after ~8-12 weeks
Find and treat the blood-loss cause (heavy periods, GI loss) rather than just topping up

causes.confusedWith: Anemia-of-fatigue alone — iron deficiency and hypothyroidism cause near-identical tiredness and often coexist, so test both

causes.name: Under-eating / chronic stress — functional low-T3 (non-thyroidal)

chain.node: Aggressive dieting, very-low-carb/keto, over-training, or chronic psychological stress/illness
Low insulin/leptin signal energy shortage; cortisol and inflammatory cytokines suppress activating deiodinase (DIO1) and induce inactivating DIO3
T4-to-T3 conversion falls and T4 is diverted into inactive reverse-T3 — an inert metabolic end-product (a marker of the shift, not a proven receptor blocker) — rather than active T3
Cold, fatigue, low body temperature, stalled weight loss — with a normal TSH

tell.symptoms: Onset tracks a crash diet, keto phase, big calorie/carb cut, or a period of overtraining, illness and high stress; TSH looks normal but you feel hypothyroid; low libido, cold, plateaued.
tell.labMarker: Low free T3 with NORMAL TSH and normal/high free T4; elevated reverse T3 (rT3), most reliably in outright fasting/illness rather than modest low-carb alone.

fixes.what: Eat enough total calories; stop chronic aggressive restriction
Reintroduce adequate carbohydrate (isocaloric diets with even ~50 g/day prevented the T3 drop in controlled studies) rather than staying zero-carb
Cut over-training, prioritise sleep and stress reduction to lower cortisol
Ensure adequate protein and omega-3 to support recovery and lower inflammatory conversion blockade

causes.confusedWith: True primary hypothyroidism — check TSH and TPO antibodies before assuming it is purely functional; this one reverses with feeding and rest

thyroid.theOneThing: Get the actual labs before you treat: a full panel of TSH, free T4, free T3 PLUS TPO antibodies, ferritin, and selenium/iodine status — because these five drivers look identical but need opposite fixes. For most people the highest-leverage move is to secure the raw materials and conversion cofactors (iodine sufficiency without excess, selenium, iron/ferritin) and eat enough food and carbohydrate — while a positive TPO antibody means Hashimoto's and warrants medical management, not just supplements.

ladder: The cuff reading is the last rung, not the cause — it's the pressure your arteries are already under
Proximate cause: too much fluid volume in the pipes AND pipes that won't relax (lost nitric-oxide vasodilation)
Upstream drivers differ per person: sodium-potassium imbalance, insulin resistance, sympathetic/aldosterone overdrive, or age-stiffened arteries
Root behaviours: what you eat (salt vs. potassium, refined carbs), how you sleep and breathe at night, how much you move and drink
blood-pressure.intro: Blood pressure is the number where several different problems all surface as one reading, which is why two people with identical 150/95 cuffs can need completely different fixes. At its core, pressure is set by two things: how much fluid volume your kidneys are holding, and how easily your arteries relax to let that volume through. Most "essential" hypertension is your kidneys quietly retaining sodium — driven by a salty, low-potassium diet, by insulin resistance, or by the hormone aldosterone — while your artery walls simultaneously lose their nitric-oxide "relax" signal and stiffen with age. But a meaningful slice of cases is powered by things you'd never guess from the number alone: undiagnosed sleep apnea hammering your nervous system every night, or an over-active adrenal gland. The job here is not to memorise all of them — it's to find YOURS, because the lever that fixes a salt-sensitive 30-year-old does almost nothing for a 65-year-old with stiff arteries or a snorer with sleep apnea.

causes.name: Salt-sensitivity: high sodium, low potassium + endothelial nitric-oxide deficit

chain.node: High-sodium, low-potassium diet (processed/restaurant food, little fruit & veg)
Kidney fails to fully excrete the sodium load; distal-tubule NCC keeps reabsorbing salt and water
Expanded blood volume, plus reduced nitric-oxide vasodilation so vessels can't offload the extra volume
Volume-loaded, under-dilated arteries
Elevated blood pressure

tell.symptoms: Readings jump after salty/restaurant meals; puffiness or ankle swelling; you eat few fruits and vegetables; BP responds well when you cut salt and add produce. Salt-sensitivity is more common with age, higher body weight, insulin resistance, and in people of African descent (roughly twice as prevalent as in white populations).
tell.labMarker: 24-hour urine sodium >~4 g/day flags high intake; a high spot urinary sodium-to-potassium ratio predicts BP better than either alone; serum potassium often low-normal.

fixes.what: Cut processed and restaurant sodium (the ~70% of dietary salt that is hidden) toward <2 g sodium/day
Eat a DASH-style plate: 4-6 daily servings of potassium-rich plants (leafy greens, beans, potato, banana) to shift the sodium:potassium ratio
Dietary nitrate to restore nitric-oxide vasodilation (meta-analyses: ~3-5 mmHg systolic drop, at the higher end in hypertensives)
L-citrulline to raise arginine/NO substrate for vasodilation (RCT meta-analyses: ~4-7 mmHg systolic)
Magnesium, which relaxes vascular smooth muscle and modestly lowers BP, mostly in deficiency or when already on BP drugs

causes.confusedWith: Primary aldosteronism — also salt-sensitive and volume-driven, but with suppressed renin and a tendency to low potassium; screen if BP is resistant.

causes.name: Insulin resistance / metabolic syndrome (hyperinsulinemia)

chain.node: Excess refined carbohydrate/calories, inactivity, visceral fat accumulation
Insulin resistance → compensatory chronically high insulin
Kidney shows SELECTIVE insulin resistance: IRS1 signalling is blunted but IRS2 stays intact, so high insulin keeps driving proximal-tubule sodium reabsorption, plus sympathetic activation
Volume expansion + vasoconstriction + vascular smooth-muscle proliferation
Elevated blood pressure

tell.symptoms: Central/belly weight, high triglycerides, low HDL, creeping fasting glucose, skin tags or darkened neck creases (acanthosis), energy crashes after carb meals. BP tends to travel with weight and waist size.
tell.labMarker: Fasting insulin elevated / HOMA-IR >~2; triglyceride-to-HDL ratio elevated; HbA1c or fasting glucose rising; waist circumference above threshold.

fixes.what: Cut added sugar and refined starch; build meals around protein, fibre and non-starchy veg to lower the insulin load
Resistance training + Zone-2 cardio to restore muscle insulin sensitivity
Lose visceral fat — even 5-10% body-weight loss meaningfully lowers BP
Magnesium, often depleted in insulin resistance and involved in insulin signalling
Omega-3 for triglycerides and endothelial function

causes.confusedWith: Simple overweight without true insulin resistance — check fasting insulin/triglycerides rather than assuming. Note the IR→BP link is a strong association and mechanistically plausible, but direct causation in humans is only partly proven, so treat this as a driver that travels with metabolic disease rather than a guaranteed single cause.

causes.name: Obstructive sleep apnea & chronic sympathetic overdrive

chain.node: Obstructive sleep apnea, fragmented sleep, or chronic psychological stress
Repeated nighttime hypoxia and arousals trigger surges of sympathetic nerve activity
Sustained catecholamine drive plus secondary aldosterone/RAAS elevation from the apnea-induced hypoxemia
Vasoconstriction and BP that fails to dip at night (non-dipping)
Sustained, often drug-resistant, daytime hypertension

tell.symptoms: Loud snoring, witnessed pauses in breathing, daytime sleepiness, morning headaches, overweight or large neck circumference, and BP that resists 2-3 medications. Often the BP won't drop overnight.
tell.labMarker: Home sleep study / apnea-hypopnea index (AHI): >5 mild, >15 moderate, >30 severe; 24-hour ambulatory BP showing a non-dipping nighttime pattern. OSA is present in roughly 70-83% of resistant hypertension.

fixes.what: Get a sleep study; if OSA confirmed, CPAP (or a mandibular device) lowers BP, especially nighttime readings (effect is real but modest, ~2-3 mmHg on average, larger with good adherence)
Weight loss and side-sleeping to reduce airway collapse
Stop evening alcohol, which relaxes airway muscles and worsens apnea and BP
Daily slow-breathing / down-regulation practice to lower baseline sympathetic tone

causes.confusedWith: White-coat or anxiety-driven readings — confirm with home or 24-hour ambulatory monitoring before assuming true sustained hypertension.

causes.name: Aldosterone / RAAS overactivation (obesity-linked and primary aldosteronism)

chain.node: Visceral adiposity driving aldosterone, or an autonomously over-producing adrenal gland (primary aldosteronism)
Aldosterone excess relative to suppressed renin
ENaC-driven sodium and water reabsorption with potassium wasting in the distal nephron
Volume expansion plus direct vascular and cardiac remodeling/fibrosis
Hard-to-control hypertension

tell.symptoms: BP resistant to 2-3 drugs, hypertension at a young age or unusually severe, muscle cramps or weakness, needing potassium supplements. Note: potassium is often normal, so normal potassium does NOT rule it out.
tell.labMarker: Aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR) elevated with suppressed renin is the screening test; primary aldosteronism is found in ~5-10% of all hypertension and up to ~20% of resistant cases — and is badly underdiagnosed.

fixes.what: Ask to be screened with an aldosterone-to-renin ratio if BP is resistant, early-onset, or comes with low potassium
If confirmed, a mineralocorticoid-receptor blocker (spironolactone/eplerenone) targets the actual driver
Sodium restriction, which amplifies the BP benefit when aldosterone is high
Weight loss to reduce adipose-driven aldosterone secretion

causes.confusedWith: Essential hypertension with low potassium caused by thiazide diuretics — check aldosterone/renin off, or while accounting for, interfering drugs.

causes.name: Arterial stiffness of aging (accelerated by alcohol & inactivity) → isolated systolic hypertension

chain.node: Aging plus accelerants: excess alcohol, physical inactivity, and glycation from high glucose
Elastin degradation and collagen cross-linking in the aortic wall, with falling nitric-oxide availability
Loss of aortic Windkessel cushioning — the aorta can no longer buffer each heartbeat
Wide pulse pressure / isolated systolic hypertension (high systolic, normal-to-low diastolic)

tell.symptoms: Older age; systolic high (e.g. 150s-160s) while diastolic is normal or even low (e.g. 60s-70s); a wide gap between the two numbers; often a regular-drinker or sedentary pattern.
tell.labMarker: Wide pulse pressure on the cuff (>60 mmHg; normal is 30-60) is the bedside clue; carotid-femoral pulse wave velocity is elevated if measured.

fixes.what: Cut alcohol — the BP rise is dose-dependent with NO safe threshold (even 1-2 drinks/day measurably raises it), via sympathetic and RAAS activation
Regular aerobic exercise, which improves arterial compliance and endothelial function (structural collagen cross-linking is only partly reversible, so aim to slow progression)
Dietary nitrate to improve vasodilation and reduce arterial stiffness markers
Omega-3 for endothelial function and modest arterial-stiffness benefit

causes.confusedWith: Volume-driven or salt-sensitive hypertension in older adults — pulse pressure width helps distinguish stiffness-dominant cases.

blood-pressure.theOneThing: Shift your sodium-to-potassium ratio: cut hidden processed/restaurant salt AND add 4-6 daily servings of potassium-rich plants (a DASH pattern). It is the single lever that simultaneously reduces fluid volume, restores endothelial vasodilation, and directly counters salt-sensitivity — the mechanism at work in the largest share of people. Then, if BP stays high despite this, screen for the hidden drivers: get a sleep study if you snore, and ask for an aldosterone-to-renin ratio if it's resistant.

ladder: You see high LDL / cholesterol (or high triglycerides) on your lipid panel

The proximate cause is almost always a clearance problem: your liver's LDL receptors aren't pulling enough LDL out of the blood — or your liver is over-producing lipid particles faster than they clear

That clearance/overproduction is driven by one or more upstream levers: a saturated-fat and low-fibre diet, insulin resistance from visceral fat and sugar, an underactive thyroid, falling estrogen, or an inherited receptor defect
Root behaviours: what fat and fibre you eat, how much visceral fat, refined sugar and alcohol you carry — plus three non-diet checks (thyroid, hormonal status, family history) that change everything
cholesterol.intro: High cholesterol is not one disease — it is a readout of the balance between how well your liver clears LDL particles and how fast it makes them. The panel can look identical whether the real driver is your diet, insulin resistance, an underactive thyroid, falling estrogen, or an inherited receptor defect — but the fix for each is completely different, so the whole job is to work out which lever is yours. Two cheap checks (a TSH and your family history) rule the causes that will NOT respond to diet in or out before you commit to a lifestyle-only plan.

causes.name: Saturated-fat-heavy, low-soluble-fibre diet

chain.node: Diet high in saturated fat (fried food, fatty/processed meat, butter, coconut/palm) and low in soluble fibre
Saturated fat raises the liver's regulatory cholesterol pool, which suppresses SREBP2 processing and therefore LDL-receptor gene expression
Fewer LDL receptors clearing particles + little bile-acid loss of cholesterol (soluble fibre normally binds bile and forces the liver to burn cholesterol to replace it)
LDL particles accumulate in the circulation
Elevated LDL-C on the lipid panel

tell.symptoms: No physical symptoms — this is the person whose diet history is heavy in fried/fatty food, cheese, processed meat and refined starch, and light on vegetables, oats, beans and nuts. LDL usually moves meaningfully within 6-12 weeks of changing the diet.
tell.labMarker: LDL-C above ~3.4 mmol/L (130 mg/dL); typically NOT extreme (rarely >4.9) and responds to diet — which distinguishes it from familial hypercholesterolemia

fixes.what: Replace saturated fat (fried food, fatty/processed meat, butter, coconut oil) with unsaturated fats — extra-virgin olive oil, nuts, avocado, oily fish
Add 10-25 g/day soluble fibre — oats/oat bran, legumes, psyllium husk, apples, barley — to pull cholesterol out via bile
Citrus Bergamot — polyphenols shown in trials to lower LDL and total cholesterol (product quality varies)
Red Yeast Rice — contains monacolin K (chemically identical to lovastatin); genuinely lowers LDL 15-25% but dose/purity varies wildly between products and it carries the same cautions as a statin

causes.confusedWith: Familial hypercholesterolemia — if LDL stays very high (>4.9 mmol/L) despite a genuinely clean diet, it is not just the food

causes.name: Insulin resistance / metabolic dyslipidemia (visceral fat + sugar + alcohol)

chain.node: Excess refined carbohydrate/sugar and visceral (belly) fat driving chronically high insulin
High free-fatty-acid flux to the liver — amplified by alcohol, which directly stimulates hepatic triglyceride synthesis — drives hepatic VLDL overproduction
CETP swaps triglycerides from VLDL onto LDL and HDL; hepatic lipase then remodels them
Result is high triglycerides, low HDL, and small dense LDL particles
Atherogenic pattern on panel: high triglycerides, low HDL, elevated non-HDL/ApoB

tell.symptoms: The giveaway is the PATTERN: triglycerides high and HDL low (not just LDL up), plus central adiposity, larger waist, skin tags, sometimes fatty liver on ultrasound. Regular alcohol intake is a common and reversible amplifier. Often LDL-C looks only mildly high but ApoB/non-HDL is worse than it appears.
tell.labMarker: Triglycerides >1.7 mmol/L (150 mg/dL), HDL <1.0 (men)/<1.3 (women) mmol/L, high TG:HDL ratio; fasting glucose/HbA1c/fasting insulin (HOMA-IR) elevated

fixes.what: Cut refined carbohydrate, added sugar (sugary drinks, white rice/bread, desserts) and alcohol — the strongest single levers on triglycerides
Lose visceral fat via calorie deficit + resistance and zone-2 cardio; even 5-10% weight loss shifts the whole panel
Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) — high-dose (2-4 g) meaningfully lowers triglycerides
Berberine — improves insulin sensitivity and lowers LDL/triglycerides by stabilising LDL-receptor mRNA (AMPK) and inhibiting PCSK9

causes.confusedWith: Pure dietary high-LDL — that raises LDL without the low-HDL/high-triglyceride signature seen here

causes.name: Hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid)

chain.node: Low circulating thyroid hormone (Hashimoto's, iodine issues, post-partum, medication) with rising TSH
Loss of thyroid-hormone drive on LDL-receptor gene transcription; high TSH independently raises hepatic PCSK9, which degrades LDL receptors
Fewer functional LDL receptors → reduced LDL clearance
LDL and total cholesterol accumulate
Elevated LDL-C / total cholesterol, sometimes strikingly so

tell.symptoms: High cholesterol that seems out of proportion to the diet, PLUS thyroid clues: fatigue, cold intolerance, unexplained weight gain, constipation, dry skin, hair thinning, heavy/irregular periods. Common and routinely missed — every new high-cholesterol workup should include a TSH.
tell.labMarker: TSH elevated (typically >4-5 mIU/L; overt if >10), free T4 low or low-normal; lipids often normalize once thyroid is treated

fixes.what: Get TSH and free T4 tested BEFORE starting a statin — treating the thyroid can fix the cholesterol on its own
Levothyroxine to restore euthyroid state under a doctor; recheck lipids after 6-8 weeks
Ensure adequate iodine and selenium intake if deficient (do not megadose)

causes.confusedWith: Primary/diet-driven hypercholesterolemia — the fix is completely different, so confirm thyroid status first

causes.name: Familial hypercholesterolemia (inherited receptor defect)

chain.node: Inherited pathogenic variant in LDLR, APOB, or PCSK9 (autosomal dominant; ~1 in 250)
Defective LDL receptor or its ligand — LDL cannot be efficiently bound and cleared from birth
Lifelong LDL accumulation; cholesterol deposits in tendons (xanthomas) and arterial walls decades early
Very high LDL from a young age and premature atherosclerotic disease

tell.symptoms: LDL that stays very high despite a genuinely good diet; strong family history of early heart attack or stroke (men <55, women <65); tendon xanthomas (thickened Achilles/knuckle tendons) or corneal arcus at a young age.
tell.labMarker: LDL-C persistently >4.9 mmol/L (190 mg/dL) in adults (>4.0/155 in children); genetic testing of LDLR/APOB/PCSK9 confirms

fixes.what: High-intensity statin is first-line and usually essential — diet alone will not normalise FH
Add ezetimibe and/or a PCSK9 inhibitor if LDL target is not reached on statin
Cascade-screen first-degree relatives (parents, siblings, children) — each has a 50% chance of carrying it
Diet/fibre and Red Yeast Rice help at the margin but are adjuncts, never the whole answer

causes.confusedWith: Diet-driven high LDL — FH persists despite clean eating and needs pharmacotherapy, not just lifestyle

causes.name: Menopausal estrogen decline

chain.node: Loss of estrogen through perimenopause/menopause
Reduced estrogen signalling lowers hepatic LDL-receptor expression and slows conversion of cholesterol to bile acids
Reduced LDL clearance raises LDL and total cholesterol; Lp(a) also tends to rise, but through a separate estrogen-dependent pathway that is independent of the LDL receptor
LDL/total cholesterol climb in a woman whose lipids were previously fine

tell.symptoms: A woman in her late 40s-50s whose LDL and total cholesterol start rising in step with perimenopausal symptoms (cycle changes, hot flushes, sleep disruption), with no big change in her diet. Note the magnitude attributable to menopause versus chronological ageing and concurrent visceral-fat gain is genuinely debated.
tell.labMarker: Rising LDL-C and total cholesterol tracking the menopause transition; elevated FSH confirms menopausal status

fixes.what: Double down on the diet levers (unsaturated fat swap + soluble fibre) and resistance training, which blunt the menopausal lipid shift
Soluble fibre and oily fish/omega-3 to support LDL and triglyceride control
Discuss menopausal hormone therapy with a clinician — it can improve the lipid profile, but the decision is individual and it is not prescribed for lipids alone
Consider a statin if cardiovascular risk warrants, independent of hormones

causes.confusedWith: Metabolic dyslipidemia — menopause also drives visceral fat gain, so the two often overlap and both should be addressed

cholesterol.theOneThing: For most people the highest-leverage move is a single dietary swap plus one blood test: replace saturated fat (fried food, fatty/processed meat, butter) with unsaturated fats (olive oil, nuts, oily fish) AND add 10-25 g/day of soluble fibre (oats, legumes, psyllium) — this simultaneously restores LDL-receptor clearance and drags cholesterol out through bile, and it also improves the insulin-resistance pattern. Before assuming it is just diet, get a TSH and know your family history, because a missed thyroid or an inherited receptor defect needs a completely different fix.

brain-fog.intro: Brain fog isn't a diagnosis — it's what your cortex does when neurons can't get three things at once: steady fuel, clean signaling, and enough spare bandwidth to hold a thought. Take any one away and thinking turns slow, effortful, and unfocused, like reading through frosted glass. The trap is that the fog feels identical no matter what's causing it, but the causes are wildly different person to person: one person's fog is last night's broken sleep, another's is a mind so loaded with stress and low mood there's no working memory left, a third's is the reactive dip 90 minutes after a rice-heavy lunch, a fourth's is a ferritin of 18 or a B12 that quietly slid low on a plant-based diet, and a fifth's is low-grade inflammation left over from a virus. Most people who stay foggy have two or three of these stacked together. So the job isn't to find THE cause — it's to find YOURS by matching the pattern: when it hits, what makes it worse, and what one lab would confirm it.
ladder: The fog: thinking feels slow, effortful, words and recall don't come
Proximate: your neurons aren't getting steady glucose, clean neurotransmission, or enough spare attentional bandwidth
Upstream drivers: sleep debt, chronic stress or low mood, blood-sugar swings, low iron/B12, inflammatory cytokines, or low thyroid — usually more than one
Root: your nightly sleep window, your stress/mood load, what and when you eat, and untested nutrient/thyroid gaps
brain-fog.theOneThing: Fix sleep first. Protect a consistent 7-9 hour window with a fixed wake time and get bright daylight within an hour of waking. It's the single most common driver of fog, it clears the adenosine that directly drives the haze, and it silently amplifies every other cause on this list — stress, blood sugar, and inflammation all get worse on a sleep-deprived brain, so nothing else you try works well until this is handled.

causes.name: Sleep debt & circadian disruption

chain.node: Short, fragmented, or mistimed sleep
Adenosine accumulates and A1 receptors upregulate — measured in humans by PET, highest in orbitofrontal cortex (sleep pressure)
Overnight glymphatic clearance of metabolic waste likely drops — mechanism robust in rodents but contested/unproven in humans (tier 2)
