Prefrontal cortex under-fuels and under-fires
Slow processing, poor recall, effortful thinking

tell.symptoms: Fog is worst in the morning or after a bad night, and lifts noticeably after a couple of good nights. You're under ~7h, waking often, or sleeping at irregular times. Loud snoring, witnessed pauses, or unrefreshing sleep despite enough hours points to apnea driving it.
tell.labMarker: No routine blood test; use a sleep tracker for duration/regularity. If snoring or gasping, a sleep study — AHI >5 confirms obstructive apnea as the driver.

fixes.what: Fixed wake time 7 days/week and a protected 7-9h window; morning daylight within an hour of waking
No caffeine after early afternoon; dim screens and lights in the last hour before bed
Creatine to buffer brain ATP — a single high dose blunted sleep-deprivation cognitive decline in a controlled crossover trial; use on days you're short
Screen for and treat sleep apnea if you snore or wake unrefreshed

causes.confusedWith: Depression or adult ADHD — both cause fog and both can be driven or mimicked by chronic sleep loss.

causes.name: Chronic stress, anxiety & depression

chain.node: Sustained psychological stress, an anxiety disorder, or depression
Rumination and worry occupy working-memory and attentional bandwidth; sustained HPA-axis activation raises cortisol (the cortisol-to-cognition mechanism is real but more associative, tier 2)
Prefrontal executive control and hippocampal memory encoding are down-regulated — a core, measurable feature of clinical depression and anxiety
Poor concentration, forgetfulness, mental fatigue, 'can't think straight'

tell.symptoms: Fog tracks your mood, worry, and life circumstances rather than a bad night or a meal. Comes with low motivation, irritability, a racing mind at bedtime, anhedonia, or persistent worry. Worse under deadlines and around stressful periods; eases on low-demand days or holidays.
tell.labMarker: No blood test — this cause is invisible to labs. A validated screen (PHQ-9 for depression, GAD-7 for anxiety) is the confirmation; a positive screen reframes the fog as a treatable mood/anxiety problem.

fixes.what: Treat the mood/anxiety directly — CBT or another evidence-based psychotherapy targets the rumination that eats working memory
Regular aerobic exercise — antidepressant-grade effect on both mood and cognition
SSRI/SNRI or a referral if depression or anxiety is moderate-to-severe
Cut evening stimulation and build a wind-down routine so a racing mind doesn't also wreck sleep (which compounds the fog)

causes.confusedWith: Hypothyroidism, anemia, or sleep apnea — all produce low mood plus fog, so screen those before settling on 'just stress'; but the reverse error, calling treatable depression 'brain fog', is just as common.

causes.name: Blood-sugar swings / post-meal crashes

chain.node: High-glycemic or sugary meal (refined carbs, liquid sugar)
Large insulin surge, sometimes overshooting

Sharp glucose fall 60-120 min later — in a minority it drops below baseline (true reactive hypoglycemia); more often it's the size of the swing itself, not documented hypoglycemia, that tracks with the dip in cognition
Neurons — which hold no fuel reserve — lose steady glucose supply
Post-meal haze, drowsiness, can't concentrate

tell.symptoms: Fog reliably hits 1-2 hours after high-carb meals (the classic post-lunch crash), comes with drowsiness, shakiness, or sugar cravings, and eating again relieves it. Often paired with an expanding waistline or a family history of type 2 diabetes.
tell.labMarker: A CGM showing sharp post-meal spikes then rapid dips is the most direct evidence. HbA1c high-normal to prediabetic (5.5-6.4%), fasting insulin elevated, or HOMA-IR high indicate the insulin-resistant tendency behind it.

fixes.what: Eat protein and fiber before carbs; cut refined sugar and all liquid sugar (lower sugar_g, raise fiber_g/protein_g)
10-15 minute walk after meals to blunt the glucose spike
Swap refined carbs for whole, lower-glycemic sources so fuel arrives steadily

causes.confusedWith: Normal drowsiness from a large meal — the tell is the delayed crash with concentration loss, not immediate post-meal sleepiness.

causes.name: Nutrient deficiency (iron / B12 — plus weaker D / omega-3)

chain.node: Low iron/ferritin or B12 (menstrual loss, plant-based diet, PPIs/metformin, poor absorption)
Iron is a cofactor for tyrosine hydroxylase, the rate-limiting enzyme in dopamine synthesis; B12 drives methylation and myelin upkeep, and functional shortfall raises MMA and homocysteine
Reduced O2 delivery + impaired neurotransmitter synthesis and nerve conduction
Attention and processing-speed networks under-supplied
Persistent fog, fatigue, poor focus

tell.symptoms: Fog with fatigue, breathlessness on stairs, hair shedding, cold hands, or restless legs (iron); or tingling/numbness in hands and feet, sore tongue, balance issues (B12). Common in menstruating women, vegans/vegetarians, over-60s, and long-term acid-blocker users.
tell.labMarker: Ferritin <30 ng/mL confirms iron deficiency (30-50 is suboptimal and can drive fatigue/restless legs, though the link to cognition specifically is weaker). B12 <300 pg/mL is a borderline grey zone — check MMA/homocysteine, both of which rise when B12 is functionally low at the tissue level.

fixes.what: Replete iron with a vitamin-C source to a ferritin >50, then retest in 8-12 weeks
B12 as methylcobalamin if low or if plant-based/over-60/on a PPI

Red meat, shellfish, and leafy greens with a vitamin-C source; correct low vitamin D and omega-3 intake (both weaker, more associative contributors — worth fixing if deficient, not a reliable stand-alone cause)
Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) to support membrane and neurotransmission if dietary intake is low

causes.confusedWith: Depression or chronic fatigue syndrome — both overlap heavily, which is why a ferritin and B12 panel is worth doing before concluding it's 'just stress'.

causes.name: Chronic / post-viral neuroinflammation

chain.node: Infection (post-viral/long COVID), chronic low-grade inflammation, obesity or gut dysbiosis
Circulating IL-6 and TNF-alpha signal across to the brain
Microglial activation shifts neurotransmitter metabolism (sickness behavior) — well-mapped in animals; the causal link to chronic human brain fog outside acute post-viral states is more associative
Prefrontal cortex and anterior cingulate function suppressed
Mental sluggishness, malaise, fog

tell.symptoms: The fog started after an infection or runs alongside fatigue, malaise, and post-exertional worsening. It flares when you're run down or sick, and feels 'systemic' rather than tied to a bad night or a meal.
tell.labMarker: hs-CRP mildly elevated (1-3 mg/L average, >3 high) suggests low-grade inflammation; IL-6/TNF-alpha aren't routine clinical tests.

fixes.what: Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) for resolution-phase, anti-inflammatory support of brain signaling
Cut refined sugar and raise fiber and polyphenols to lower systemic and gut-driven inflammation
Regular moderate exercise and adequate sleep, both directly anti-inflammatory; treat the root source (gut, weight, infection)
Lion's Mane for neurotrophic support — human cognition trials are mixed and mostly small (emerging, tier-1 evidence)

causes.confusedWith: Depression or generalized anxiety — cytokine-driven 'sickness behavior' looks like low mood and slowed thinking, but tracks with inflammation and infection history rather than life circumstance.

causes.name: Low thyroid / hormonal

chain.node: Underactive thyroid — clearest when overt (Hashimoto's, low iodine/selenium, postpartum)

Low free T4/T3 lowers cerebral metabolic rate and slows neurotransmission — this mechanism applies to overt hypothyroidism; in subclinical disease T4 is still normal and the fog link is weak and conflicting across studies
Neuronal energy metabolism and neurotransmission slow
Sluggish thinking, memory lapses, heavy fatigue

tell.symptoms: Fog with weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, thinning hair or outer-eyebrow loss, and a slow pulse. More common in women, postpartum, and mid-life.
tell.labMarker: Overt hypothyroidism — TSH clearly elevated (often >10 mIU/L) WITH low free T4 — is the confident driver. Subclinical (TSH 4-10, normal free T4) correlates only weakly and inconsistently with fog. Positive anti-TPO antibodies point to Hashimoto's.

fixes.what: Get a TSH + free T4 (and anti-TPO) test — this cause is invisible without it
Levothyroxine replacement if overt hypothyroidism is confirmed (treating fog on subclinical numbers alone is not reliably beneficial)
Adequate iodine and selenium (e.g. a Brazil nut or two) to support thyroid hormone synthesis

causes.confusedWith: Depression or perimenopause — the overlap is large, so an abnormal free T4 (not a borderline TSH alone) is the deciding factor.

low-mood.intro: Low mood rarely has one cause, and the \"chemical imbalance\" story is too small. In most people, depression is the downstream signal of a reward-and-mood system being suppressed from several directions at once: a stress axis stuck in the \"on\" position, a broken sleep/circadian clock, having withdrawn from the people and activities that used to reward you, low-grade inflammation quietly stealing the raw material for serotonin, missing nutrient cofactors, or (rarely) a slow thyroid. Each can produce the exact same surface experience: flat, joyless, no drive. That is why two people with identical symptoms need opposite fixes. The job here is to find YOURS. Notice which \"tell\" fits: does your mood track your sleep, your stress, your isolation and inactivity, a physical heaviness, or fatigue that started when your diet or health changed? The pattern points to the lever.
ladder: Low mood, no drive, nothing feels enjoyable (anhedonia)
Reward + mood circuits are being suppressed — blunted dopamine/serotonin signaling, low BDNF/plasticity
Driven upstream by chronic stress, broken sleep/circadian rhythm, social withdrawal and inactivity, low-grade inflammation, nutrient gaps, or a slow thyroid
which trace back to daily inputs — light timing, sleep, diet, movement, human contact, unmanaged stressors — plus a few fixable medical deficits (thyroid, B12/folate, iron, vitamin D)
low-mood.theOneThing: Anchor your circadian clock: a fixed early wake time with 10–30 minutes of bright OUTDOOR light within an hour of waking, plus daily movement — ideally outdoors and, where possible, with another person. This one routine pushes on several drivers at once: it stabilizes sleep, helps reset the cortisol rhythm, raises daytime serotonergic tone, and forces behavioral activation and social/physical re-engagement. Do it while getting a simple blood panel (TSH, 25-OH vitamin D, ferritin, B12/folate, hs-CRP) to catch the fixable medical drivers most people never test for. It will not fix a thyroid or nutrient deficit — that is what the panel is for.

causes.name: Chronic stress / HPA-axis dysregulation

chain.node: Relentless, unresolved stressor (work, caregiving, financial, loss)
Sustained CRH-driven cortisol output
Glucocorticoid-receptor resistance (FKBP5-linked) weakens negative feedback, so the axis stays switched on
Suppressed hippocampal BDNF and neurogenesis, reduced plasticity
Anhedonia, low drive, early-morning waking

tell.symptoms: The low mood started or deepened during a period of relentless stress. Often 'wired but tired' — anxious and flat at once, waking at 3–4am unable to get back down, worse in the morning.
tell.labMarker: No routine blood test. Some (not all) show a flattened diurnal salivary cortisol curve or loss of the normal morning peak; frank hypercortisolemia is mainly seen in melancholic/severe depression, and cortisol is often normal — the pattern matters more than any single number.

fixes.what: Actually offload or restructure the chronic stressor — this is causal, not optional; nothing downstream fixes an ongoing source
Daily Zone-2 exercise + a wind-down practice (slow breathing/meditation) to lower sympathetic tone and help restore cortisol rhythm
Protect sleep — cortisol dysregulation and sleep loss feed each other
EPA-predominant omega-3 to blunt the stress-inflammation coupling

causes.confusedWith: Generalized anxiety disorder or burnout — heavy symptom overlap; the anhedonia/low-drive core points to depression.

causes.name: Poor sleep / circadian disruption

chain.node: Insomnia, irregular sleep timing, or minimal daytime bright light
Circadian misalignment: delayed melatonin onset, weakened daytime serotonergic tone
Impaired overnight emotional processing (REM/slow-wave sleep), amygdala hyper-reactivity
Low mood, irritability, blunted reward

tell.symptoms: Mood visibly tracks your sleep — worse after bad nights, lighter after good ones. Night-owl schedule, little morning sunlight, screens late, socially jet-lagged on weekends.
tell.labMarker: No standard blood test. A 2-week sleep diary or actigraphy reveals the pattern; dim-light melatonin onset only in specialist settings.

fixes.what: Fixed wake time 7 days a week + 10–30 min bright outdoor light within an hour of waking (or a 10,000-lux lamp in winter)
Dim lights and stop screens 1–2h before bed; keep the bedroom cool and dark
CBT-I (cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia) — first-line and more durable than sleeping pills

causes.confusedWith: Bipolar II depression — screen for past hypomania before assuming unipolar, because antidepressants alone can destabilize it.

causes.name: Social disconnection / behavioral withdrawal & inactivity

chain.node: Loneliness/social isolation, loss of rewarding activity, and physical inactivity — often set off by loss, a life change, or the depression itself
Falling rate of positive reinforcement + sedentary living; reward-learning circuits get little to act on
Blunted mesolimbic dopamine reward signaling and lower exercise-driven BDNF
Deepening anhedonia, inertia and withdrawal — a self-reinforcing downward spiral

tell.symptoms: Life has quietly narrowed — you see fewer people, do fewer things you used to enjoy, and move less. The less you do, the less you feel like doing. Loneliness or a recent loss/relocation often precedes the dip.
tell.labMarker: No lab test. Track it behaviorally: activity/step count and a simple log of social contact and enjoyable activities over 1–2 weeks.

fixes.what: Behavioral activation: schedule small, valued or pleasurable activities and do them regardless of motivation — action precedes mood, not the reverse
Rebuild regular social contact deliberately (loneliness is a causal driver, not just a symptom)
Regular exercise — Mendelian-randomization and trial evidence support a genuine antidepressant effect; cut sedentary time (especially TV)

causes.confusedWith: Normal grief or situational sadness — expected after loss and usually retains reactivity; persistent pervasive anhedonia and functional impairment mark the crossover to depression.

causes.name: Inflammatory / 'sickness-behavior' depression

chain.node: Visceral adiposity, ultra-processed/high-sugar diet, autoimmune disease, or chronic infection
Elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF) and CRP
Cytokines induce IDO, shunting tryptophan down the kynurenine pathway and away from serotonin
Less serotonin plus neurotoxic kynurenine metabolites (e.g. quinolinic acid, an NMDA agonist) in limbic circuits
Fatigue, anhedonia, social withdrawal, appetite/sleep changes

tell.symptoms: The depression has a physically 'sick' quality — leaden fatigue, achiness, brain fog, strongly blunted reward, often alongside obesity, metabolic syndrome, or an autoimmune condition. Frequently poorly responsive to standard SSRIs.
tell.labMarker: hs-CRP elevated (>3 mg/L = higher inflammatory load; 1–3 intermediate); IL-6 elevated if measured. Caveat: only a SUBSET of depressed people are inflamed — a normal CRP largely rules this driver out.

fixes.what: Cut refined sugar and ultra-processed foods (lower sugar_g); shift to a Mediterranean/anti-inflammatory pattern high in fiber (fiber_g) to feed short-chain-fatty-acid producers
EPA-predominant omega-3 (≥60% EPA, ~1–2g EPA/day) — the fraction with the best anti-inflammatory antidepressant signal, strongest as an add-on to standard treatment
Correct low vitamin D, which modulates immune tone (a supporting move, not a primary antidepressant — RCTs are weak)
Exercise and fat loss to lower visceral-adiposity cytokine output; treat any underlying infection/autoimmune driver

causes.confusedWith: Chronic fatigue/post-viral syndromes — overlapping fatigue and malaise; the mood/anhedonia core distinguishes depression.

causes.name: Nutrient deficiency (B12/folate, iron, omega-3, vitamin D)

chain.node: Low intake or malabsorption of B12, folate, iron, omega-3, or vitamin D (vegan diet, PPIs/metformin, gastric issues, heavy periods, low sun)
Missing cofactors for monoamine synthesis and methylation (B12/folate → SAMe and BH4; iron → tyrosine hydroxylase, the rate-limiter for dopamine)
Reduced serotonin/dopamine synthesis; iron-deficiency anemia lowers oxygen delivery to brain
Fatigue-dominant low mood, poor concentration

tell.symptoms: Fatigue-dominant low mood. Iron: cold, hair loss, restless legs, pica, vegetarian/heavy periods. B12: vegan diet, PPI/metformin use, tingling. Vitamin D: little sun exposure, indoor lifestyle.
tell.labMarker: Ferritin low (<30 ng/mL = iron deficiency even without anemia); B12 <300 pg/mL borderline (confirm with MMA); low RBC folate; 25-OH vitamin D low (<20 ng/mL deficient, 20–30 insufficient); omega-3 index <4%.

fixes.what: Food-first repletion: oily fish, leafy greens, red meat/legumes for iron, eggs/dairy for B12
B12/folate and iron repletion under lab guidance — these deficiency corrections have the clearest mood payoff when a genuine deficit exists; don't blind-dose iron
Vitamin D3 to bring 25-OH D to ~40–60 ng/mL if low (modest, mainly-if-deficient effect on mood)
Omega-3 to raise the omega-3 index
5-HTP / L-tryptophan is optional and weakly evidenced; only after cofactors are repleted, and NOT alongside SSRIs/SNRIs (serotonin-syndrome risk)

causes.confusedWith: Anemia of chronic disease vs true iron deficiency — ferritin can be falsely raised by inflammation, so interpret alongside CRP.

causes.name: Hypothyroidism (overt)

chain.node: Autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's), iodine imbalance, or postpartum thyroid slowing
Low circulating thyroid hormone (Free T4/T3) with elevated TSH
Reduced brain metabolic rate and blunted serotonergic tone
Flat mood, fatigue, cognitive slowing, weight gain

tell.symptoms: The low mood comes packaged with cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, thinning hair, unexplained weight gain, and mental slowing.
tell.labMarker: TSH elevated WITH low Free T4 = overt hypothyroidism (the form that genuinely drives mood). TSH high but Free T4 normal = subclinical — its link to depression is inconsistent and levothyroxine does NOT reliably improve mood, so don't treat the number for the mood. Check anti-TPO antibodies for the autoimmune cause.

fixes.what: Levothyroxine replacement for OVERT hypothyroidism (physician-managed); mood often lifts as levels normalize. Do not expect mood benefit from treating subclinical cases — large RCTs show none
Adequate iodine and selenium intake to support thyroid function
Retest and address the underlying autoimmune driver rather than the number alone

causes.confusedWith: Primary major depression — overt hypothyroidism is the classic mimic, which is exactly why a TSH (with Free T4) is mandatory before concluding the depression is 'just' psychiatric.

ladder: You can't focus — you keep task-switching, lose the thread, and your motivation sags
Your prefrontal control network isn't staying "on": the mind-wandering default-mode network isn't being suppressed during tasks

That switching runs on catecholamine (dopamine/noradrenaline) signalling in the prefrontal cortex — which is dragged out of its optimal range by heritable wiring, lost sleep, low iron, an underactive thyroid, blood-sugar swings, or chronic stress
Fix the root that's yours: sleep timing, thyroid/iron status, meal composition, stress load — and for some, dopaminergic medication
adhd-focus.intro: "Can't focus" almost never has one cause. Attention is your prefrontal cortex holding the relevant task online while suppressing the brain's idle chatter (the default-mode network), and that switching depends on prefrontal catecholamine tone — dopamine and noradrenaline sitting in a narrow optimal band, neither too little nor too much (an inverted-U). Anything that pushes signalling out of that band reproduces the same scattered, restless, low-drive state: heritable low-dopamine-signalling wiring (ADHD), a sleep debt that leaves the PFC un-restored, an underactive thyroid slowing the whole cortex, an iron tank too empty to build dopamine efficiently, a post-meal glucose swing, or a stress load whose catecholamine surge floods the PFC and knocks it offline. The outward picture looks identical, which is why people misdiagnose themselves — the whole game is figuring out which of these is driving YOUR fog, because the fix for each is completely different. Most people who struggle have two or three stacked at once.
adhd-focus.theOneThing: Before reaching for a stimulant, protect sleep: a fixed 7.5–9h window at consistent times. Insufficient or fragmented sleep produces prefrontal hypoactivation and failed default-mode suppression that overlaps closely with the ADHD signature, and it worsens every other driver on this list — so it's the highest-leverage, most-reversible lever for the largest number of people.

causes.name: Intrinsic catecholamine dysregulation (heritable ADHD trait)

chain.node: Polygenic dopamine/noradrenaline signalling variants (many small-effect genes; DRD4, DAT1/SLC6A3 among them)
Prefrontal catecholamine tone sits outside its optimal band (dopamine hypothesis — a partial, pharmacologically-supported model)
Default-mode network poorly suppressed during tasks → intrusive mind-wandering
Dorsolateral PFC can't reliably sustain task-relevant networks
Distractibility, task-switching, procrastination, low drive

tell.symptoms: Lifelong and present since childhood, across ALL settings (school, work, home) — not just when you're tired or stressed. Chronic lateness, unfinished projects, but hyperfocus on things you find genuinely interesting. Note: a good response to caffeine or stimulants is NOT diagnostic — stimulants improve focus in most people, ADHD or not.
tell.labMarker: No blood test exists; diagnosis is clinical (DSM-5, or a structured DIVA interview). Adult self-screen: ASRS-v1.1 — a positive 6-item Part A suggests referral, not a diagnosis.

fixes.what: Daily aerobic exercise — acutely raises prefrontal dopamine and noradrenaline; the most evidence-backed non-drug lever
Methylphenidate or other stimulant IF formally diagnosed — blocks DAT/NET reuptake to restore catecholamine signalling
External structure: single-tasking, timers, captured to-do lists, removed distractions (compensates for weak internal control)
Caffeine for acute alertness; L-tyrosine only meaningfully helps when catecholamines are depleted by acute stress or sleep loss — no proven baseline focus benefit
L-theanine to smooth caffeine jitter

causes.confusedWith: Situational focus loss from sleep debt, thyroid, stress, or blood-sugar swings — those are recent-onset and setting-specific rather than lifelong and pervasive.

causes.name: Sleep debt / fragmented sleep (including sleep apnea)

chain.node: Chronic short or fragmented sleep (late screens, insomnia, undiagnosed OSA)
Prefrontal cortex not restored; failed suppression of the default-mode network during tasks
Hypoactivation of executive-control networks — overlaps closely with the ADHD signature
Attentional lapses, weak impulse control, mental fog

tell.symptoms: Focus tracks your sleep — clearly worse after bad nights, better on holidays or after catch-up sleep. Daytime sleepiness, needing caffeine to function. If snoring, gasping, or waking unrefreshed: suspect obstructive sleep apnea.
tell.labMarker: If OSA suspected: home sleep-apnea test / overnight oximetry — AHI ≥5 confirms. Otherwise track sleep duration and consistency for 2 weeks.

fixes.what: Fixed 7.5–9h sleep window with the same bed/wake times every day
Morning bright-light exposure + hard screen cutoff ~1h before bed
Screen for sleep apnea; CPAP or mandibular device if AHI elevated
No caffeine after early afternoon (protects sleep depth)

causes.confusedWith: Intrinsic ADHD — the daytime picture is near-identical, but sleep-driven fog lifts fully once sleep is restored.

causes.name: Underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism)

chain.node: Autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's), post-partum, or iodine/medication causes → underactive thyroid
Low T3/T4 lowers CNS metabolic rate and blunts catecholamine receptor sensitivity
Global cortical and prefrontal slowing
Brain fog, slowed processing, inattention, low drive — can mimic ADHD or depression

tell.symptoms: Fog comes packaged with cold intolerance, unexplained weight gain, constipation, dry skin, hair thinning, fatigue, and low mood; onset is gradual. More common in women, post-partum, and with a family history of thyroid or autoimmune disease.
tell.labMarker: TSH (elevated) with free T4 (low or low-normal); add TPO antibodies to confirm Hashimoto's. A single cheap blood test rules it in or out.

fixes.what: Levothyroxine replacement if overt hypothyroidism, titrated to TSH
Retest and treat the underlying thyroid disease — cognitive symptoms reverse as levels normalise
Ensure adequate iodine and selenium only if deficient (do not megadose)

causes.confusedWith: ADHD, depression, and iron deficiency — all share fatigue and inattention; a TSH + ferritin panel separates them.

causes.name: Blood-sugar swings / post-meal glucose crashes

chain.node: High-glycemic meal or a carb-heavy, protein-poor breakfast
Insulin overshoot → rapid post-meal glucose dip
Transient brain glucose supply < demand + an adrenaline/cortisol counter-surge
Hippocampus and PFC briefly underfuelled
Mid-morning or afternoon fog, jittery, irritable, can't concentrate

tell.symptoms: Focus and energy crash 1–3h AFTER carb-heavy meals; shaky, hungry, irritable, or anxious at the dip. Classic 'coffee + pastry' breakfast then a 10–11am wall. Noticeably steadier on protein-forward or lower-carb meals.
tell.labMarker: No confirmatory test (HbA1c is usually normal). True documented reactive hypoglycemia is rare and the 5-hour glucose-tolerance test is discredited — treat this as a self-observed symptom-meal timing pattern, not a diagnosis. A CGM showing post-meal spikes then dips is supportive but not proof.

fixes.what: Protein-forward breakfast — replace the pastry-and-coffee
Pair every carbohydrate with protein/fat/fibre to blunt the spike
10-minute walk after meals to flatten the glucose curve
Cut liquid sugar and refined-carb snacks

causes.confusedWith: A normal mild post-lunch dip (which everyone gets) — the meaningful version is sharper, symptomatic, and tightly time-locked to high-glycemic meals.

causes.name: Iron / ferritin deficiency

chain.node: Low iron intake (vegetarian/vegan) or losses (heavy periods, GI bleeding, frequent donation)
Depleted ferritin → less iron cofactor for tyrosine hydroxylase, the rate-limiting dopamine-synthesis enzyme
Reduced dopamine synthesis; animal iron-depletion data also show lower D2 receptor and transporter density
Weakened frontostriatal dopamine signalling
Inattention, restlessness, low motivation (can mimic ADHD)

tell.symptoms: Fatigue, cold hands/feet, hair shedding, restless legs at night, breathlessness on exertion, pale. Higher risk if menstruating heavily, pregnant, vegetarian/vegan, or a frequent blood donor.
tell.labMarker: Serum ferritin — low, especially <30 ng/mL; some ADHD-symptom literature flags <50 ng/mL as suboptimal for brain dopamine, but the ADHD-symptom link is genuinely mixed (several unmedicated-only cohorts show no ferritin difference). Pair with CBC and transferrin saturation.

fixes.what: Iron supplement to rebuild ferritin, then retest in ~3 months (don't over-supplement without a low result — iron overload is harmful)
Vitamin C alongside iron-rich foods to boost non-heme absorption
Investigate and treat the source of loss (heavy menses, GI bleeding)
Keep tea/coffee away from iron-containing meals (tannins block absorption)

causes.confusedWith: Intrinsic ADHD and hypothyroidism — both produce overlapping fatigue and inattention; a ferritin + thyroid panel separates them.

causes.name: Chronic stress / stress-driven catecholamine overshoot

chain.node: Sustained psychological stress or overload
Amygdala/locus-coeruleus-driven surge of noradrenaline and dopamine pushes PFC past the top of the inverted-U (D1 and alpha-1 overstimulation)
PFC effectively 'goes offline' — persistent firing collapses, working memory and top-down control drop; prolonged stress + cortisol drive dendritic-spine loss
Scattered, forgetful, racing/looping mind, can't hold a thought

tell.symptoms: Focus collapses under deadlines or worry; racing/looping thoughts, physical tension, wired-but-tired. You were fine before this stretch, and it eases on holiday or once the stressor resolves.
tell.labMarker: No reliable single-timepoint cortisol test for this; the diagnosis is clinical and temporal (symptom onset tracking the stress period).

fixes.what: Reduce chronic stress load — boundaries on demands, offload commitments (targets the driver, not just the symptom)
Daily breathwork or meditation to lower sympathetic/catecholamine drive
L-theanine to dampen stress-driven overarousal
Aerobic exercise + protected sleep to buffer the HPA axis

causes.confusedWith: Intrinsic ADHD, anxiety disorder, and depression — all impair concentration; onset timing and mood symptoms help distinguish.

ladder: You keep forgetting names, losing words mid-sentence, walking into rooms blank
Your hippocampus and its white-matter wiring aren't consolidating or retrieving cleanly
because the things that keep those circuits alive — blood flow, overnight clearance, fuel signaling, cholinergic tone, and stress hormones — have drifted off
and each of those traces back to sleep, cardiometabolic health, chronic stress, the medications in your cabinet, and a couple of fixable deficiencies you can act on now
memory.intro: Age-related memory decline almost never has a single cause — it's a stack, and the mix differs for every person. The same symptom (forgetting names, groping for a word, losing the thread mid-sentence) can come from starved blood flow in a brain carrying cardiovascular risk; from years of short or apnea-broken sleep that never let the brain clear metabolic waste or file the day's memories; from insulin resistance quietly starving hippocampal neurons of fuel; from chronic stress bathing that same hippocampus in cortisol; from everyday medications — sleep aids, older antihistamines, bladder drugs, benzodiazepines — that blunt the brain's acetylcholine signaling; or from a cheap, fully reversible deficiency like low B12 or an underactive thyroid that can mimic dementia outright. The hippocampus and the small vessels feeding it are the shared victims — but the driver upstream is what differs. So the goal here isn't to 'boost memory' in the abstract; it's to find which of these is YOURS, because the person whose problem is untreated sleep apnea needs something completely different from the person whose homocysteine is high or whose fog started three months after a new prescription. Read each one and ask: does this sound like me?

causes.name: Cerebrovascular decline & chronic hypoperfusion

chain.node: High blood pressure, high LDL/ApoB, high blood sugar, smoking
Endothelial dysfunction & arterial stiffening (impaired NO signaling)
Chronic cerebral hypoperfusion + small-vessel white-matter lesions
Hippocampus & fronto-subcortical white-matter tracts
Slowed processing, word-finding lapses, 'tip-of-tongue'

tell.symptoms: You already have heart-health risk factors (high BP, high cholesterol, pre-diabetes, family history). The pattern is slowness and word-retrieval more than dense forgetting; you know the fact but can't pull it up fast.
tell.labMarker: Blood pressure (target <130/80), HbA1c (pre-diabetes 5.7-6.4%), LDL/ApoB elevated; brain MRI often shows white-matter hyperintensities

fixes.what: Aerobic (zone-2) exercise 150+ min/week — the most reliable lever for cerebral blood flow and vascular health
Mediterranean/DASH pattern: oily fish, leafy greens, olive oil, less sodium and refined carbs
Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) for endothelial function and membrane integrity

Citicoline (CDP-Choline) — supports acetylcholine synthesis and neuronal membranes; RCT evidence for memory is modest and mixed in vascular cognitive impairment, so treat it as an adjunct, not the fix
Treat hypertension/dyslipidemia with your doctor (antihypertensive, statin if indicated) — this targets the actual driver

causes.confusedWith: Early Alzheimer's/MCI — vascular decline tends to be stepwise and slowness-dominant, whereas amyloid disease shows earlier dense episodic-memory loss; the two frequently coexist

causes.name: Poor sleep quality & untreated sleep apnea

chain.node: Chronically short/fragmented sleep or untreated obstructive sleep apnea
Loss of deep NREM slow-wave sleep
Impaired glymphatic clearance of amyloid-β/tau + failed overnight memory consolidation
Hippocampus
Can't retain new information; day feels foggy and unfiled

tell.symptoms: You sleep <7h or wake unrefreshed; snoring, witnessed breathing pauses, or morning headaches point to apnea. Memory is worse on bad-sleep nights and the problem is holding NEW information rather than old facts.
tell.labMarker: Home sleep test / AHI (apnea-hypopnea index ≥5 = at least mild apnea); overnight pulse-oximetry showing desaturations

fixes.what: Protect 7-9h with a fixed wake time; get evaluated for sleep apnea and use CPAP if positive — treatment can substantially reverse the cognitive impairment
Cut alcohol and late heavy meals — both suppress slow-wave sleep and worsen apnea
Treat what's fragmenting sleep (apnea, nocturia, restless legs, poorly-timed light/caffeine) rather than reaching for a sedative, which further suppresses slow-wave sleep

causes.confusedWith: Dementia — apnea-driven cognitive impairment is often substantially reversible with treatment, unlike neurodegeneration. Note: the glymphatic clearance mechanism is strongest in animal models and is still being confirmed in humans, but the slow-wave-sleep-to-consolidation link is well established

causes.name: Insulin resistance / metabolic ('type-3-diabetes' brain fuel failure)

chain.node: High-refined-carb diet, visceral fat, sedentary lifestyle
Systemic hyperinsulinemia → brain insulin resistance (IRS-1 serine-phosphorylation)
Reduced hippocampal insulin/IGF-1 signaling & glucose uptake; impaired amyloid clearance
Hippocampus
Post-meal brain fog, poor consolidation, sluggish recall

tell.symptoms: Central weight gain, energy crashes and fog after high-carb meals, pre-diabetes or type-2 diabetes. Recall is worse when metabolically dysregulated.
tell.labMarker: Fasting insulin & HOMA-IR (elevated), HbA1c (pre-diabetes 5.7-6.4%), fasting glucose; high triglyceride-to-HDL ratio

fixes.what: Cut refined carbs and sugar-sweetened drinks; prioritize protein, fiber, and whole foods
Resistance training + post-meal walks to clear glucose and restore insulin sensitivity
If diabetic/pre-diabetic, discuss metformin or a GLP-1 agonist with your doctor — but note metformin can lower B12 over time, so monitor it (see the deficiency cause)

causes.confusedWith: Vascular decline — they overlap heavily and often coexist; a metabolic panel helps identify the dominant driver. 'Type-3 diabetes' is a useful shorthand for brain insulin resistance, not a formal diagnosis

causes.name: Chronic stress & cortisol overload

chain.node: Chronic psychological stress, overwork, rumination, poor sleep
Sustained cortisol acting on hippocampal glucocorticoid receptors
Dendritic atrophy, suppressed neurogenesis, impaired long-term potentiation
Hippocampus & prefrontal cortex
Blanking under pressure, working-memory lapses, scattered attention

tell.symptoms: Memory fails most when you're stressed or overloaded; you 'blank' in high-pressure moments, feel wired-but-tired, and attention is fragmented. Often paired with anxiety or low mood.
tell.labMarker: No reliable single test; diurnal salivary cortisol may show a flattened/elevated pattern but is imperfect — judge mainly by the symptom pattern

fixes.what: Daily stress down-regulation: meditation/breathwork, exercise, and hard boundaries on work/rumination
Bacopa Monnieri (standardized extract, 300-450 mg/day) — RCT/meta-analytic evidence specifically for memory free-recall in healthy adults; give it 12 weeks
Protect sleep — one of the fastest ways to lower a chronically elevated cortisol curve

causes.confusedWith: Depression-related 'pseudodementia' — depression can produce identical memory complaints and is treatable; screen for it. The mechanistic damage is best proven in Cushing's, exogenous steroid use, and animal models; the everyday-stress version is more inferential

causes.name: Medication burden (anticholinergics & sedatives)

chain.node: Older antihistamines (diphenhydramine), overactive-bladder drugs, tricyclics, benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, opioids, some 'PM' sleep aids
Blockade of central muscarinic acetylcholine signaling + CNS sedation
Impaired memory encoding & attention now; cumulative long-term use is associated with higher dementia risk
Cholinergic basal forebrain → hippocampus & cortex
Foggy, slowed, forgetful — worse after a dose and with polypharmacy

tell.symptoms: You take (or recently started) sleep aids, bladder drugs, older antihistamines, tricyclics, benzodiazepines or opioids; the fog tracks with dose and with how many meds you're on. Most relevant if you're older or on several prescriptions.
tell.labMarker: No blood test — total up your Anticholinergic Cognitive Burden (ACB) score from your medication list; a score ≥3 is clinically meaningful

fixes.what: Bring your full medication + supplement list to a doctor or pharmacist for a deprescribing review — taper high-burden anticholinergics and benzodiazepines gradually; never stop abruptly
Fix the reason you started a sleep aid (sleep hygiene, treat apnea) so you can come off it rather than staying on it
Switch to lower-burden alternatives (e.g., a non-sedating antihistamine, a non-anticholinergic bladder agent) under medical supervision

causes.confusedWith: Irreversible dementia — high anticholinergic/sedative burden can make an older adult look demented yet improve markedly on deprescribing; this is one of the most commonly missed reversible causes

causes.name: Reversible deficiency: B12 / folate / thyroid

chain.node: Low B12 (age, vegan diet, PPIs, long-term metformin) or hypothyroidism
Impaired methylation → hyperhomocysteinemia; or low thyroid hormone slowing brain metabolism
Demyelination + vascular/oxidative injury; homocysteine-associated hippocampal atrophy
Hippocampus & white matter
Forgetfulness ± tingling, fatigue, low mood — often reversible with correction

tell.symptoms: You're older, vegetarian/vegan, or on long-term metformin or acid-blockers; memory fog comes with fatigue, numbness/tingling, cold intolerance, or low mood. This is the one you don't want to miss — genuine deficiency can reverse.
tell.labMarker: Serum B12 (borderline <300 pg/mL, deficient <200; confirm ambiguous cases with MMA), homocysteine (elevated >15 µmol/L), TSH (elevated in hypothyroidism); folate

fixes.what: Correct confirmed low B12 with methylcobalamin (oral high-dose, or injections if severe/malabsorptive)

Folate + B6/B12 to lower homocysteine — in the VITACOG MCI trial this slowed brain atrophy specifically in those with elevated homocysteine; expect little benefit if your homocysteine is already normal
Treat confirmed hypothyroidism with levothyroxine if TSH is elevated (physician-managed)

causes.confusedWith: Irreversible dementia — these deficiencies can mimic it and correct with treatment in genuinely deficient people, which is why a basic blood panel is step one. Supplementing when you're not deficient does not improve memory

memory.theOneThing: Do two cheap things before guessing: (1) get a simple blood panel — B12, homocysteine, HbA1c, fasting insulin, lipids/ApoB, TSH — to find YOUR driver, and (2) review your medication list for anticholinergics and sedatives, since those are the most commonly missed reversible cause. Then attack the highest-leverage lever for most people: protect 7-9 hours of quality sleep and get screened for sleep apnea, because deep slow-wave sleep is when the brain both clears metabolic waste and files the day's memories, and no supplement compensates for its absence.

ladder: You feel exhausted, cynical/detached, and unable to recover no matter how much you rest — the three core dimensions of burnout (exhaustion, depersonalisation, reduced efficacy)
Underneath, your body is stuck in 'demand chronically exceeds recovery': the systems meant to refill the tank (stress axis, sleep, cellular energy production) are running a sustained deficit
That deficit is driven by one or more of four things — unrecovered stress load, chronic sleep debt, low-grade inflammation, or nutrient depletion — and which one is YOURS differs person to person
Each traces back to a modifiable behaviour or a testable lab, so the fix is to identify your specific driver and restore recovery, not to push harder
burnout.intro: Burnout feels like one thing — the tank is empty and won't refill — but it is a shared end-state reached by several different roads, and the road you're on decides what actually fixes you. In many people the axis that mobilises energy under stress (the HPA axis) has been driven for months with no true off-signal, and its output blunts — the cortisol rhythm flattens (a finding that's real but strongest under stress-challenge, and mixed at rest), producing the classic 'wired but tired'. Sitting alongside that, and often mistaken for it, are three quieter drivers: a chronic sleep debt that objectively degrades focus and mood while you feel you've 'adapted'; a low-grade inflammatory load from diet, visceral fat and poor sleep that can make the brain behave as if mildly sick; and a plain nutrient shortfall — most often iron or magnesium — that limits the raw materials mitochondria need to make energy. The trap is treating every burnout as a willpower problem. It is a recovery-deficit problem with a small number of measurable candidate causes, so the move is to find which one (or two) are yours and correct that — not to push harder. Note that clinical depression overlaps heavily with burnout and is a different diagnosis; rule it in or out first.

causes.name: Unrecovered chronic stress load (HPA-axis dysregulation)

chain.node: Months of high demand / effort-reward imbalance / low autonomy with no real recovery window
Persistent CRH and ACTH drive keeps the stress axis switched on
HPA axis output shifts: blunted, flattened diurnal cortisol slope (evidence strongest under stress-challenge; mixed for resting/single levels, so a normal reading doesn't exclude it)
Reduced glucocorticoid signalling and blunted parasympathetic/autonomic recovery
Exhaustion, cynicism, 'wired but tired', can't wind down or recover

tell.symptoms: Wired-but-tired: tired all day but can't switch off at night; symptoms track your workload (worse in crunch weeks, better on genuine holidays); low stress tolerance where small things feel overwhelming.
tell.labMarker: Diurnal salivary cortisol / cortisol awakening response — often flattened or blunted, but evidence is mixed, so a normal result does NOT rule this out; it is a supporting, not confirming, test.

fixes.what: Install non-negotiable recovery blocks: a hard daily stop, one full work-free day, and rebalancing effort vs reward (say no, delegate, renegotiate scope, reclaim autonomy)

Ashwagandha — meta-analyses of RCTs consistently show it lowers cortisol, but its effect on subjectively perceived stress is inconsistent; treat it as a physiological-stress tool, not a guaranteed mood fix
Rhodiola Rosea — small RCT evidence for reducing stress-related fatigue and blunting the cortisol awakening response
Magnesium and L-Theanine to dampen sympathetic overdrive and aid wind-down (modest evidence)

causes.confusedWith: Clinical depression — the single strongest overlap with burnout, needing different treatment (therapy/medication); low mood, guilt, and anhedonia that persist even on holiday point to depression, not burnout.

causes.name: Chronic sleep debt and circadian misalignment

chain.node: Habitual short (<6-7h) or irregular / late sleep, social jet-lag
Incomplete overnight recovery and under-restored prefrontal cortex
Degraded executive function, working memory and emotional regulation — objectively impaired even when you subjectively feel 'used to it' (causal, well established from sleep-restriction studies)
Raised sympathetic tone and inflammatory signalling compound the deficit
Persistent fatigue, irritability, brain fog, short fuse

tell.symptoms: You run on <7h or on wildly variable timing, 'catch up' on weekends, feel foggy and short-tempered; a few nights of proper sleep noticeably lifts you (unlike thyroid/iron fatigue, which doesn't).
tell.labMarker: No blood test — a two-week sleep log or wearable showing average sleep well under 7h or high night-to-night variability is the objective marker.

fixes.what: Fix a constant wake time, give yourself a 7.5-8.5h sleep opportunity, get bright light early and cut screens/caffeine late
L-Theanine to ease sleep-onset arousal without sedation
Magnesium (glycinate) — may modestly support sleep onset (evidence is low-quality)

causes.confusedWith: Obstructive sleep apnoea — if you get 7-8h but wake unrefreshed, snore, or are told you stop breathing, get a sleep study; no amount of sleep hygiene fixes apnoea.

causes.name: Low-grade inflammation (cytokine-driven sickness behaviour)

chain.node: High added-sugar / low omega-3 & fibre diet, visceral fat, poor sleep

Shift toward pro-inflammatory signalling (e.g. higher TNF-alpha, altered IL-6) — a mixed and modest association with burnout, NOT a proven causal driver; some studies even find blunted IL-6 stress-reactivity

When present, cytokine signalling reaches the brain, reduces dopamine/serotonin availability and lowers motivation — this cytokine-to-brain 'sickness behaviour' mechanism is itself well established (tier 3)
'Sickness behaviour': low motivation, anhedonia, heavy fatigue, urge to withdraw

tell.symptoms: Fatigue dominated by flat motivation and loss of pleasure rather than anxiety; often carrying extra abdominal weight, high-sugar diet, achy/run-down, catching every minor bug.
tell.labMarker: hs-CRP persistently in the ~1-3 mg/L low-grade range (with no acute infection) supports a chronic-inflammatory contribution; a single value is only suggestive.

fixes.what: Cut added sugar and refined carbohydrate; add fibre, vegetables and fermented foods; move daily
Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) to lower inflammatory tone
Reduce visceral fat via the above plus resistance training — visceral fat is itself an inflammatory (cytokine-secreting) tissue

causes.confusedWith: Subclinical hypothyroidism — a slowed-metabolism, 'energy-dial-turned-down' fatigue with cold intolerance and weight gain; check TSH before assuming inflammation.

causes.name: Iron / magnesium depletion (cellular-energy shortfall)

chain.node: Low dietary iron, heavy menstrual losses, or a plant-heavy diet; poor magnesium intake
Depleted ferritin / tissue iron stores (falls before haemoglobin ever drops)
Impaired mitochondrial electron transport (iron sits in the iron-sulfur clusters of complex I-III and in the cytochromes)
Reduced ATP output in muscle and brain
Fatigue, brain fog, breathlessness on exertion, poor exercise tolerance

tell.symptoms: Fatigue plus exercise intolerance, cold hands/feet, hair shedding, or restless legs; common in menstruating people and vegetarians. The mechanism (iron in the mitochondrial chain) is established; that low ferritin alone causes fatigue is supported by some RCTs in non-anaemic women but not by all, so it's probable, not settled.
tell.labMarker: Ferritin <30 ng/mL is the diagnostic threshold for iron deficiency even with NORMAL haemoglobin; symptomatic benefit in trials clustered below ~50. Serum magnesium is insensitive to total-body status.

fixes.what: Eat iron-rich foods and pair non-heme iron with vitamin C; treat heavy periods
Iron supplement (e.g. ferrous bisglycinate) ONLY if ferritin is low, then retest in ~8-12 weeks — do not supplement blind (iron overload is harmful)
Magnesium to correct a common subclinical shortfall that can worsen fatigue and sleep

causes.confusedWith: B12 / folate deficiency — also causes fatigue and brain fog and is common in the same diets; check these alongside iron rather than assuming.

burnout.theOneThing: Reinstate genuine recovery before anything else: lock a fixed, protected 7.5-8.5h sleep window AND carve out one real work-free decompression block every day. The two most common drivers — unrecovered stress load and chronic sleep debt — both come down to the same missing ingredient (recovery), and they compound each other. Fix the recovery deficit first; only then chase the lab-based drivers (iron, inflammation, thyroid), and if low mood, guilt or anhedonia persist even after rest, get assessed for depression rather than assuming burnout.

ladder: You lie down but sleep won't come
Your brain/body is still in 'day mode' — arousal is too high, the sleep signal is late, or the sleep drive itself is too low
Driven by stress load, late-night light, lingering caffeine, going to bed before you're sleepy, a too-warm body, restless legs, or a bed you've learned to feel alert in
Root fixes: dim the evening, time caffeine, match bedtime to real sleepiness, drop core temperature, fix low iron if legs are restless, and stop 'trying' to sleep
insomnia.intro: Falling asleep isn't an act of will — it's a hand-off between two systems. Rising sleep pressure (adenosine built up across the waking day) has to meet a falling circadian arousal signal (melatonin climbing, cortisol and core temperature dropping). Trouble falling asleep means one side of that hand-off is jammed — and the jam differs person to person. For one reader the brain simply won't stop firing: stress keeps cortisol and noradrenaline elevated past midnight. For another the clock itself is shifted late by evening phone light, so real sleepiness doesn't arrive until 2am. For a third it's the afternoon coffee still blocking the 'you're tired' signal; for a fourth there simply isn't enough sleep pressure yet because they went to bed early to 'catch up' or napped; for a fifth a bedroom too warm for the body to cool into sleep; for a sixth an uncomfortable urge to move the legs the moment they lie down; and for a seventh, years of bad nights that have trained the bed itself into a cue for alertness. Same complaint, different engines. The point of what follows is to find which one (or two) is yours — the fix for a racing mind does nothing for a delayed clock, low sleep pressure, or restless legs, and vice versa.
insomnia.theOneThing: For most people the highest-leverage single move is a hard 60–90 minute wind-down before bed that does two jobs at once: kill bright/blue light (dim lamps, screens off or on warm/red, no overhead LEDs) AND keep a fixed wake time every day. Dimming lowers arousal and lets melatonin rise on schedule; the fixed wake time re-anchors the circadian clock so sleepiness arrives earlier over the following week. It costs nothing and works on the two most common drivers — hyperarousal and circadian delay — simultaneously. (If your trouble is restless legs or clearly too little sleep drive, skip to those causes — this won't touch them.)

causes.name: Cognitive-somatic hyperarousal (stress / racing mind)

chain.node: Chronic stress or bedtime rumination/worry
Sustained HPA-axis activation keeps cortisol from falling to its nighttime low
Noradrenaline keeps cortex and autonomic arousal circuits online
Brain and body can't down-shift; sleep latency prolonged

tell.symptoms: The moment your head hits the pillow your mind speeds up — replaying the day, planning tomorrow, looping on worries. You feel 'wired-tired': exhausted but alert, maybe tense jaw, fast heartbeat. Clearly worse after high-stress days.
tell.labMarker: Late-evening / bedtime salivary cortisol runs modestly higher than the near-zero nighttime nadir it should sit at (meta-analysis: moderate, heterogeneous elevation across day and night in chronic insomnia — supportive, not diagnostic).

fixes.what: A fixed 'worry-dump' — write tomorrow's tasks and open loops on paper ~2h before bed so the brain can stop rehearsing them
Slow breathing with a longer exhale than inhale (e.g. 4-in / 6-out) for a few minutes at lights-out to shift toward parasympathetic tone
L-Theanine 100–200mg in the evening to raise alpha-wave calm without sedation
Magnesium (glycinate form) — NMDA antagonist and GABA modulator; low status is common in poor sleepers, though sleep benefit in trials is modest
Apigenin (or chamomile) — binds the benzodiazepine site of GABA-A; chamomile shows a mild anxiolytic signal in trials (effect is gentle, and apigenin's exact action at the site is not fully settled)

Skip alcohol as a nightcap and heavy or sugary meals near bedtime — alcohol shortens onset but rebounds into fragmented, wakeful sleep, and a late glucose swing can drive an overnight cortisol/adrenaline wake

causes.confusedWith: A primary anxiety disorder or hyperthyroidism — if you feel wired all day (not just at night) and have weight loss, tremor or palpitations, check thyroid (TSH).

causes.name: Circadian delay from evening light (late chronotype / DSPD)

chain.node: Bright / blue light after sunset — phones, laptops, overhead LEDs
Light activates melanopsin in ipRGCs, which signal the SCN to suppress pineal melatonin release
Dim-light melatonin onset (DLMO) pushed later
Internal 'night' arrives after your intended bedtime — you're not biologically sleepy yet

tell.symptoms: You're simply not sleepy at your target bedtime but fall asleep fine at 1–3am, and if allowed to wake late you sleep well. You've always been a night owl. Screens/bright rooms in the last hour are your norm.
tell.labMarker: Salivary DLMO occurs late relative to desired bedtime (research/specialist test); core body temperature nadir also shifted late.

fixes.what: Dim household lights and get off bright screens (or use warm/red mode + blue-blockers) for 1–2h before bed
Get bright light — ideally sunlight — within an hour of waking to advance the clock earlier

Low-dose Melatonin 0.3–0.5mg taken ~5–6h before your current sleep onset (i.e. a few hours before DLMO) — used as a clock-shifter, not a sedative; timing matters more than dose, and phase moves ~2–3h over weeks, not days
Hold a consistent wake time 7 days a week, even after a bad night, to stabilise the phase

causes.confusedWith: True sleep-onset insomnia — in delayed phase the problem is only timing (sleep is normal once it starts); in insomnia sleep is unrefreshing even on a free schedule.

chain.node: Afternoon or evening caffeine (half-life ~5–6h, longer in slow metabolisers)
Antagonises adenosine A1/A2A receptors (arousal effect chiefly via A2A)
Masks the accumulated adenosine 'sleep-pressure' signal
Reduced drive to sleep; onset latency lengthens

tell.symptoms: You drink coffee, tea, energy drinks or pre-workout after ~2pm, or you're sensitive/slow to clear caffeine. Onset is clearly worse on days with later or bigger caffeine doses.
tell.labMarker: No routine lab; CYP1A2 genotype flags slow metabolisers (they clear caffeine far more slowly), but a caffeine-timing experiment is more useful.

fixes.what: Set a caffeine cutoff 8–10 hours before bed and hold it for two weeks to see the effect
Swap the afternoon/evening coffee or tea for decaf or a herbal (caffeine-free) drink
Watch hidden sources — pre-workout, dark chocolate, matcha, green tea, some painkillers and 'energy' supplements
L-Theanine to blunt the jittery edge of any caffeine still on board

causes.name: Too little sleep pressure (bedtime before your sleep drive is ready)

chain.node: Going to bed early to 'catch up' after bad nights, spending 9+ hours in bed, or daytime naps
Not enough adenosine (homeostatic sleep pressure) has accumulated by the time you lie down
Too much time in bed spreads the available sleep drive thin
You're simply not sleepy at lights-out; a long wait to fall asleep

tell.symptoms: You head to bed 'to catch up' after poor nights, lie in bed 8–9+ hours for ~6–7h of actual sleep, or nap in the afternoon. You're not actually sleepy when you lie down but feel you ought to be in bed.
tell.labMarker: None — compare time in bed vs. actual sleep over a 1–2 week diary; low sleep efficiency (<85%) points here.

fixes.what: Sleep restriction / bedtime compression (a core CBT-I technique): shrink time in bed toward your actual sleep total, delay bedtime until genuinely sleepy, keep a fixed wake time — this concentrates sleep drive
Go to bed only when sleepy (heavy eyelids), not merely tired or bored — and don't move bedtime earlier to 'catch up'
Cut or shorten daytime naps, especially after ~2pm, so adenosine can build across the day
Build homeostatic pressure with a full active day — morning light and daytime movement

causes.confusedWith: Circadian delay — both leave you un-sleepy at bedtime, but low sleep pressure improves by building more wake drive, whereas a delayed clock needs re-timing with morning light and timed melatonin regardless of how long you've been awake.

causes.name: Warm body / bedroom (blunted core-temperature drop)

chain.node: Warm bedroom, late heavy exercise, or hot/heavy late meal
Impaired distal (hands/feet) skin vasodilation and heat loss
Core body temperature fails to fall the ~0.5–1°C that gates sleep onset
The thermal sleep-onset signal is delayed — you lie there hot and restless

tell.symptoms: You feel hot or restless at lights-out, kick the covers off, yet your hands and feet can be cold. Noticeably worse in a warm room or after a late workout. (Very relevant in hot, humid climates.)
tell.labMarker: None clinical — check bedroom temperature; the sleep-friendly range is roughly 18–19°C (65°F).

fixes.what: Cool the bedroom to ~18–19°C and use breathable bedding
Warm bath or shower 1–2h before bed (~10 min) — the rebound distal vasodilation afterward speeds core cooling; meta-analysis shows ~36% shorter sleep latency
Glycine ~3g before bed — acts at NMDA receptors in the SCN to promote peripheral vasodilation and speed the ~0.3°C core-temperature decline that eases sleep onset
Finish vigorous exercise more than ~3h before bed so core temperature has time to come back down
Warm socks or a foot-warmer paradoxically help — they open distal blood flow so the body dumps heat faster

causes.confusedWith: Restless legs syndrome — RLS is defined by a compelling urge to move the legs that is relieved by movement, not just a feeling of being too hot; its evening/at-rest timing and family history distinguish it.

causes.name: Restless legs syndrome (iron-dopamine, urge to move)

chain.node: Low brain iron (often with low-normal serum ferritin), genetics, pregnancy, or aggravating drugs
Iron is the cofactor for tyrosine hydroxylase; low brain iron impairs dopamine synthesis and signaling
Dopaminergic dysfunction generates an urge to move the legs, worst at rest in the evening
Uncomfortable legs at lights-out force movement and delay sleep onset

tell.symptoms: An uncomfortable crawling, achy or restless urge to move your legs (sometimes arms) that starts or worsens when you lie down in the evening, is relieved by moving or walking, and eases by morning. Often runs in families; worse in pregnancy or on sedating antihistamines and most antidepressants.
tell.labMarker: Serum ferritin — aim >75–100 µg/L for sleep (the lab 'normal' floor of ~15–30 is far too low here); also check transferrin saturation.

fixes.what: Correct iron if ferritin <75 µg/L: oral iron (e.g. ferrous bisglycinate/sulfate with vitamin C, alternate-day dosing) under clinician guidance; IV iron for refractory or malabsorbing cases
With your doctor, review and reduce aggravating drugs — sedating antihistamines, most SSRIs/SNRIs, and dopamine-blocking anti-nausea agents
Cut evening caffeine and alcohol, which reliably worsen RLS; evening leg movement, stretching or massage can give short-term relief
For persistent RLS the guideline first-line is an alpha-2-delta ligand (gabapentin/pregabalin), not dopamine agonists (augmentation risk) — see a clinician

causes.confusedWith: Simple restlessness or a too-warm body — RLS requires a genuine urge to move that eases with movement and follows an evening/at-rest pattern; without that, look elsewhere.

causes.name: Conditioned (psychophysiological) arousal — 'trying' to sleep

chain.node: Repeated nights of struggling in bed
Bed becomes a learned cue for wakefulness and anticipatory anxiety
Effort and clock-watching raise arousal further
The harder you try to sleep, the longer onset takes

tell.symptoms: You fall asleep easily on the couch or away from home but not in your own bed. You dread bedtime, watch the clock, and feel a jolt of alertness when you get into bed. Often follows an earlier bout of stress-driven insomnia that has since 'stuck'.
tell.labMarker: None — this is a behavioural/conditioned pattern, diagnosed by the story, not a test.

fixes.what: Stimulus control: bed is for sleep only; if awake ~20 min, get up, do something dull in dim light, return only when sleepy
CBT-I (cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia) — the first-line, most durable treatment for this pattern
Paradoxical intention: deliberately stop trying to fall asleep — removing the effort removes the arousal
Turn the clock away so you can't check the time during the night

causes.confusedWith: Circadian delay or an organic cause — conditioned arousal is distinguished by sleeping fine in other settings but not in your own bed.

sleep-maintenance.intro: Sleeping through the night is not one skill that breaks in one way. Everyone surfaces briefly between sleep cycles - the difference between a good sleeper and someone who \"wakes through the night\" is whether something jolts the arousal system hard enough to fully wake you, and then keeps you awake. That jolt has several possible sources, and they usually feel identical from the inside (you're just suddenly awake at 3am). But they come from very different machinery: a stress-primed nervous system that never fully powered down, alcohol wearing off and rebounding, a mid-night blood-sugar dip that trips your counter-regulatory alarm, an airway quietly collapsing dozens of times an hour, a menopausal thermostat misreading normal body temperature as overheating, or an underlying low mood that fires you awake before dawn. Most people have one or two dominant drivers, not all of them - so the whole game is figuring out which alarm is yours by its fingerprint (the clock time, whether you're wired or sweaty or hungry, whether you snore, whether your mood is flat, what you did that evening), because the fix for each is completely different.
ladder: You wake at 2-4am and lie there unable to drop back off
Something fires a burst of arousal mid-sleep - a cortisol/noradrenaline surge, a breathing pause, a glucose dip, a heat flush, or an early pre-dawn wake
Each is driven by a different upstream load: daytime stress, evening alcohol, a late sugar-heavy dinner, an unstable airway, shifting hormones, or a depressed mood
Fix = identify which alarm is firing for YOU by its fingerprint, then remove its trigger (or treat its root) before it fires again
sleep-maintenance.theOneThing: For most people the highest-leverage move is to remove the biggest nighttime alarm trigger - most commonly evening alcohol and a late sugar/refined-carb dinner - and pair that with a strict stimulus-control rule: if you're awake more than 20 minutes, get out of bed rather than lie there wiring your brain to associate the bed with wakefulness. That single change addresses the two most reversible drivers at once. Two exceptions where behaviour alone won't fix it: if you snore, gasp, or wake needing to pee, get a sleep-apnea test first; and if you wake before dawn with a flat, low mood and loss of interest, get screened for depression - that's a root cause, not a sleep-hygiene problem.

causes.name: Stress-driven hyperarousal (HPA axis + sympathetic tone)

chain.node: Chronic stress load / bedtime rumination
HPA-axis activation blunts the normal overnight fall in cortisol, keeping evening/nighttime levels higher
Sympathetic (noradrenergic) tone stays elevated through the night
Thalamocortical arousal system won't stay quiet - sleep stays shallow, arousal threshold is low
Wake 2-4am wired and alert, mind racing, can't return to sleep

tell.symptoms: You wake feeling switched-on and tense rather than groggy; thoughts start racing instantly; worse during high-stress stretches; you may describe yourself as a lifelong light sleeper.
tell.labMarker: No diagnostic test. Some insomnia studies show elevated evening/pre-sleep cortisol and higher overnight noradrenergic tone; the cortisol awakening response is inconsistent and should not be relied on. Pattern, not any single cutoff.

fixes.what: CBT-I stimulus control: if awake >20 min, get out of bed and do something dull in dim light until sleepy - do not lie there fighting it
Hard wind-down: no work/screens/news in the last hour; brain-dump tomorrow's worries onto paper before bed
Ashwagandha in the evening (small RCTs show reduced perceived stress and serum cortisol)
Magnesium (glycinate form) to dampen nervous-system arousal - evidence is modest, mainly helpful if intake is low
Glycine (~3g) before bed - small RCTs show improved subjective sleep quality

causes.confusedWith: The normal brief awakenings everyone has between cycles - only a problem if you can't return within ~20 minutes or it degrades your day. Also depressive early-morning waking, which comes with daytime low mood and anhedonia.

causes.name: Evening alcohol (rebound after it clears)

chain.node: Alcohol in the evening, even 1-2 drinks
GABAergic sedation front-loads deep (N3) sleep and suppresses REM in the first half of the night
As alcohol is metabolized (~3-5h), GABA tone drops while glutamate, noradrenaline and sympathetic activity rebound and overshoot
REM rebound plus raised heart rate and falling HRV fragment the second half of the night
Fall asleep fast, then wake 3-5h later hot, heart pounding, with vivid dreams

tell.symptoms: Nights you drink are clearly worse; you wake roughly 3-5 hours after a nightcap feeling sweaty or with a racing heart and vivid dreams; dry nights are noticeably better.
tell.labMarker: No standard lab; a wearable often shows elevated overnight resting heart rate and suppressed HRV on drinking nights versus dry nights.

fixes.what: Stop alcohol at least 3-4 hours before bed; ideally keep most weeknights dry
If you do drink, make it earlier, smaller, and hydrate alongside

causes.confusedWith: Glucose-swing waking - similar clock time, but this one only appears on drinking nights.

causes.name: Nocturnal glucose / counter-regulatory swing

chain.node: Late high-sugar/refined-carb dinner or alcohol on a glucose-dysregulated system
Exaggerated insulin response drives a reactive glucose dip a few hours later
A falling glucose triggers a counter-regulatory adrenaline and cortisol surge (proven to provoke awakening when glucose is driven low experimentally)
Sympathetic surge forces an awakening - but in non-diabetics ordinary dinners rarely drive glucose to true hypoglycemic levels, so the everyday link is more associative than mechanistically clean
Wake around 3-4am, sometimes hungry, shaky or sweaty, but alert

tell.symptoms: Worse after sugary or carb-heavy late dinners, dessert, or alcohol; you may wake hungry, shaky or clammy; better when dinner is protein/fat/fiber-forward and you skip the late sweets.
tell.labMarker: A continuous glucose monitor showing a nocturnal glucose dip just before you wake is the cleanest confirmation, though dips rarely reach true hypoglycemia in non-diabetics; fasting glucose and HbA1c are usually normal.

fixes.what: Build dinner around protein, fat and fiber; cut late-evening sugar and refined carbs
If prone to it, a small protein/fat snack before bed (e.g. a handful of nuts) to blunt the overnight dip
Magnesium to support glucose handling and sleep depth (modest evidence)
Limit evening alcohol, which worsens the overnight glucose swing

causes.confusedWith: Stress-hyperarousal waking - same clock time, different trigger; test by cleaning up dinner and watching whether the 3am wake persists.

causes.name: Obstructive sleep apnea

chain.node: Upper-airway collapses during sleep (worse supine, with excess weight, alcohol, or age)
Repeated apneas cause intermittent drops in oxygen and rises in CO2

Breathing against a closed airway generates negative intrathoracic pressure that increases venous return and distends the atria, releasing atrial natriuretic peptide and driving nighttime urine production
Each event provokes a cortical micro-arousal and sympathetic surge - dozens per hour fragment sleep; you wake to breathe or to urinate
Repeated wakings, snoring/gasping, unrefreshing sleep, one or more bathroom trips

tell.symptoms: Snoring or gasping (ask your partner), waking unrefreshed, morning headache or dry mouth, daytime sleepiness, and waking needing to pee; more likely if overweight, male, large neck circumference, or postmenopausal.
tell.labMarker: Home sleep test or in-lab polysomnography: AHI 5-15 is mild, 15-30 moderate, >30 severe (AHI >=5 with symptoms is diagnostic); overnight pulse oximetry showing repeated oxygen desaturation dips.

fixes.what: Get a home sleep study; if positive, CPAP or a mandibular-advancement oral appliance - this is the definitive fix
Side-sleeping, weight loss where relevant, and stopping evening alcohol (which relaxes the airway)
ENT evaluation for airway/anatomical contributors

causes.confusedWith: Simple age/prostate-related nocturia (BPH) - the giveaway is that apnea-driven nighttime urination largely resolves once CPAP is used.

causes.name: Perimenopausal vasomotor waking (night sweats)

chain.node: Perimenopausal estrogen decline and fluctuation
Falling estrogen disinhibits hypothalamic KNDy neurons (via NKB signalling at the NK3 receptor), destabilizing the thermoregulatory set-point
The thermoneutral zone narrows to a hair-trigger heat-dissipation response
The normal nighttime core-temperature dip is misread as overheating, firing a flush and sweat
Wake suddenly drenched and hot, then chilled - often repeatedly across the night

tell.symptoms: Age roughly 45-55 with irregular or changing periods; you wake drenched in sweat even in a cool room, and get daytime hot flushes too.
tell.labMarker: Clinical diagnosis (vasomotor symptoms plus menstrual-cycle change); FSH may be elevated but fluctuates too much to be required or relied on.

fixes.what: Menopausal hormone therapy (estradiol, with progesterone if you have a uterus) - the most effective option for vasomotor sleep disruption
Non-hormonal options: an NK3-receptor antagonist (fezolinetant) or a low-dose SSRI/SNRI
Cool bedroom, layered/moisture-wicking bedding, and limiting alcohol and spicy triggers in the evening

causes.confusedWith: Night sweats from other causes (infection, hyperthyroidism, or OSA) - suspect these if you're outside the menopausal window or symptoms don't track with cycle changes.

causes.name: Depression-linked early-morning (terminal) awakening

chain.node: Depressed mood / major depressive disorder
HPA-axis dysregulation shifts the cortisol rhythm so cortisol rises abnormally early (hypercortisolemia is a common finding in depression)
Altered REM regulation - shortened REM latency and increased REM density, attributed to a cholinergic/aminergic imbalance
Arousal and circadian drive shift earlier, so you surface well before your alarm and can't return
Wake 1-3h before you want to (often 4-5am), unable to fall back, with low or flat mood on waking

tell.symptoms: Persistent pre-dawn waking that you can't return from, paired with daytime low mood, loss of interest/pleasure, appetite or energy change, and mood that is often worst in the morning.
tell.labMarker: No lab test - clinical. A validated screen (e.g. PHQ-9) is the right first step; the mechanism (early cortisol rise, short REM latency) is well documented but not a bedside test.

fixes.what: Screen for depression (e.g. PHQ-9) and get evidence-based treatment - psychotherapy/CBT and, where indicated, an antidepressant; this targets the root, not the symptom
CBT-I still helps the insomnia component and can be delivered alongside depression treatment
Morning light exposure and consistent wake time to stabilize circadian timing

causes.confusedWith: Stress-driven hyperarousal insomnia - overlaps heavily, but depressive terminal insomnia is characteristically early (4-5am) and carries daytime anhedonia and low mood, which pure hyperarousal does not.

shift-work.intro: Jet lag and shift-work fatigue feel like one thing, but they are really a traffic jam of clocks. Your body doesn't run on a single timepiece — the master clock in the brain (the SCN) sets the pace, but your liver, gut, pancreas and even fat tissue keep their own local time, and all of them are normally kept in formation by three signals: light hitting your eyes, when you sleep, and when you eat. Cross five time zones or flip to a night shift and those signals suddenly disagree with each other and with your schedule. The master clock is stubborn — it only drifts about an hour a day and, in permanent night workers, fewer than a quarter ever partially adjust and under 3% fully flip — so you end up alert when you need to sleep and wrecked when you need to perform. Which driver dominates differs person to person: for some it is pure light misalignment, for others it is that they cannot sleep in a bright bedroom during the day, for others it is a giant meal eaten at 3am or a coffee too late in the shift. The fix is to find YOUR loudest wrong signal and correct its timing.
shift-work.theOneThing: Control your light. Get bright light (outdoors or a 10,000-lux box) right at your chosen wake time, and aggressively block light — dark wrap-around glasses on the commute home, dim/blue-blocked screens for the 2-3 hours before your chosen sleep. Light is the single strongest lever for dragging the master clock onto your schedule. To pull in the same direction, add 0.5 mg melatonin — and time it to your goal: for advancing to an earlier schedule take it in the late afternoon/early evening, a few hours before your target bedtime (its phase-advance effect peaks ~3 h before your own melatonin onset), not at bedtime, where it mostly just sedates.
ladder: The surface complaint: you feel wired-but-wrecked, foggy, and awake at the wrong hours
Proximate cause: your internal body clocks are pointing at a different time than your work-sleep schedule (circadian misalignment)
Upstream drivers: mistimed light, sleep attempted during your biological day, and food eaten during your biological night pull the master clock and the organ clocks apart
Root behaviour: give your body day/night cues (light, sleep, meals, caffeine) that match when you actually need to work and sleep

causes.name: Central clock (SCN) misalignment from mistimed light

chain.node: Bright light hits the eyes at your biological night (or you sit in darkness during your biological day)
Melanopsin in ipRGCs signals the SCN via the retinohypothalamic tract
SCN clock genes phase-shift only ~1h/day and get pulled the wrong direction (eastward advances are especially slow)
Pineal melatonin, cortisol and core-temperature rhythms stay locked to the old time zone
Wide awake at 3am, crushed at 3pm, brain fog, 'wrong time zone' feeling

tell.symptoms: You feel alert and sleepy at the wrong clock times no matter how long you were in bed; the mismatch tracks your schedule/flight and eases roughly one time zone per day. Classic hallmark of true jet lag.
tell.labMarker: Dim-light melatonin onset (DLMO) from timed evening saliva samples: melatonin rises at a clock time out of step with when you need to sleep. Relative to your new schedule it is advanced (too early) after westward travel and night shifts, which need a phase DELAY; and delayed (too late) after eastward travel, which needs a phase ADVANCE. Core-body-temperature minimum also falls at the wrong hour.

fixes.what: Seek bright light immediately at your chosen wake time (outdoors or 10,000-lux box, 20-30 min); wear dark wrap-around glasses to block light during your biological morning when night-working

Take 0.5 mg melatonin to phase-shift the clock. To ADVANCE to an earlier schedule (eastward), take it late afternoon/early evening, a few hours before target bedtime (peak advance is ~3 h before your own melatonin onset); a morning dose DELAYS. Dosing right at bedtime mostly just promotes sleep; higher doses only sedate, they do not re-entrain faster
For travel, pre-shift your sleep and light exposure 1-2 days before departure and keep a fixed anchor sleep schedule

causes.confusedWith: Primary insomnia or depression-related fatigue — but circadian misalignment is tied to the schedule change, catches up over days, and is absent on properly aligned days.

causes.name: Fragmented daytime sleep (circadian-homeostatic opposition)

chain.node: You try to sleep during your biological day
The SCN's circadian alerting signal opposes sleep while core temperature is still high and morning cortisol is rising
Sleep is shallow and truncated — you fall asleep but wake early, cutting into the REM-rich later portion of sleep
1-2 hours less sleep, unrefreshing rest, accumulating sleep debt, microsleeps on shift

tell.symptoms: You fall asleep fine but wake after 3-4 hours in daylight and can't get back down; total sleep is short even when you gave yourself enough time in bed; the bedroom is bright, warm, or noisy.
tell.labMarker: Actigraphy or a sleep tracker showing short (<6h) daytime sleep with low sleep efficiency and multiple awakenings, despite adequate time in bed.

fixes.what: Make the bedroom cave-dark and cool (a lower ambient temperature helps core temperature drop), plus eye mask and earplugs; protect one fixed anchor sleep block
Magnesium at bedtime to support sleep quality
L-theanine to ease sleep-onset arousal without grogginess

causes.confusedWith: Obstructive sleep apnea, which also fragments sleep — worth ruling out if you snore, wake gasping, or feel unrefreshed even on aligned nights.

causes.name: Suppressed / delayed melatonin from light at night

chain.node: Bright or blue-rich light in the evening or on the night shift — screens, phone, overhead workplace lighting
Melanopsin activation (SCN to pineal via the sympathetic pathway) acutely suppresses pineal melatonin synthesis and delays its onset
Pineal gland releases little or late melatonin around your intended bedtime
Long sleep latency, 'tired-but-wired', weak sleep drive after the shift
