tell.symptoms: You come off shift exhausted yet lie awake unable to switch off; you're on your phone or under bright lights right up until you try to sleep.
tell.labMarker: Salivary melatonin measured before your intended bedtime is low/absent and DLMO is pushed later than your schedule allows.

fixes.what: Dim household lights and switch screens to amber/blue-blocking (or wear blue-blocker glasses) for the 2-3 hours before your target sleep — evening light in the hours before bed is what blunts melatonin most

0.5 mg exogenous melatonin at target bedtime to replace the signal the light suppressed (this dose acts as a sleep-promoting replacement; for actively phase-shifting the clock, see the earlier-timed dose above)

causes.confusedWith: General anxiety or racing-mind insomnia — but here it resolves quickly once evening light is controlled.

causes.name: Peripheral clock desync from eating at night

chain.node: Large meals eaten during your biological night
Food-entrainable oscillators in the liver and gut shift toward mealtime within days, independently of the slower SCN, so organ clocks and brain clock disagree
Glucose tolerance is at its circadian low overnight (~17% lower in the biological evening, worsened further by misalignment)
Exaggerated blood-sugar swings, plus bloating, reflux and nausea ('jet-lag gut') and energy crashes

tell.symptoms: You eat your biggest meal in the middle of the night shift or on the plane and feel bloated, refluxy or queasy; energy dips hard afterward. GI symptoms are your loudest signal.
tell.labMarker: A continuous glucose monitor shows higher and more prolonged glucose excursions after the same meal eaten at night versus during the day.

fixes.what: Aim your eating window at your biological day; on a night shift keep overnight intake to a small, light snack rather than a full meal (daytime-restricted eating has been shown to prevent the internal misalignment and glucose intolerance of simulated night work)
Keep overnight food low in sugar to blunt the exaggerated nighttime glucose swings

causes.confusedWith: Food intolerance or IBS — but this is timing-driven and eases when the same foods are eaten by day.

causes.name: Mistimed caffeine and alcohol

chain.node: Caffeine in the back half of a shift, or alcohol used to force daytime sleep

Caffeine blocks adenosine receptors and phase-delays the melatonin rhythm (a double espresso ~3h before bed shifts it ~40 min); alcohol is sedating early but its metabolism triggers second-half REM rebound and fragmentation
Prolonged sleep latency; alcohol-suppressed early REM followed by rebound awakenings as it clears
Even harder to fall asleep, lighter and more broken rest, deeper next-day fatigue

tell.symptoms: You rely on coffee late in the shift or a drink to wind down, and your sleep is shallow and choppy; cutting them noticeably improves rest.
tell.labMarker: Sleep tracker shows long sleep latency and frequent second-half awakenings on days with late caffeine or evening alcohol.

fixes.what: Stop caffeine at least 6-8 hours before target sleep; use it strategically only in the first half of the shift
Pair necessary early-shift caffeine with L-theanine to smooth the stimulation and jitter
Don't use alcohol as a sleep aid — it fragments the second half of sleep as it clears

causes.confusedWith: Underlying anxiety or true caffeine sensitivity — but the pattern is dose- and timing-dependent here.

ladder: You feel it as low drive, flat mood, fatigue and poor recovery from training
Your testosterone output has genuinely dropped (confirmed on a repeat morning blood test, not just symptoms)
Either the brain-to-testis (HPG) signal is being throttled, or the testes themselves can no longer respond

Usually the throttle is visceral fat, sleep debt, chronic stress, prior anabolic-steroid use or a real deficiency (mostly fixable) - occasionally it is testicular or pituitary failure (needs medical treatment)
low-testosterone.intro: Testosterone is not a single dial that simply runs down with age - it is the output of a feedback loop between your brain (hypothalamus/pituitary sending GnRH then LH) and your testes (which make the hormone). \"Low T\" almost always means that loop is being actively suppressed somewhere, and the suppressor differs from man to man. For the majority it is lifestyle- or drug-driven \"secondary\" hypogonadism: carrying visceral fat, chronically under-sleeping, living wired-but-tired on cortisol, prior/current anabolic-steroid use, or a real micronutrient deficiency - most of these throttle the brain's signal and are reversible. For a smaller group the testes or pituitary have genuinely failed (Klinefelter, varicocele, mumps orchitis, a prolactin-secreting tumour, long-term opioids, chemo, haemochromatosis), and no amount of sleep or fat loss will fix it - that needs blood tests and medication. The whole game is figuring out which of these is YOURS, because the fix for one is useless for another. The single most useful first step is a morning total testosterone (repeated to confirm) plus LH/FSH - that panel tells you whether the problem is upstream (brain, often lifestyle-fixable) or downstream (testes, needs a doctor). One honest caveat: many symptoms blamed on low T (fatigue, low mood, low libido) are non-specific, so a number only counts alongside genuine symptoms.

causes.name: Visceral obesity / metabolic syndrome (obesity-related secondary hypogonadism)

chain.node: Excess visceral and subcutaneous body fat
Adipose aromatase (CYP19A1) converts testosterone into estradiol
Estradiol negative feedback, plus insulin resistance and blunted leptin signalling, suppress the brain's GnRH/LH pulses
Leydig cells receive less LH, so testis output falls
Low free testosterone, low drive, and more fat gain - a self-reinforcing loop

tell.symptoms: Central weight gain / rising waistline, fatigue, soft or growing chest tissue (gynecomastia), and the pattern worsened as the belt got tighter. This is your driver if you are overweight or carry fat around the middle.
tell.labMarker: Low free testosterone with low or low-normal (not high) LH, low SHBG, and estradiol at the high end or elevated; often raised fasting glucose/HbA1c and triglycerides

fixes.what: Lose visceral fat - this directly reduces the aromatase driving the loop; ~5-10% weight loss measurably raises testosterone, with larger gains at 10%+
Add resistance training 3-4x/week to build muscle and improve insulin sensitivity
Cut refined carbs, added sugar and alcohol to reduce the fat and insulin resistance feeding the cycle
Correct a genuine zinc shortfall, a cofactor for testosterone synthesis (adjunct, not the main lever here)
Correct vitamin D deficiency if present
Support insulin sensitivity if magnesium intake is low

causes.confusedWith: Type 2 diabetes / insulin resistance itself, which independently lowers SHBG and testosterone - treat them together

causes.name: Chronic sleep debt and obstructive sleep apnea

chain.node: Sleeping under ~5-6 hours, or fragmented sleep / untreated sleep apnea
Loss of the consolidated sleep during which the nightly rise in LH-driven testosterone secretion occurs
Reduced testosterone secretion by the testes across the following day
Daytime (esp. afternoon-evening) testosterone ~10-15% lower, low libido, poor energy and mood

tell.symptoms: You sleep less than 6-7h, wake unrefreshed, snore loudly, or your partner notices you stop breathing; daytime sleepiness. One week of 5h nights lowered daytime testosterone 10-15% in a small controlled crossover study (n=10 young men), independent of cortisol.
tell.labMarker: Morning total testosterone blunted after poor sleep (the study's largest drop was actually in the afternoon); a home sleep study showing AHI >5 confirms apnea

fixes.what: Protect 7-9 hours of sleep with a consistent schedule - the fastest lever for most tired men
Get screened and treated for sleep apnea (CPAP/weight loss); untreated apnea also blunts every other fix
Stop alcohol within 3-4h of bed - it fragments sleep and disrupts the nocturnal testosterone rise
Correct low magnesium, which may support sleep depth

causes.confusedWith: Depression, which shares the low-energy/low-libido picture but does not respond to sleep correction alone

causes.name: Chronic stress and overtraining / low energy availability (cortisol-driven)

chain.node: Sustained psychological stress, or high training volume / underfuelling without recovery
Chronically elevated cortisol and, in overtraining, a chronic energy deficit
Cortisol dampens GnRH pulsatility (in animal models partly via gonadotropin-inhibitory hormone/RFRP-3), and energy deficit raises SHBG
Lower LH reaches the testes and more testosterone is bound to SHBG, so less is free and active
Low drive, poor recovery, wired-but-tired fatigue

tell.symptoms: Wired-but-tired, poor recovery from workouts, high training load with no deloads, or a life-stress spike that tracks with the symptoms. Common in lean, hard-training, or underfuelling men who are otherwise healthy.
tell.labMarker: Elevated or flattened diurnal cortisol; low free testosterone with low-normal LH and often high-normal or high SHBG

fixes.what: Cut training volume / add deload weeks and eat enough - overtraining and underfuelling are common hidden causes in fit men
Deliberate stress downregulation: fixed wind-down, breathwork, offloading real stressors
Reduce alcohol, which is a direct Leydig-cell toxin and raises cortisol
Correct low magnesium to support recovery
Consider tongkat ali - some small trials suggest it lowers cortisol and supports testosterone, but evidence is limited

causes.confusedWith: Early clinical secondary hypogonadism - if lifestyle correction fails, get LH and prolactin checked

causes.name: Micronutrient deficiency (zinc, vitamin D, magnesium) or chronic underfuelling

chain.node: True deficiency in zinc, vitamin D or magnesium (restrictive diet, low sun, heavy sweat losses)

Zinc deficiency downregulates the steroidogenic enzymes (3B-HSD, 17B-HSD) that build testosterone; low magnesium may reduce T's binding to SHBG and low vitamin D status associates with lower T (largely observational)
Leydig cells synthesize less testosterone and less remains free/active
Suboptimal testosterone - but only meaningfully so if you were genuinely deficient

tell.symptoms: Little sun exposure, restrictive or low-meat/low-seafood diet, heavy sweating/training (zinc and magnesium losses). Honest caveat: supplementing when you are NOT deficient does little - the benefit is correcting a real shortfall, not a generic 'boost'.
tell.labMarker: Serum 25-OH vitamin D below ~30 ng/mL, low serum/plasma zinc, low RBC magnesium

fixes.what: Correct vitamin D deficiency (RCTs show benefit chiefly when a true deficit is fixed, not as a generic booster)
Replete zinc if intake is low
Replete magnesium if intake is low
Boron may lower SHBG and raise free testosterone, but this rests on very small short studies - low confidence
Eat whole foods rich in zinc and cholesterol precursors - red meat, shellfish (oysters), eggs

causes.confusedWith: Broader undereating / very low body fat - chronic energy deficit lowers T independent of any single micronutrient

causes.name: Testicular or pituitary failure, incl. anabolic-steroid-induced (clinical primary or organic secondary hypogonadism)

chain.node: Testicular damage (aging, varicocele, mumps orchitis, Klinefelter, chemo/radiation) OR a hypothalamic-pituitary cause (prolactinoma, long-term opioids, prior/current anabolic-androgenic steroid or exogenous testosterone use, haemochromatosis)
Primary: testes cannot make testosterone even though the brain shouts louder (high LH/FSH)
Secondary/organic: the pituitary fails to send LH, so LH is low or inappropriately normal
Persistently low testosterone that does NOT respond to sleep, fat loss or diet

tell.symptoms: Symptoms persist despite good sleep, healthy weight and diet; small or soft testes, infertility, gynecomastia. Any history of anabolic-steroid/prohormone use, opioids, or a known testicular injury/illness is a strong clue - prior AAS use is the single most common cause of profound low T in young men.
tell.labMarker: Two separate morning total testosterone readings low (~264-300 ng/dL / 9.2-10.4 nmol/L cutoff; Endocrine Society uses 264, AUA uses 300). Then LH/FSH split it: HIGH LH/FSH = primary (testicular); LOW/normal LH = secondary - next check prolactin, iron/ferritin and pituitary

fixes.what: Testosterone replacement therapy for confirmed, symptomatic deficiency (primary, or secondary when fertility is not a goal)
Preserve testicular function/fertility in secondary cases
Restart the brain's own signal in secondary hypogonadism, preserving fertility

Treat the specific cause instead of masking it - e.g. cabergoline for a prolactinoma, tapering opioids, venesection for haemochromatosis, or stopping anabolic steroids (many recover within 1-2 years, some never fully)

causes.confusedWith: Anabolic-steroid-induced hypogonadism (from prior gear, prohormones or self-prescribed TRT) and reversible mimics like hyperprolactinemia and thyroid disease - some recover after cessation or treatment, so rule them out before committing to lifelong TRT

low-testosterone.theOneThing: Get two morning (before 10am) total testosterone readings plus LH and FSH before you buy a single supplement - and be honest about whether you have real symptoms, not just a wish for a higher number. If LH is low/normal with low T, the problem is upstream and often lifestyle-fixable - and for most men the highest-leverage moves are losing visceral fat and protecting 7-9 hours of sleep, which remove the two biggest brakes (aromatase and disrupted nocturnal testosterone secretion). If you have ever used anabolic steroids/prohormones, say so - that is the most common hidden cause in young men. If LH/FSH are HIGH, the testes have failed and you need a doctor, not a pill.

ladder: Surface: hot flushes, night sweats, broken sleep, mood dips, brain fog, vaginal dryness, aching joints, thinning bones
Proximate: several different tissues losing their hormone signal at once — the brain's thermostat, mood/arousal circuits, the vulvovaginal/urinary lining, joints and muscle, and bone

Upstream: ovaries run out of follicles with age, so estradiol falls (and progesterone falls even earlier in perimenopause, while estradiol itself swings erratically before its final decline) as FSH climbs
Root: the source isn't a behaviour you caused — but WHICH symptoms hit and HOW hard is shaped by HRT timing, alcohol/caffeine/heat triggers, sleep, protein and load-bearing exercise
menopause.intro: Menopause is not one problem with one cause — it is several different tissues simultaneously losing a hormone they relied on for decades, and each fails in its own way.
menopause.theOneThing: For most women with bothersome symptoms, the single highest-leverage move is an informed conversation about systemic estradiol-based HRT, ideally started within ~10 years of the final period or before age 60 (the "window of opportunity") — one intervention that eases flushes, sleep, mood and bone loss at once. Add local vaginal estrogen for dryness/urinary symptoms (it works even if you skip systemic HRT), and regardless of HRT, protect bone and muscle now with protein, calcium, vitamin D and resistance training.

causes.name: Vasomotor instability (hot flushes & night sweats) — hypothalamic KNDy hyperactivity

chain.node: Ovarian follicle depletion → estradiol falls
Loss of estrogen's brake lets hypothalamic (arcuate) KNDy neurons hypertrophy and fire; excess neurokinin B signals through NK3R
Thermoregulatory centre (median preoptic nucleus) narrows the thermoneutral zone
Inappropriate heat-dissipation: cutaneous vasodilation, flushing and sweating

tell.symptoms: Sudden waves of heat/flushing and drenching night sweats, often worse with alcohol, caffeine, spicy food, warm rooms and stress. This is your driver if heat episodes dominate.
tell.labMarker: Diagnosis is clinical; a raised FSH (roughly >25-30 IU/L) with low estradiol supports the menopausal transition, but FSH fluctuates month-to-month in perimenopause so a single normal value does not rule it out.

fixes.what: Systemic estradiol (HRT) — the most effective option, cuts flush frequency/severity by ~75% (vs placebo in RCTs), best started within ~10 yrs of the final period or under age 60
Fezolinetant, an NK3R antagonist, for women who can't or won't take estrogen — blocks the neurokinin B mechanism directly, non-hormonal (monitor liver enzymes)
Cut the personal triggers: alcohol, caffeine, spicy food, hot environments; dress in layers and keep the bedroom cool
Lose excess adiposity and practise paced breathing — higher body fat and stress both worsen flushes

causes.confusedWith: Thyroid dysfunction (hyperthyroidism mimics flushing, sweating, palpitations) — check TSH before assuming menopause

causes.name: Brain estrogen withdrawal — low mood, irritability & brain fog

chain.node: Estradiol decline (and erratic swings in perimenopause)
Reduced estrogen upregulation of tryptophan hydroxylase-2 (serotonin synthesis) and serotonin receptor/transporter tone, plus altered dopamine and noradrenaline signalling
Prefrontal, hippocampal and limbic circuits lose modulatory tone
Low mood, irritability, tearfulness, poor concentration, word-finding lapses

tell.symptoms: New-onset low mood, irritability or cognitive fog appearing alongside cycle changes or flushes, in someone without a prior mood disorder. A history of marked PMS or postpartum mood sensitivity predicts this driver.
tell.labMarker: No specific marker — diagnosis is clinical; screen TSH, ferritin/CBC and B12 to exclude thyroid disease, anaemia and B12 deficiency that mimic it.

fixes.what: Systemic estradiol (HRT) — improves depressive symptoms of the PERImenopausal transition (RCT evidence strongest here, weaker/absent for established post-menopausal depression); its effect on cognition/'brain fog' is inconsistent, so don't promise a memory fix
An SSRI/SNRI where mood symptoms are prominent — also independently reduces hot flushes
Aerobic + resistance exercise and protecting sleep — both lift mood and buffer cognitive symptoms
Correct a documented vitamin D deficiency (association with low mood is weak — treat the deficiency, don't expect it to fix mood alone)

causes.confusedWith: Major depressive disorder, thyroid disease or anaemia — treat these on their own terms if present

causes.name: Progesterone / allopregnanolone withdrawal — perimenopausal anxiety & insomnia

chain.node: Anovulatory cycles in perimenopause → progesterone falls, often earlier than the final estradiol decline
Less conversion to allopregnanolone → weaker positive allosteric modulation of GABA-A receptors
Reduced inhibitory tone in limbic and arousal circuits
Anxiety, racing thoughts, early waking and fragmented sleep

tell.symptoms: Anxiety and insomnia that begin in EARLY perimenopause while periods are still fairly regular, often cyclical, and sleep problems not fully explained by night sweats. Progesterone is typically the first hormone to fall, so this can precede flushes.
tell.labMarker: No reliable single test — serum progesterone swings across the cycle; the timing and pattern are the tell, not a number.

fixes.what: Micronized progesterone taken at night — mildly sedating via allopregnanolone, and required alongside estrogen in women with a uterus
Correct a magnesium deficiency to support sleep and relaxation (adjunct, not a primary fix)
CBT-I and tight sleep hygiene; limit alcohol, which fragments sleep and triggers night sweats

causes.confusedWith: Primary insomnia, generalised anxiety disorder, or obstructive sleep apnoea (its prevalence rises after menopause) — screen if snoring/daytime sleepiness

causes.name: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause (GSM) — vaginal & urinary atrophy

chain.node: Estradiol decline
Loss of estrogen support to vulvovaginal and urethral epithelium, collagen and blood flow; less epithelial glycogen for lactobacilli
Tissue thins and loses elasticity/lubrication; vaginal pH rises and protective lactobacilli fall
Dryness, burning, painful sex, urinary urgency and recurrent UTIs

tell.symptoms: Vaginal dryness or pain with sex, urinary urgency or recurrent UTIs appearing or worsening years into menopause. Unlike flushes, GSM is progressive and does NOT resolve on its own — it worsens without treatment.
tell.labMarker: Elevated vaginal pH (>5) and thinned tissue on exam support it; largely a clinical diagnosis.

fixes.what: Local vaginal estrogen — highly effective with minimal systemic absorption; safe for most, including many who avoid systemic HRT
Regular non-hormonal vaginal moisturisers plus lubricants for intercourse
Continued sexual activity helps maintain tissue blood flow and elasticity

causes.confusedWith: Vulvovaginal infection, lichen sclerosus or contact dermatitis — examine before assuming atrophy

causes.name: Accelerated bone loss — estrogen-deprivation osteoporosis

chain.node: Estradiol decline
RANKL and inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-alpha) rise, OPG falls, and osteoclast lifespan lengthens
Bone remodelling shifts to net resorption, hitting trabecular bone hardest
Rapid density loss (fastest in the first ~5-7 years) and rising fracture risk — silent until a fracture

tell.symptoms: Usually no symptoms at all until height loss or a low-trauma fracture. Suspect it if you are thin, eat little protein/calcium, are vitamin-D deficient, smoke, or went through menopause early.
tell.labMarker: DEXA T-score <= -2.5 = osteoporosis, -1 to -2.5 = osteopenia; check 25-OH vitamin D (correct if low).

fixes.what: Resistance training plus weight-bearing/impact exercise to load the skeleton
Adequate protein (~1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) to support the bone matrix and muscle
Reach ~1200 mg/day total calcium, diet-first, supplementing only the shortfall
Correct vitamin D deficiency, required for calcium absorption
Systemic estradiol (HRT) preserves bone density; a bisphosphonate (or denosumab) if fracture risk is high

causes.confusedWith: Secondary osteoporosis (hyperthyroidism, hyperparathyroidism, coeliac disease, glucocorticoid use) — screen if bone loss is severe or unexpectedly early

causes.name: Musculoskeletal syndrome of menopause — joint aches, stiffness & muscle loss

chain.node: Estradiol decline
Loss of estrogen's anti-inflammatory and tissue-supporting action on synovium, cartilage, tendon and muscle, plus age-related sarcopenia and a rise in inflammatory tone
Joints, connective tissue and skeletal muscle lose hormonal support; low-grade inflammation rises
Widespread joint aches and morning stiffness, higher risk of frozen shoulder, and accelerating loss of muscle mass and strength

tell.symptoms: New widespread joint and muscle aches with stiffness (often worse in the morning) that cluster with other menopausal symptoms, WITHOUT the swelling or systemic features of an inflammatory arthritis. Frozen shoulder is notably more common in this window.
tell.labMarker: No specific marker; normal CRP/ESR and negative rheumatoid factor/anti-CCP help separate it from inflammatory arthritis, and normal TSH rules out hypothyroid myalgia.

fixes.what: Progressive resistance training to preserve muscle mass, support joints and reduce stiffness
Adequate protein (~1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) to defend against sarcopenia

Systemic estradiol (HRT) is associated with less joint pain (observed in the WHI) — evidence is associative, so a supervised therapeutic trial is reasonable when aches cluster with other menopausal symptoms

causes.confusedWith: Inflammatory/autoimmune arthritis (rheumatoid arthritis, polymyalgia rheumatica), osteoarthritis, or hypothyroidism — check inflammatory markers and thyroid if there is joint swelling, marked prolonged stiffness or systemic features

low-libido.intro: Low libido almost never has a single switch. Desire is the output of a fragile circuit — dopamine-driven "wanting" in the brain's reward centres, permitted by adequate sex hormones and unimpeded blood flow — and that circuit gets throttled by whatever is loudest in your life. For one person it's a cortisol bath from months of grinding stress; for another it's five-hour nights quietly shaving testosterone; for a third it's the SSRI that lifted their mood but flattened their drive, or visceral fat converting testosterone into estrogen, or a relationship where safety has curdled into obligation. The trap is treating everyone's low libido as "low testosterone" and reaching for the same pill. The fix starts with finding which of these levers is yours — and most people have two or three stacked at once.
ladder: "I just don't want sex anymore"
The brain's desire circuit — dopamine 'wanting' plus sex-hormone signaling — is being dialed down
Because stress hormones suppress it, sex hormones are low, or a medication is blocking it
Rooted in chronic stress load, sleep debt, metabolic/hormonal health, mood, and what's in your medicine cabinet
low-libido.theOneThing: For most people the highest-leverage move isn't a pill — it's sleep and stress. Consistently protect 7-9 hours and pull chronic load down: both directly raise testosterone and restore the dopamine reward signaling that desire runs on, and doing so unmasks whichever remaining driver (a medication, a hormone deficiency, or mood) is really yours.

causes.name: Chronic stress / elevated cortisol

chain.node: Chronic stress / relentless daily load
Sustained cortisol elevation
GnRH pulse suppressed at hypothalamus (inhibitory GnIH/RFRP-3 raised)
Less LH → gonads make less testosterone/estrogen; attention hijacked away from sexual cues
Desire falls

tell.symptoms: Libido tanks during high-stress stretches and rebounds on holidays; you feel wired-but-tired, mind racing, hard to switch off; sex feels like one more item on the to-do list.
tell.labMarker: Disrupted diurnal cortisol (high late-night or flattened curve) and low DHEA-S are supportive, not diagnostic — the pattern (stress-linked dips) is the real tell.

fixes.what: Pull chronic load down and add daily down-regulation: zone-2 exercise, breathwork/meditation, real boundaries on work hours
Magnesium to support sleep and blunt the stress response
Tongkat Ali — a 4-week RCT in stressed adults lowered cortisol ~16% and raised testosterone status ~37%

causes.confusedWith: Depression — overlaps, but depression brings pervasive anhedonia across all pleasures, not just stress-linked dips.

causes.name: Sleep debt / poor sleep (incl. sleep apnea)

chain.node: Chronic short or fragmented sleep (incl. undiagnosed sleep apnea)
Lost deep/REM sleep blunts the sleep-associated testosterone rise
Daytime testosterone falls ~10-15% (equivalent to ageing 10-15 years); evening cortisol drifts up
Lower androgen drive plus daytime fatigue sap sexual motivation
Reduced desire

tell.symptoms: You sleep under 6-7h or wake unrefreshed; you snore or have witnessed apneas; morning erections have faded; desire is worst in your most sleep-deprived weeks.
tell.labMarker: Low morning total testosterone despite young age; elevated AHI on a sleep study if apnea is present.

fixes.what: Protect 7-9h: fixed wake time, dark cool room, no late screens or alcohol

Screen and treat sleep apnea if you snore or wake unrefreshed — CPAP improves daytime energy, sleep quality and erectile function (note: meta-analyses show it does NOT reliably raise testosterone on its own)
Magnesium to deepen sleep

causes.confusedWith: Age-related or metabolic low testosterone — overlaps heavily; sleep is the fastest reversible piece, so fix it first before assuming a permanent hormone problem.

causes.name: Sex-hormone deficiency (metabolic low-T in men; menopause/OCP in women)

chain.node: Aging, visceral fat, insulin resistance (men); menopause or the pill (women)
Adipose aromatase converts testosterone→estrogen; insulin lowers SHBG; ovarian output falls / OCPs raise SHBG
Low free testosterone (men) / low estradiol + testosterone (women)
Reduced androgen/estrogen signaling in brain reward centres and genital tissue
Desire and arousal decline

tell.symptoms: Men: fewer morning erections, low energy, lost muscle, central weight gain, thinning body hair. Women: post-menopausal or on a combined pill, with vaginal dryness alongside the low desire.
tell.labMarker: Low total AND free testosterone (men — total often flagged low under ~300 ng/dL); low SHBG with insulin resistance; women low estradiol ± testosterone. Always check prolactin and TSH to exclude mimics.

fixes.what: Lose visceral fat and resistance-train — a 10% weight loss raises total testosterone by roughly 100 ng/dL (e.g. ~300→400 in trials) and cuts aromatization
Correct zinc and vitamin D if deficient — both are required for testosterone synthesis (meaningful effect only when truly deficient)
Tongkat Ali to raise free testosterone, partly by lowering SHBG
Testosterone therapy (men) or transdermal estradiol ± testosterone (menopausal women) if labs confirm deficiency; switch an oral pill to a non-oral method for OCP-driven cases
L-Citrulline or a PDE-5 inhibitor for the blood-flow (arousal/erection) component

causes.confusedWith: Hyperprolactinemia or hypothyroidism — both lower libido and mimic low-T; a prolactinoma needs imaging, so check prolactin and TSH before blaming lifestyle.

causes.name: Medication side effect (SSRIs, hormonal contraceptives, finasteride, beta-blockers)

chain.node: SSRI/SNRI, combined hormonal contraceptive, finasteride, or beta-blocker
Serotonin (via 5-HT2) damps dopamine reward drive and can raise prolactin (SSRIs); OCPs raise SHBG; finasteride blocks DHT
Dopamine 'wanting' signal and genital nitric-oxide/blood flow blunted
Desire and orgasm suppressed

tell.symptoms: Desire dropped within weeks of starting a drug; delayed or absent orgasm (the classic SSRI signature); sometimes genital numbness. The timing correlation is the giveaway.
tell.labMarker: No specific lab — the medication list and the timing are the diagnosis.

fixes.what: Ask the prescriber to lower the dose, switch to bupropion or mirtazapine, or non-oral contraception — never stop an antidepressant abruptly
Bupropion as an add-on can restore desire lost to an SSRI
PT-141 (bremelanotide) acts centrally on MC3R/MC4R melanocortin receptors to raise desire

causes.confusedWith: A coincidental depression relapse — the illness itself also lowers libido, so tease apart whether the drop tracks the drug or the mood.

causes.name: Depression / psychological & relationship factors

chain.node: Depression, anhedonia, or relationship distress
Reduced mesolimbic dopamine reward signaling
Collapse of sexual 'wanting' / motivation to initiate
Desire absent even when the situation allows it

tell.symptoms: Loss of pleasure across the board, not just sex; low mood and no motivation. If desire is fine alone or with novelty but absent with your partner, it's relationship-driven rather than hormonal.
tell.labMarker: No hormonal marker — a PHQ-9 or clinical mood screen; morning testosterone only to rule out the overlapping low-T picture.

fixes.what: Treat the depression (therapy, exercise, appropriate meds) — desire usually returns as mood lifts
Couples or psychosexual therapy if desire is specifically partner-directed
Bupropion — a pro-dopaminergic antidepressant that tends to raise rather than lower libido
PT-141 acts centrally to raise desire while the root cause is addressed

causes.confusedWith: Low testosterone — shares the fatigue and anhedonia; check a morning testosterone to separate the two.

ladder: Your bones are thinning because resorption is outpacing formation
Old bone is broken down faster than new bone is laid down
That imbalance is driven by hormone loss, vitamin-D/calcium shortfall, too little mechanical load, too little protein, or a bone-toxic drug
Rooted in menopause/andropause, sedentary living, low sun/dairy intake, under-eating protein, and long-term steroid use
bone-density.intro: Bone is not the dead scaffolding it looks like on an X-ray. It is living tissue continuously demolished by osteoclasts and rebuilt by osteoblasts, and your density at any moment is just the running balance between the two. Osteoporosis is what happens when the demolition crew outworks the construction crew for years. The catch: several completely different problems all tip that balance the same way, and the mix differs person to person. In a post-menopausal woman the dominant driver is estrogen withdrawal unleashing the osteoclasts. In a housebound or desk-bound person it is loss of mechanical loading switching off bone formation. In an older adult who eats little and rarely sees the sun it is a chronic vitamin-D and calcium shortfall that quietly leaches mineral out of bone via a rising parathyroid-hormone signal, or simply not enough protein to build the collagen matrix mineral sits on. In a man it may be falling testosterone and its estrogen metabolite; in anyone on long-term steroids it is the drug itself poisoning the osteoblasts. Most people over 60 have two or three running at once. The job is to find YOURS, because the fix for a hormone problem is not the fix for a loading problem.
bone-density.theOneThing: For nearly everyone, start progressive resistance training plus weight-bearing/impact loading (2-3x/week) AND lock in vitamin-D, calcium and protein sufficiency at the same time. Mechanical load is the main way to switch bone FORMATION back on through your own physiology (by suppressing osteocyte sclerostin, and it also lowers resorption); calcium and vitamin D mainly defend the resorption side. Do both, because a supplement cannot build bone that mechanical load never asked for.

causes.name: Estrogen deficiency (post-menopausal bone loss)

chain.node: Menopause / ovarian estrogen withdrawal
Falling estradiol removes the brake on bone turnover
RANKL/OPG ratio rises, driving osteoclastogenesis (with IL-6/TNF-a)
Osteoclast resorption outpaces formation in trabecular bone (spine, hip)
Rapid BMD loss (fastest in first 5-7 yrs post-menopause), fragility fracture

tell.symptoms: Woman within ~10 years of menopause (or surgical/early menopause, or amenorrhea from low body fat/RED-S); accelerated height loss; hot flushes signalled the estrogen drop. Bone loss began around the last period, not gradually with age.
tell.labMarker: Serum estradiol low with FSH >30 IU/L (post-menopausal pattern); urinary/serum CTX (resorption marker) high-normal or elevated; DEXA T-score <= -2.5

fixes.what: Discuss menopausal hormone therapy (estrogen +/- progestogen) with a doctor if within ~10 yrs of menopause and no contraindication - the direct fix for this driver
Weight-bearing + resistance training to independently stimulate formation
Ensure calcium 1000-1200 mg/day (diet-first)
Vitamin D3 to keep 25-OH-D sufficient so the calcium is absorbed

causes.confusedWith: Age-related bone loss in an older woman decades past menopause - there the loading/protein/vitamin-D drivers usually dominate over pure estrogen loss

causes.name: Vitamin-D / calcium deficiency (secondary hyperparathyroidism)

chain.node: Low vitamin-D status and/or low dietary calcium (little sun, low dairy, malabsorption)
Reduced intestinal calcium absorption lowers serum calcium
Parathyroid glands secrete more PTH to defend blood calcium
Chronically elevated PTH drives osteoclasts to pull calcium out of bone
Progressive low BMD; in severe/long-standing cases bone softening (osteomalacia)

tell.symptoms: Limited sun exposure, veiled/indoor lifestyle, darker skin at higher latitude, low dairy/leafy-green intake, or gut malabsorption (celiac, bariatric surgery, IBD). Often bone/muscle aching and proximal muscle weakness. Very common and silent.
tell.labMarker: 25-OH vitamin D <20 ng/mL (<50 nmol/L) WITH an inappropriately high PTH; serum calcium low-normal. Rising PTH as D falls is the smoking gun.

fixes.what: Vitamin D3 to restore 25-OH-D above ~30 ng/mL, which switches off the compensatory PTH
Calcium to ~1000-1200 mg/day total, diet-first (dairy, tofu, sardines, greens)
Magnesium - cofactor for vitamin-D activation and PTH regulation
10-20 min midday sun exposure on skin where practical

causes.confusedWith: PRIMARY hyperparathyroidism (a parathyroid adenoma) - here calcium is HIGH with high PTH and vitamin D is not the cause; must be surgically worked up, not supplemented

causes.name: Mechanical unloading + sarcopenia (disuse)

chain.node: Sedentary life, bed rest, immobility, or muscle loss = low mechanical load on bone
Under-loaded osteocytes raise sclerostin, which antagonizes Wnt/beta-catenin
Osteoblast bone formation is suppressed (the build side shuts down)
Cortical and trabecular bone thin at sites that lost load
Progressive BMD decline plus weak muscles = high fall-and-fracture risk

tell.symptoms: Desk-bound or housebound, recent prolonged bed rest/immobilization, wheelchair use, or visibly declining muscle mass and grip strength. Swimming/cycling-only exercisers are not protected - non-impact exercise does not load bone enough.
tell.labMarker: No specific blood marker; low appendicular lean mass on DEXA body composition and weak grip strength / slow chair-stand support this driver

fixes.what: Progressive resistance training 2-3x/week - the strongest physiologic anabolic bone stimulus
Weight-bearing impact work (walking with load, hopping/stamping, stair climbing) as tolerated
Adequate protein to rebuild the muscle that loads bone
Balance/fall-prevention training to cut fracture risk while density recovers

causes.confusedWith: Simple age-related BMD loss - but disuse adds a distinct, reversible formation deficit on top; the tell is that muscle is wasting in parallel

causes.name: Inadequate dietary protein (matrix substrate shortfall)

chain.node: Low protein intake, common in older or under-eating adults
Lower IGF-1 and reduced supply of amino acids for type-I collagen
Osteoblasts have too little substrate to build the collagen matrix that mineral binds to; muscle also wastes
Low BMD plus poor bone quality and higher fall risk

tell.symptoms: Eating well under ~1.0-1.2 g/kg/day of protein, small appetite, frail/underweight, unintended weight loss. Often paired with the sarcopenia picture above. (Contrary to old belief, adequate protein HELPS bone when calcium is sufficient.)
tell.labMarker: Low serum albumin/prealbumin and a dietary recall under ~0.8 g/kg/day; low lean mass on DEXA

fixes.what: Raise protein to ~1.0-1.2 g/kg/day, spread across meals (dairy, fish, eggs, legumes, meat)
Pair protein with sufficient calcium so the extra protein is bone-positive, not bone-negative
Combine with resistance training so amino acids are directed into bone and muscle

causes.confusedWith: General malnutrition/frailty - protein is one lever within it; also rule out malabsorption driving both low protein and low calcium

causes.name: Glucocorticoid-induced bone loss (long-term steroids / Cushing's)

chain.node: Long-term oral or high-dose glucocorticoids (or endogenous cortisol excess, Cushing's)
Glucocorticoids trigger apoptosis of osteoblasts/osteocytes and divert stem cells away from the bone-forming lineage
They also raise RANKL, lower OPG, cut intestinal calcium absorption and increase renal calcium loss - adding resorption on top
Formation collapses while resorption rises; trabecular bone (spine, ribs) is hit first
Rapid early BMD loss and fractures - risk rises within 3-6 months of starting steroids and at a HIGHER BMD than usual

tell.symptoms: On prednisone-equivalent >=5 mg/day for >3 months, or repeated/inhaled courses; underlying RA, asthma/COPD, IBD, or transplant. If endogenous: central obesity, thin bruising skin, purple striae, proximal weakness (Cushing's).
tell.labMarker: No single bone marker; if endogenous excess is suspected, overnight dexamethasone suppression or late-night salivary cortisol. FRAX with the glucocorticoid input flagged - DEXA alone under-reads the risk.

fixes.what: Use the lowest effective steroid dose and steroid-sparing agents; treat the cortisol source if endogenous
Start a bone-protective drug early per guideline (bisphosphonate; teriparatide or romosozumab if high risk) - do not wait for a low T-score
Calcium 1000-1200 mg/day plus vitamin D3 as the baseline, since steroids impair calcium handling
Resistance/weight-bearing training to partly offset the formation shutdown

causes.confusedWith: Postmenopausal or age-related loss - but steroid fractures occur at HIGHER BMD, so a 'reassuring' DEXA is misleading; the drug/cortisol history is the tell

causes.name: Sex-hormone deficiency in men (low testosterone / estradiol)

chain.node: Aging andropause, or hypogonadism (pituitary/testicular disease, opioids, obesity)
Low testosterone AND low estradiol from reduced aromatization
Loss of the estrogen brake raises RANKL-driven, high-turnover resorption
Cortical and trabecular bone loss in the male skeleton
Low BMD and fragility fractures in men (often under-diagnosed)

tell.symptoms: Man with low libido, erectile dysfunction, fatigue, loss of muscle/body hair, or on long-term opioids/androgen-deprivation therapy. Estradiol (from aromatized testosterone) is the dominant regulator of the male skeleton, so it must be measured too.
tell.labMarker: Total testosterone low (repeat morning fasting, <~300 ng/dL) with low serum estradiol; check LH/FSH to localize the cause

fixes.what: Treat the cause of hypogonadism; discuss testosterone therapy with a doctor where clinically indicated
Resistance training + fat loss (adipose lowers testosterone; muscle loads bone)
Vitamin D3, calcium and protein sufficiency as the shared foundation

causes.confusedWith: Other secondary causes in men - screen for glucocorticoid use (its own driver above), heavy alcohol, and malabsorption before pinning low BMD on hormones alone

pcos.intro: PCOS isn't one disease — it's a shared endpoint (irregular cycles, stubborn weight, acne, unwanted hair) that several different engines can produce, and yours may be running one, two, or all of them at once. The common thread is too much androgen for a female body, but WHAT is cranking the androgens up differs person to person: for most women it's high insulin from insulin resistance; for some it's an over-firing brain signal (LH) even at a lean weight; for others the adrenal glands over-produce DHEA on their own — an intrinsic, exaggerated response to a normal ACTH signal, not simply 'stress'; and inflammation quietly amplifies all three. That's why one woman thrives on a low-sugar, muscle-building protocol while another needs different work — they're fixing different rungs of the same ladder. The whole game is finding YOUR dominant driver, because that's the one lever that unwinds the rest.
ladder: Surface: stubborn weight, missed/irregular periods, acne, unwanted facial/body hair
Proximate cause: too much free androgen (testosterone/DHEA) plus insulin-driven fat storage
Upstream drivers: high insulin + low SHBG, or over-firing LH signal, or intrinsic adrenal androgen output, or inflammation
Root behaviours (where modifiable): refined-carb load & low muscle mass, poor sleep, chronic stress, under-eating/over-training — note the adrenal driver is largely intrinsic, not behaviour-made

causes.name: Insulin resistance & compensatory hyperinsulinemia

chain.node: Refined carbs, visceral fat, low muscle mass, genetics
Cells resist insulin → pancreas over-secretes it (hyperinsulinemia)
Insulin acts as a co-gonadotropin on ovarian theca cells (raising P450c17 activity) AND suppresses hepatic SHBG production
Free testosterone rises; insulin also drives fat storage
Anovulation, central weight gain, acne, hirsutism

tell.symptoms: Weight sits around the belly, strong sugar/carb cravings and energy crashes after meals, dark velvety skin in neck/armpit folds (acanthosis nigricans), skin tags, family history of type 2 diabetes; weight is very hard to lose.
tell.labMarker: Fasting insulin often >10 µIU/mL and HOMA-IR >2.0-2.5; SHBG low (roughly <30-40 nmol/L is suggestive, though cutoffs vary and SHBG alone is specific but insensitive); HbA1c or fasting glucose may creep to high-normal.

fixes.what: Build a fiber-forward, protein-forward plate at every meal to blunt insulin spikes
Cut added sugar and refined-carb/liquid-sugar load
Resistance training 3-4x/week to build the muscle that soaks up glucose
Myo-inositol (~2-4g/day) as insulin second-messenger; best-evidenced supplement for cycle regularity, though a minority of trials show no metabolic benefit
Berberine (activates AMPK, metformin-like) to lower HOMA-IR and testosterone
Metformin for insulin sensitisation when supplements aren't enough
Semaglutide (GLP-1) where significant weight/metabolic burden dominates

causes.confusedWith: Type 2 diabetes / prediabetes on its own — but PCOS adds the androgen/cycle features.

causes.name: Neuroendocrine drive — elevated LH / GnRH pulse frequency

chain.node: Intrinsic hypothalamic wiring (often lean phenotype), reduced progesterone feedback restraint on GnRH
GnRH pulses stay persistently rapid (~1/hr, failing to slow toward the normal ~90-min follicular pace)
Pituitary over-secretes LH, raising the LH:FSH ratio
LH drives theca-cell androgen synthesis; follicles stall (no ovulation)
Irregular/absent cycles, hyperandrogenism at a normal weight

tell.symptoms: You're lean or normal-weight but still have irregular cycles and high-androgen signs (acne, hair). Insulin markers look fine. This is the classic 'lean PCOS' picture.
tell.labMarker: LH elevated with a high LH:FSH ratio (often >2:1) alongside normal fasting insulin/HOMA-IR and normal-to-high SHBG. Note: LH:FSH is variable and is no longer a formal diagnostic criterion — treat it as supportive, not definitive.

fixes.what: Myo-inositol — shown in several trials to reduce LH and lower the LH:FSH ratio
Protect sleep and manage stress load (both modulate GnRH pulse dynamics)
Avoid under-eating/over-exercising, which further destabilises the axis

causes.confusedWith: Hypothalamic amenorrhea (from under-eating/over-training) — the mimic here is the opposite: LOW LH. Getting the LH:FSH direction checked separates them.

causes.name: Adrenal androgen excess (DHEA-S predominant)

chain.node: Intrinsic adrenal steroidogenic hyperresponsiveness to ACTH (an exaggerated, adrenarche-type response), genetically influenced — stress/sleep may modulate but are NOT the primary cause
A normal ACTH signal triggers an outsized adrenal steroid response; cortisol itself stays normal or even low (partly from faster 5β-reductase clearance) — so this is not a 'high-cortisol' state
Adrenal zona reticularis selectively over-produces DHEA-S
Circulating androgens rise despite a normal ovarian and insulin picture
Hyperandrogen signs, cycle disruption — often at normal weight

tell.symptoms: Normal weight and normal insulin markers, but androgen symptoms (acne, hirsutism) traced mainly to adrenal output. Isolated adrenal androgen excess is the only abnormality in roughly 10% of PCOS; elevated DHEA-S overall appears in ~20-30%. Reality check: despite the popular 'stress/adrenal-fatigue PCOS' label, cortisol is usually normal — the adrenal trait is largely intrinsic, not stress-manufactured.
tell.labMarker: DHEA-S elevated above the age-specific reference range while total testosterone and androstenedione are relatively normal. Basal cortisol is typically normal (not high).

fixes.what: Manage genuine chronic-stress and sleep load if present — a modest, adjunct lever; evidence that lifestyle meaningfully lowers an intrinsic adrenal trait is limited, so set expectations accordingly
Magnesium to support sleep quality and HPA-axis regulation (supportive, not curative)
Regular protein-adequate meals to avoid blood-sugar-driven cortisol swings

causes.confusedWith: Non-classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency) — order a morning 17-hydroxyprogesterone to rule it out before assuming adrenal PCOS, especially when DHEA-S or androgens run high.

causes.name: Chronic low-grade inflammation

chain.node: Visceral fat, high-sugar/processed diet, gut dysbiosis
Adipose tissue releases inflammatory cytokines (e.g. TNF-α, IL-6)
Cytokines worsen insulin resistance AND (shown in vitro/animal models) can directly stimulate theca androgen output
Amplified androgen excess + metabolic dysfunction (vicious cycle)
Weight gain, cycle irregularity, acne

tell.symptoms: Central adiposity plus other inflammatory signs (joint aches, skin issues, fatigue, gut trouble). Acts as an amplifier on top of insulin or adrenal drivers rather than alone.
tell.labMarker: hs-CRP may be elevated (e.g. >3 mg/L) and often tracks with high HOMA-IR — but much of the CRP rise in PCOS is explained by adiposity/BMI, so read it as a marker of the visceral-fat/inflammation bundle, not proof of an independent inflammatory driver.

fixes.what: Cut added sugar and ultra-processed foods that feed adipose inflammation
Omega-3-rich foods / anti-inflammatory whole-food pattern
Fiber to support gut barrier and reduce inflammatory load
Semaglutide or meaningful fat loss to shrink the inflammatory visceral depot

causes.confusedWith: Thyroid dysfunction (Hashimoto's) — also causes weight gain, fatigue and cycle changes; check TSH before pinning it all on PCOS inflammation.

pcos.theOneThing: For the ~70% of women whose PCOS is insulin-driven (a figure inflated by referral bias, but still the largest group), the single highest-leverage move is to combine a fiber-first, protein-forward, low-added-sugar way of eating with resistance training 3-4x/week — this lowers insulin, restores SHBG, and unwinds the androgen excess at its source. Add myo-inositol as the best-evidenced supplement alongside it. (If you're lean with normal insulin markers, your lever is likely the neuroendocrine LH driver or an intrinsic adrenal pattern instead — get SHBG, HOMA-IR, LH:FSH and DHEA-S checked to find YOURS.)

ladder: You see wrinkles, sagging and dullness on the surface
The dermis is losing collagen and elastin faster than fibroblasts rebuild it, and repeated facial folds etch permanent creases into it
Collagen-eating MMP enzymes get switched on (UV, smoke) and cross-linking stiffens the fibres (sugar) while collagen output falls (age, low estrogen)
Root levers: unprotected sun, a high-sugar diet, smoking/pollution, repeated facial expression, and hormonal + chronological time
skin-aging.intro: Your skin's firmness lives in the dermis, a scaffold of collagen and elastin fibres that fibroblast cells constantly build and repair. A wrinkle appears when that scaffold is torn down faster than it is rebuilt, or when the same fold is creased into it thousands of times. There is no single cause: the same face can be driven by decades of sun firing up collagen-shredding enzymes, by dietary sugar cross-linking the fibres so they stiffen and yellow, by a menopausal drop in estrogen that starves fibroblasts of their main growth signal, by cigarette smoke and pollution suppressing collagen synthesis, by years of frowning and squinting etching expression lines into a thinning dermis, and by chronological aging that simply slows collagen production. Most people have two or three of these running at once, in different proportions. The point of this page is to find which drivers are actually yours, because sunscreen fixes one of them and does almost nothing for another. Compare your sun-exposed skin (face, neck, hands) with skin that never sees light (inner arm, buttock): the gap between them is roughly how much of your aging is sun versus everything else.

causes.name: UV photoaging

chain.node: Cumulative UVA/UVB sun exposure
Reactive oxygen species surge in keratinocytes and fibroblasts
ROS activate MAPK/AP-1 and NF-kB, driving collagenase transcription
MMP-1 degrades type I/III collagen while UV also suppresses new synthesis via reduced TGF-beta signalling
Dermal collagen loss, disorganised elastin (solar elastosis), sunspots and wrinkles

tell.symptoms: Wrinkles, leathery texture and brown sunspots concentrated on face, neck, chest and backs of hands, often worse on the window/driver's side; a sharp line at the collar. Skin that is normally covered (inner upper arm, buttock) looks a decade or more younger.
tell.labMarker: No blood test; diagnosed visually. Wood's lamp / cross-polarised skin imaging reveals sub-surface UV pigment damage before it is visible.

fixes.what: Daily broad-spectrum SPF 30+ on all exposed skin, reapplied outdoors, the single biggest lever
Nightly tretinoin, the best-evidenced topical to boost collagen and reverse early photodamage
Morning topical vitamin C to quench UV-induced ROS and support collagen synthesis
Oral astaxanthin as an adjunct systemic antioxidant (raises UV redness threshold; wrinkle-depth evidence is mixed)
Topical niacinamide to reduce pigment transfer and support the barrier
Sulforaphane (broccoli sprouts) to switch on the Nrf2 antioxidant defence

causes.confusedWith: Intrinsic chronological aging, which produces fine lines even in sun-protected skin

causes.name: Intrinsic chronological aging (fibroblast senescence)

chain.node: Chronological time and cumulative cell divisions
Fibroblast senescence: telomere attrition, mitochondrial decline, SASP inflammation
Falling collagen gene transcription and TGF-beta signalling lower procollagen output
Thinner dermis with less collagen, elastin and water-binding glycosaminoglycans
Fine lines, crepiness and gradual volume loss

tell.symptoms: Fine, uniform wrinkling and thinning that appears even on sun-shielded skin (inner arm), coming on gradually from the mid-30s; often follows a family pattern. Loss of glow and 'crepey' texture rather than deep sun-cut furrows.
tell.labMarker: No specific lab; it is the baseline age-related component seen once sun, sugar and smoking are excluded.

fixes.what: Retinoid nightly, the best-evidenced way to nudge aging fibroblasts back toward collagen production
Hydrolysed collagen peptides to supply glycine/proline and signal dermal collagen synthesis (moderate RCT evidence for elasticity)
Adequate daily protein (roughly 1.2-1.6 g/kg) to provide collagen building blocks
Topical GHK-Cu peptide to stimulate collagen and remodelling (limited human evidence)

causes.confusedWith: UV photoaging, distinguished by comparing sun-exposed vs covered skin

causes.name: Dietary glycation (AGE cross-linking)

chain.node: Chronic high blood glucose / high added-sugar diet (and high-heat cooked foods)
Glucose non-enzymatically binds collagen, forming advanced glycation end-products
AGE cross-links stiffen collagen and make it resistant to normal MMP turnover
AGE-RAGE binding drives NF-kB inflammation and blunts antioxidant defences
Stiff, sallow, less elastic skin with deeper-set wrinkles

tell.symptoms: A dull, yellowish/sallow tone and loss of 'snap' or elasticity, out of proportion to sun exposure. Accelerates in people with a high-sugar diet, prediabetes or diabetes.
tell.labMarker: HbA1c above ~5.7% or elevated fasting glucose indicate glycation load; skin AGE autofluorescence readers show raised dermal AGEs.

fixes.what: Cut added sugar and refined carbs; blunt post-meal glucose spikes
Cook at lower temperatures with moisture (steam/boil) rather than searing/frying to lower dietary AGE intake
More fibre to slow glucose absorption and flatten spikes
Vitamin C, which supports collagen and has some anti-glycation activity
Astaxanthin to counter AGE-RAGE oxidative and inflammatory signalling

causes.confusedWith: Smoking-related sallowness, which shows the same grey/yellow tone

causes.name: Repetitive facial expression (dynamic to static wrinkles)

chain.node: Repeated contraction of expression muscles (frowning, squinting, raising brows, smiling)
Each contraction mechanically folds the overlying skin; repetition remodels the dermal/hypodermal connective tissue along the crease
As the dermis thins with age and UV, the fold no longer springs back and becomes etched in at rest
Static lines fixed over the muscles of expression: glabellar frown lines, horizontal forehead lines, crow's feet

tell.symptoms: Lines located precisely over muscles of expression (between the brows, across the forehead, at the outer corners of the eyes) that deepen when you frown, raise your brows or smile, and that persist at rest as skin ages. They do not track with the inner-arm comparison the way sun and intrinsic aging do.
tell.labMarker: None; clinical, assessed dynamically by having you frown and smile and watching which lines appear.

fixes.what: Botulinum toxin to the corrugator/procerus/orbicularis; relaxing the muscle stops the folding and lets the etched line remodel and soften over repeated treatments
Reduce habitual squinting: correct your vision and wear sunglasses so you aren't screwing up the same muscles all day
Nightly retinoid to thicken the dermis so creases spring back rather than set in

causes.confusedWith: Static photoaging and intrinsic wrinkles; distinguished because expression lines sit directly over the muscles of expression and deepen on movement

causes.name: Estrogen decline (perimenopause and menopause)

chain.node: Perimenopausal / menopausal fall in circulating estrogen
Less estradiol reaching fibroblast estrogen receptors (ER-alpha/ER-beta)
Drop in procollagen synthesis, hyaluronan/GAG production and sebum; shift toward higher MMP activity
Rapid dermal collagen loss (about 30% in the first ~5 years post-menopause, then ~2% per year)
Sudden sagging, dryness, thinning and new fine wrinkles

tell.symptoms: In women in their 40s-50s, a noticeably fast change over 1-2 years around menopause: new dryness, jawline sagging and fine wrinkling that tracks with hot flushes and cycle changes rather than with sun.
tell.labMarker: Elevated FSH (typically >25-30 IU/L) with low estradiol confirms the menopausal transition.

fixes.what: Discuss systemic or topical estrogen therapy with a clinician; trials show it increases dermal collagen and thickness (skin benefit is supportive, not a stand-alone indication for HRT)
Collagen peptides to support dermal collagen density during the transition
Topical GHK-Cu to stimulate collagen synthesis locally (limited evidence)
Vitamin C plus adequate protein for collagen substrate and cofactor

causes.confusedWith: General chronological aging, but the tempo (rapid, over months) and timing around menopause distinguish it

causes.name: Smoking and air pollution (oxidative/AhR load)

chain.node: Cigarette smoke or particulate air pollution (polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons)
ROS generation plus aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) activation
Type I/III collagen synthesis falls (~18%/22% lower in smokers in vivo) and ECM turnover shifts toward breakdown (MMP-8 up, TIMP-1 down); smoke extract also induces MMP-1 in fibroblasts in vitro
Nicotine-driven dermal microvessel constriction and net collagen loss
Deep vertical 'smoker's lines' and a grey, sallow complexion

tell.symptoms: Deep facial and vertical lip wrinkling out of proportion to age and sun, with a grey/ashen sallow tone. Seen in current or long-term smokers and people in high-pollution urban environments.
tell.labMarker: No skin lab; serum/urine cotinine confirms tobacco/nicotine exposure.

fixes.what: Stop smoking, the decisive lever; collagen turnover partially normalises after cessation
Sulforaphane to boost Nrf2-driven detoxification of pollution-derived oxidants
Astaxanthin and topical/oral vitamin C to buffer the oxidative load
Omega-3 fatty acids to dampen the associated inflammatory signalling

causes.confusedWith: Dietary glycation, which also causes a sallow tone; smoking adds deep mechanical lip/cheek lines

skin-aging.theOneThing: Wear a broad-spectrum SPF 30+ every single day and add a nightly retinoid. UV drives the large majority of visible facial aging and is the most modifiable input you have; sunscreen stops the ongoing damage while the retinoid rebuilds collagen already lost and also softens etched-in expression lines. Nothing else on the list comes close to that combination for most people.

ladder: You want your hair to stop thinning — but the hair you see is just the output of a growth cycle that’s failing in one of two ways.
Either follicles are permanently shrinking (miniaturizing), or a wave of healthy follicles has been kicked out of the growth phase into shedding early.
Trace it up: shrinking = androgens (DHT) acting on genetically-sensitive follicles; shedding = a systemic stressor (low iron, thyroid, illness, crash diet, a new drug).
Root levers: block DHT at the follicle, refill iron and protein stores, fix the thyroid, and remove the trigger.
hair-loss.intro: Hair thinning feels like one problem, but it’s the visible end of several different failures of the same machine — and which one you have decides whether it reverses. Every follicle cycles between a long growth phase (anagen) and a resting/shedding phase (telogen). Some people lose hair because a genetic sensitivity lets DHT slowly shrink their follicles in a fixed pattern (temples, crown, or a widening part). Others lose it because a shock to the system — low iron, a thyroid slump, a crash diet, a fever or infection, a birth, or a newly started drug — synchronizes a whole cohort of otherwise-healthy follicles into shedding at once, two to four months later. These look different up close (gradual and patterned vs. sudden and diffuse), respond to completely different fixes, and often stack on top of each other. The job isn’t to “treat hair loss” in the abstract — it’s to find which of these is yours, usually with two blood tests (ferritin and TSH), a medication review, and an honest look at the pattern.

causes.name: Androgenetic (DHT-sensitive, pattern) hair loss

chain.node: Inherited androgen-receptor sensitivity in scalp follicles
5α-reductase converts testosterone to DHT in the follicle
DHT–androgen-receptor complex shortens anagen and miniaturizes the dermal papilla over successive cycles
Receding hairline / thinning crown / widening part, hairs progressively finer

tell.symptoms: Gradual and patterned — temples and crown in men, a widening central part with a preserved frontal hairline in women. Runs in the family. No sudden 'handfuls in the shower' shed; you notice more scalp over months to years.
tell.labMarker: No blood test confirms it — diagnosis is clinical/dermoscopic (hair-shaft diameter variability = miniaturization). In women with acne, irregular cycles or excess hair, check free testosterone, DHEAS and SHBG to screen for a hyperandrogenic driver (e.g. PCOS).

fixes.what: Oral finasteride or dutasteride to inhibit 5α-reductase and lower scalp DHT (most effective lever; women who could become pregnant must not use it)
Topical or oral minoxidil — a potassium-channel opener (activated by follicular sulfotransferase) that prolongs the anagen growth phase and enlarges follicles
Correct any zinc deficiency and keep protein intake high — supports the follicle but does not replace DHT blockade
Start early — once follicles are fully miniaturized and lost, no drug regrows them

causes.confusedWith: Telogen effluvium — TE sheds suddenly, diffusely and evenly and then regrows; AGA is slow, patterned and progressive.

causes.name: Telogen effluvium (systemic stressor / drug-induced shedding)

chain.node: A trigger 2–4 months earlier: high fever/infection (incl. COVID), surgery, crash diet, childbirth, major psychological stress, or a newly started drug (statins, retinoids, beta-blockers, SSRIs, anticoagulants, valproate)
HPA-axis / stress signalling (incl. cortisol) or the drug's direct follicular effect pushes follicles out of anagen
A large cohort of follicles shifts prematurely into telogen (resting); shedding appears only when that telogen phase ends
Diffuse shedding across the whole scalp, ~2–4 months (avg 3) after the trigger

tell.symptoms: Sudden, even, all-over shedding (not a specific pattern) — clumps in the shower or brush, a positive gentle pull test, and you can usually name an event or a new medication 2–4 months earlier. Typically self-limits within 3–6 months once the trigger clears.
tell.labMarker: No specific marker for TE itself; test ferritin, TSH and a CBC to find the underlying trigger, and review any drug started ~2–4 months before shedding began.

fixes.what: Identify and remove the trigger (stop crash dieting, recover from illness, manage the stressor)
Review medications started 2–4 months before shedding — discuss substituting a likely culprit (statin, retinoid, beta-blocker, SSRI) with the prescriber
Eat adequate calories and ≥1.2–1.6 g/kg protein daily — hair is keratin and drops first under-fuelling
Replenish iron/protein if a deficiency drove or accompanied the episode
Reassure and wait — chronic TE aside, most cases regrow fully once the stressor resolves

causes.confusedWith: Early female-pattern AGA can also look diffuse; the difference is that AGA persists and concentrates at the part while TE recovers.

causes.name: Iron / ferritin (and protein) deficiency

chain.node: Low dietary iron, heavy menstrual bleeding, vegetarian/vegan diet, or crash dieting
Depleted ferritin (iron stores); iron is a cofactor for the rapidly dividing hair matrix, and hair is treated as low-priority tissue

Matrix keratinocytes lack the substrate for rapid division (association is strong — OR up to ~21 at ferritin ≤30 — but causality is not fully settled; some controlled studies show no difference: tier 2)
Diffuse thinning, increased shedding, slow regrowth

tell.symptoms: Fatigue, feeling cold, brittle/spooned nails, restless legs, heavy periods, or a low-meat/recent-crash diet — alongside diffuse thinning rather than a bald pattern.
tell.labMarker: Serum ferritin: below ~30 ng/mL is associated with hair loss and many dermatologists target >40–70 ng/mL for regrowth. Note the lab 'normal' floor (~12–15 ng/mL) flags anaemia, not optimal hair — 'in range' is not the same as sufficient.

fixes.what: Oral iron (taken with vitamin C, ideally alternate-day for better absorption) to raise ferritin toward 40–70 ng/mL — retest in ~3 months
Red meat, shellfish, liver or legumes-plus-vitamin-C to rebuild stores from food
Hit ≥1.2–1.6 g/kg protein daily so the follicle has keratin substrate
Correct zinc if low — another cofactor for follicle turnover

causes.confusedWith: Thyroid disease and TE produce the same diffuse picture — always check ferritin and TSH together.

causes.name: Thyroid dysfunction

chain.node: Hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, or autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's)
Altered T3/T4 signalling at thyroid-hormone receptors in the dermal papilla
Shortened anagen and reduced keratinocyte proliferation in the hair bulb
Diffuse thinning, dry/brittle hair, thinning of the outer third of the eyebrows

tell.symptoms: Hypo: fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, outer-eyebrow thinning. Hyper: weight loss, heat intolerance, palpitations, anxiety. Either disrupts the hair cycle diffusely.
tell.labMarker: TSH (elevated in hypothyroidism, suppressed in hyperthyroidism), free T4, and anti-TPO antibodies if Hashimoto's is suspected.

fixes.what: Treat the thyroid — levothyroxine for hypothyroidism; hair recovers as levels normalize (recovery can lag months)
Test TSH before assuming pattern hair loss, especially with any thyroid symptoms
Ensure adequate iodine and selenium (thyroid cofactors) if intake is low

causes.confusedWith: Iron deficiency and TE — overlapping diffuse shedding; screen for all three at once.

causes.name: Scalp microinflammation / seborrheic dermatitis

chain.node: Malassezia overgrowth, seborrheic dermatitis, or oxidative stress around the follicle
Perifollicular inflammation (raised IL-1α) and low-grade fibrosis
Inflamed dermal papilla amplifies androgenetic miniaturization and blunts regrowth (contributory/amplifier — tier 2)
Itchy, flaky, greasy, sometimes red scalp with thinning that responds poorly to treatment

tell.symptoms: Persistent dandruff, itch, redness or a greasy scalp alongside the thinning; hair treatments seem to underperform.
tell.labMarker: Clinical/dermoscopic — perifollicular erythema and scaling; no blood test.

fixes.what: Ketoconazole 2% or zinc-pyrithione shampoo (left on a few minutes) to reduce Malassezia and perifollicular inflammation — in one RCT it matched 2% minoxidil for density in AGA, and it is additive to finasteride
Topical niacinamide to support the scalp barrier
Reduce scalp oxidative/irritant load (harsh styling, heat, buildup)

causes.confusedWith: Scarring alopecias (lichen planopilaris, folliculitis decalvans, frontal fibrosing alopecia) and patchy alopecia areata — scarring types cause permanent loss with follicle destruction and need urgent dermatology referral.

hair-loss.theOneThing: Find out which engine you’re on before spending money on any treatment: get two blood tests (ferritin and TSH), review any drug you started 2–4 months ago, and look honestly at the pattern. If the shedding is sudden and even, hunt the reversible trigger — iron, thyroid, a recent illness, a new medication or a crash diet — because that regrows once fixed. If it’s gradual and patterned with family history, start DHT blockade (finasteride) plus minoxidil early, because follicle miniaturization only gets harder to reverse the longer you wait.

ladder: I want to age slower and stay healthy for longer
But your tissues are accumulating damage faster than the calendar - glycation, senescent 'zombie' cells, mitochondrial decline, stiffening vessels
Driven by chronic metabolic over-activation (high insulin & mTOR), low physical reserve (fitness + muscle), and smouldering inflammation
All rooted in how you eat, move, fast, and sleep every single day
longevity.intro: Ageing isn't one clock ticking - it's several damage processes running at once, and which ones dominate differs person to person. In one person it's chronically high insulin plus high glucose quietly caramelising proteins (glycation) and jamming the 'growth' switch permanently on; in another it's a body that stopped being physically challenged, so mitochondria thin out and muscle melts away; in a third it's low-grade inflammation from visceral fat and 'zombie' senescent cells poisoning the tissue around them. Most people over-30 have some blend of all of these, and the same few daily behaviours - over-eating refined carbs, never training hard, never fasting, sleeping badly - feed every one of them. The high-value move is to find which driver is loudest for YOU (your labs and symptoms tell you), because that's the lever that buys you the most healthy years.

causes.name: Insulin resistance & metabolic dysfunction

chain.node: Overnutrition, refined carbs & visceral fat
Chronically elevated insulin (hyperinsulinemia) with intermittent high glucose
Sustained mTORC1 signalling suppressing autophagy
Hyperglycemia-driven glycation (AGEs) binding RAGE, plus growth signalling, driving oxidative stress & vessel/organ damage
Accelerated cardiometabolic disease (and, more associatively, neurodegeneration)

tell.symptoms: Belly/central fat, energy crash and sleepiness after carb meals, sugar cravings, skin tags, creeping blood pressure - this driver is you if fat sits around the waist and meals swing your energy.
tell.labMarker: HbA1c >5.7% (ADA prediabetic range), fasting insulin high (>~8-10 uIU/mL) or HOMA-IR >2, and triglyceride:HDL ratio >2 (mg/dL) - all point directionally to insulin resistance.

fixes.what: Cut refined carbs and added sugar; build meals around protein, fibre and whole foods
Resistance train + zone-2 cardio - muscle contraction restores insulin sensitivity independent of insulin
Soluble fibre with meals to blunt glucose and insulin spikes
Metformin if genuinely prediabetic/insulin-resistant (physician-guided)

causes.confusedWith: Hypothyroidism, which also causes weight gain and fatigue - check TSH before assuming it's insulin.

chain.node: Sedentary lifestyle / low training stimulus
Falling VO2max & mitochondrial density
Loss of muscle mass and strength (sarcopenia)
Impaired glucose disposal, frailty, low physiologic reserve

tell.symptoms: Breathless climbing stairs, weak grip, softening or shrinking muscles, easy fatigue and slow recovery - this driver is you if you rarely get truly out of breath or lift anything heavy.
tell.labMarker: Estimated VO2max below your age/sex norm; grip strength <27 kg (men) / <16 kg (women) on EWGSOP2 older-adult sarcopenia cutoffs; low appendicular lean mass on DEXA. In the Mandsager/JAMA cohort, low vs elite fitness carried an adjusted ~5x mortality risk (HR 5.04) - a larger association than smoking, diabetes or coronary artery disease.

fixes.what: Resistance training 2-3x/week to build and preserve muscle
Zone-2 aerobic base plus weekly VO2max intervals to raise cardiorespiratory fitness
1.6-2.2 g/kg protein spread across meals to support muscle protein synthesis
Creatine monohydrate 3-5 g/day for strength, power and lean mass

causes.name: Chronic low-grade inflammation (inflammaging)

chain.node: Visceral fat, gut dysbiosis & accumulating senescent cells
SASP secretion + NF-kB activation
Chronically elevated IL-6, TNF-alpha and CRP
Smouldering damage across vessels, brain and joints
Cardiovascular disease, cognitive decline, frailty

tell.symptoms: Puffiness, slow injury/workout recovery, brain fog, background aches, and high visceral fat - this driver is you if you feel inflamed and recover slowly despite decent habits.
tell.labMarker: hs-CRP >3 mg/L is the AHA/CDC high-risk band (aim <1 mg/L; 1-3 is average); elevated IL-6 tracks mortality risk in older adults. Both are associations, not proof of a single mechanism - hence tier 2.

fixes.what: Reduce visceral fat through calorie/carb control - it's a major inflammatory source
Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) to lower inflammatory tone
Fibre + fermented foods to improve gut barrier and microbiome
Fisetin as a periodic senolytic to clear SASP-secreting senescent cells (potent in mice; human trials ongoing and bioavailability is a real limitation)
Protect 7-9h sleep - short sleep raises inflammatory cytokines

causes.confusedWith: Occult chronic infection or autoimmune disease - if CRP is markedly high (especially >10 mg/L), rule these out rather than treating it as generic inflammaging.

chain.node: Constant feeding, high leucine/protein load, no fasting window
Persistent mTORC1 signalling
Suppressed autophagy, protein-aggregate and damaged-organelle build-up
Geroconversion of arrested/quiescent cells into senescent cells
Accelerated tissue aging

tell.symptoms: Grazing/eating from waking to bedtime, never fasted, calorie surplus with high protein, and rarely feeling real hunger - this driver is you if your body never gets a 'repair' (fasted) window.
tell.labMarker: No clean blood test for autophagy exists; IGF-1 in the upper reference range can hint at high growth signalling, but interpret cautiously (higher isn't uniformly bad).

fixes.what: Time-restricted eating (e.g. 8-10h window) so mTOR switches off and autophagy runs daily
Periodic lower-protein / protein-cycling days
Spermidine to promote autophagy
Rapamycin (intermittent, physician-guided) - strongest animal lifespan data, human healthspan data still emerging

causes.name: Mitochondrial decline, NAD+ depletion & cellular senescence

chain.node: Aging, oxidative stress and rising NAD+ consumption
Declining NAD+ and impaired mitophagy
Accumulating dysfunctional mitochondria and 'zombie' senescent cells
Fatigue, sarcopenia, reduced endurance, organ decline

tell.symptoms: Persistent low energy, poor exercise endurance, slow recovery and age-related muscle loss despite adequate sleep and food - a driver that grows with age in almost everyone.
tell.labMarker: No validated routine clinical NAD+ or mitophagy blood test exists - this driver is inferred from age and symptoms, not directly measured. NAD+ precursors reliably raise blood NAD+ in trials, but human healthspan outcomes are still early (tier 1-2).

fixes.what: Exercise - the single strongest natural driver of mitochondrial biogenesis
NMN or NR to raise NAD+ levels (raises NAD+ reliably; downstream healthspan benefit unproven in humans)
Urolithin A to boost mitophagy and muscle endurance (best translational human RCT evidence of the mito compounds)
Ca-AKG or GlyNAC to support mitochondrial and redox function (GlyNAC has a small older-adult RCT; Ca-AKG human data are weaker)

longevity.theOneThing: Train for both strength and VO2max - resistance work plus zone-2/interval cardio, roughly 4x/week. It's the only single action that hits every driver at once: it lowers insulin, empties the mTOR/growth signal, builds mitochondria, clears inflammatory visceral fat, and preserves muscle - and cardiorespiratory fitness is the strongest modifiable predictor of how long you stay healthy (low vs high fitness ~5x mortality difference in the Mandsager/JAMA cohort, larger than smoking or diabetes).

inflammation.intro: Chronic inflammation is rarely one thing gone wrong — it's your innate immune system stuck in a low-grade \"on\" state, and several different inputs can hold that switch down at once. The final common pathway is almost always the same transcription factor (NF-κB) driving a trickle of IL-6, TNF-α and IL-1β into your blood, which is why the fix isn't a single pill but finding YOUR inputs. For one person the main driver is the visceral fat around the middle acting as a cytokine factory; for another it's blood-sugar spikes glycating proteins, too little muscle movement, a leaky gut leaking bacterial fragments, chronically short sleep, or simply the senescent cells that accumulate with age. Most people have two or three of these stacked. The whole game is identifying which rungs of the ladder are lit up in you — the achiness, slow recovery and raised CRP are the smoke, not the fire.
ladder: You feel achy, recover slowly from training, and run a high hs-CRP
Your innate immune cells are stuck in a low-grade activated state (NF-κB tone), leaking IL-6 and TNF-α
That state is fed by visceral fat, blood-sugar/AGE spikes, too little muscle movement, a leaky gut, poor sleep, and aging 'senescent' cells
Which trace back to what you eat, how much fat you carry around the middle, how much you move, how you sleep, and time
inflammation.theOneThing: Attack visceral fat by cutting refined sugar and ultra-processed food — it is the single move that removes the largest cytokine-producing tissue AND blunts the glucose/AGE spikes, and it does more than any supplement to lower CRP.

causes.name: Visceral adiposity (metabolic inflammation)

chain.node: Excess visceral fat / hypertrophic adipocytes
M1 macrophage infiltration of adipose tissue (crown-like structures)
NF-κB activation
TNF-α & IL-6 spill into circulation
Systemic low-grade inflammation, raised CRP

tell.symptoms: Waist circumference creeping up (>90cm men / >80cm women on Asian cutoffs), fat carried around the middle rather than hips, energy dips and slow recovery even if BMI looks 'normal' (skinny-fat).
tell.labMarker: hs-CRP elevated (>1 mg/L, worse >3); fasting insulin high or HOMA-IR >1.5; triglyceride/HDL ratio elevated

fixes.what: Sustained fat loss via calorie deficit — visceral fat is preferentially mobilised, shrinking the cytokine source
Resistance + zone-2 training to strip visceral fat and improve insulin sensitivity
Cut liquid sugar and ultra-processed carbs (lower sugar_g) to stop feeding fat storage
Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) — shifts adipose macrophages toward the resolving M2 phenotype and generates specialised pro-resolving mediators (resolvins)
